- Top 20 Interview Questions and Answers : Interview Tips in Hindi: Kind: captions Language: en Why should we hire you for this position? What are your strengths? Tell me about your weakness? You want to join what are your career for in. [Music] In my last video on interview skills, I explained how to prepare an introduction. Some people wrote in the comments that there should be a video in which interview question answers are discussed. Your orders sir. So in this video I will discuss with you the top 20 questions that are asked in the interview. You cannot predict your interview as to which questions will come. But if you prepare these top 20 questions, then you will be able to handle any question about yourself, your personality, your behavior, your skills without any fear. In this video we will cover all types of questions asked in the interview. Be it a basic question on strengths and weaknesses or a behaviour question on your leadership example or a mistake or a stress question like what if you are rejected. But preparing these 20 questions will make you well researched and well prepared about yourself. You will be able to understand the interviewer's point of view behind each question and you will be ready for any kind of non- academic question. And the purpose of this video is not that you memorize these questions and use them in your interview. As I always say, your answers should be according to you. It should be according to your personality. It should be based on your individual experiences. If you use the same answers, they may be over realistic or under realistic depending on your personality. So from this video you can take hints, tips, ideas, help, understand the mindset of the interview but prepare your questions naturally as per your own convenience. Because if it is natural it is good. While watching this video, wherever you feel like noting something, you can take a note or take a screenshot or take a photo with your mobile or add this video to favorites so that you can refer to it later. So let's start with the basic questions first. First Question: Why should we hire you for this position? Because I don't want to buy an iPhone and if I get a job, I won't have to sell my kidney. By giving such answers you will definitely get the answer. When I ask this question many times in interviews, some candidates take this question negatively. But this is not a harsh question. This is not a negative question. Basically the interviewer is asking for your help to tell him why he should hire you? You Give Him a Reason So That He Can Hire You? If we look at every question properly, there is an opportunity hidden in it to express ourselves. Let us see in this question what is the interviewer's expectation? Why should we hire you for this position? Meaning you have to link yourself with this position. So to prepare for this question, read the job description of the job you are applying for carefully. Understand it well and see how your qualities, your qualifications, your skills relate to that position. How you fit for that job. For example, there is a job of a technical support consultant and its description says that the job requirement is to provide technical support to customers. Actors: A link between strategic customers and engineering team. Should have knowledge of Java networking concepts and C++. Requires assistance in end-to-end incident management. Should be able to read, comprehend, and maintain understanding of technical documents. Now you can prepare your answers according to wherever your skills match. Let us take an example. Since this position involves interaction with customers as well as the engineering team, my passion for development projects is evident from my choice of engineering as a career. I have also done internship with ABC software there I learned about Java and C plus plus. My people skills combined with decision making ability will certainly add value to this position. So you told about yourself and linked yourself with that position. This also lets the interviewer know that you are serious about the position because you took the time to understand the job description, research it, and relate it. Second question, another basic question, what are your strengths? This may be going on in your mind. My strength is that if you call me right now, I can gather 10 people. Hockey, bullet, baseball bats will all be collected. But you have to tell your strengths that are worth mentioning. Oh ok ok. Always remember that the interviewer is interviewing you because he wants to know you better. The purpose of every question he asks is to know you better. So you also help him. Tell me about yourself in detail. One way to answer this question could be to simply tell your strengths. Such as my strengths sir communication skills and determination. I am a hard worker and a team player. Or whatever your strength is. But this answer can be explained better. You can back up your strength with an incident or an example. Communication skills is one of my strengths, I work well with different kinds of people and understand different perspectives of everyone. Apart from that I am a determined hard worker and an active team player. So in this way you gave the reason in your answer as to why you think this is your strength. This shows that you know yourself well and you act with logic. Yes, keep your answers natural. It is not that you should give examples of every strength. Explain one strength with an example and mention the rest of the strengths along with it. Let us look at another example. One of my most important strengths includes commitment as I always ensure meeting my deadlines and work consistently towards my boards. My other strengths include positive thinking, punctuality and interpersonal skills. I do n't know my strengths. Many times I am asked this question that we do not know our strength. If you do not know your strengths, then I am giving you a small tip with the help of which you will be able to find your strengths. Write down every achievement of your life, big or small, on a paper. Look at those achievements carefully. You will see your strength in it. If you are into sports, your strength could be leadership or being a team player. If you have participated in debates then communication or presentation skills. If you are good in relationships then interpersonal skills or listening skills. So it may be small or big, of any scale, of any size. Open your achievements box. You will get strength from within it. Next question tell me about your weaknesses. My weakness Alia Bhatt. Control control control, yes, in many websites, books and trainings, it is taught that one should express one's weakness in such a way that it appears positive. I'm over committed at times. I focus on work so much that I forget my family life. I am not in favor of this method because it can sometimes look artificial. As an interviewer, we conduct many interviews in a day. So we understand from your answer whether you are speaking honestly or you are speaking because you have read it somewhere. And we can see honesty somewhere or the other. The truth cannot be hidden. Paper flowers cannot smell good. The truth cannot be hidden by artificial principles. First type of thing. So the method I would recommend for answering this question is that you should tell about your weakness. Tell me honestly. But along with that weakness, also tell what action you are taking to correct that weakness and what results you are getting. For example, I had the habit of procrastinating. Now I'm Improving and Becoming More Organized by Using To Do Lists and Reminders. Such answers show that you know yourself well. You accept your weaknesses. You believe in taking actions and you are getting results. This type of answer shows your honesty and also your positive approach. Next question: Why do you want to join our company? We need a job. What difference does it make if any company gives it ? Hey, who is this friend? Behind every question we have to see what can be the expectations of the interview? And what should be our approach? For example, in this question why do you want to join our company? Means the interviewer wants to know how you are able to associate yourself with this company ? Like earlier we linked ourselves with the position. In this question you have to link yourself with the company. So to answer such questions, you should research about the company a few days before the interview and link yourself with the size of the company or the values of the company or its type. For example, I always wanted to work with a company which recognizes my talent. As I want to grow and contribute towards the growth of the company with my technical and organizational skills. Looking at the growth trend of your company from the past three years. It will be an honour to work with such an organisation. So here you describe yourself, link yourself, and provide a reason behind your link. Which shows that you have researched the company. You are serious about this position for this company. You researched that this company was the most admired company in India. And you are thinking about both your and the company 's benefits. Next question tell me about your achievements. Try participating in my achievement competition, you will finish the whole bottle in half an hour and you are out, man, out of the stadium. Stadium means every question of your interview is like a ball, the interviewer will give you many balls, now it depends on your preparation whether you have to get out on every ball, defend, take a single or hit a sixer, if achievements are asked then you can either tell it in a simple way like I was chosen as the captain of college cricket team and won state level big competition and take a single run or you can get more runs by telling about your qualities. There should be a break of two hours. There should be unlimited samosas in the cafe area. There should be no tension regarding holidays. Obviously you are not going to do social work or charity in your job. You are going for your own benefit. But a right employee is one who looks after both his own benefit and the company's benefit. And these are not just interviews either. If you really want to grow in life then look for your benefit in the company's profit. This is the quality of leaders. This is the quality of achievers. So your answer should contain things that will benefit both you and the organization. Such as teamwork, positive environment, growth. So your answer can be I look for opportunities that enable me to grow as a professional and help me in serving the organization. And another example can be I look for challenges which can bring the best out of you. And a scope to give knowledge which enables me to advance in my career path. You will take challenges, gain knowledge and want to move forward. This is beneficial for you as well as the organization. Next question. What are your career goals? What would be the interviewer's intention behind this question? Do n't be silent in this. No one is asking you. Your interviewer is trying to check whether your career goals match the organization's goals or not, whether you will leave in a few days and the company's money, energy and time spent on you will not be wasted. And They Want to See You're a Good Fit. Because if your goals are linked with the organization then you will be able to grow in this company. They will be able to give you opportunities and they will also be benefited. Many times when I ask this question in interviews, candidates often just tell me their immediate short term goals. But if the question is what are your career goals? So you should tell your immediate as well as long term goals. Yes, if specifically asked what are your short term goals? So short term, what are your long term goals? So long term goals. This makes it clear that you do not think only short term in your life. You also think long term and you plan ahead. And if your goals are linked with theirs then it is a win win situation for both. So your answer can be My immediate career goals include consistently adding value to myself through learning and upgrading skills. In the long term. I would like to grow as a leader and make myself capable of taking up management positions. While contributing significantly to the growth of the organization. So in this your immediate short term goal and long term ambition were visible. Again everyone's answer will be different. You can take ideas from this answer according to your career goals and prepare the answer accordingly. Next question, Where Do You See Yourself in Five Years From Now? I was once hiring for a top multinational company. So the package was 5 lakh per annum. The candidate comes and sits in front of me. I asked this question. Where Do You See Yourself in 5 Years From Now? And I remember his answer. He said I see myself as CEO of the company in five years. Right now the answer shows his big thinking or confidence but I will call it over confidence and unrealistic answer. Because the dream of becoming the CEO of a top multinational company is not wrong. But from fresher to CEO in five years, this dream is wrong. Because if you want to reach the position of CEO in such a big organization, then a lot of learning is required. A lot of experiences are required. You need exposure to multiple positions. Knowledge of how many departments is required? From how many angles should the company be viewed? Then you are able to run such a big company. And only then will you be able to become a successful CEO. So my point is that in order to show big thinking, do not give over ambitious unrealistic answers. The right answer will be one that reflects your vision and your commitment to that vision. So as a fresher your answer can be like this. My first and foremost aim is to learn new skills and enhance my personality. Five years down the line I see myself at senior positions while taking more responsibilities and contributing more to the growth of the company. So in this answer you told that you are not in such a hurry for growth that you do not want to learn. Learning is important for you right now. And by making this learning a ladder, you want to reach senior positions in the future. Next question: What challenges did you face as a student and how did you handle them? This is a behavioral question in which the interviewer wants to see what kind of challenges you have. How did you respond? How did you find the sessions ? What approach did you follow? So through such questions the interviewer is trying to judge your behavior. And such questions are a good opportunity for you to explain your strengths with real life incidents. Let me give you a strategy to deal with behavior questions which will be useful to you in many places. In such questions, first you tell the problem, then tell what action was taken and what was the result and this approach is called PROPER APPROACH PROBLEM ACTION RESULT APPROACH. With such a strategy, you can structure your answer. When you state the problem, you are able to define the situation. With action, your thought process, your attitude, your behavior becomes clear. And with the result, you are able to make your answer convincing, authenticate it and make it strong. Try it and see how many behavior questions you answer with PA R , the answers will be ready in a very clear and structured manner. So, let's take an example of an answer. Since We Only Grow When We Face Challenges. So, I value you's challenge of my life. I always had a problem coping up with the subject of Economics. I did not want this to affect my results and career prospects. So I learned time management to ensure extra time to be spent on the same. I went back to basics and sorted help from my teachers and friends and as a result of my determination I scored 83% in final year. So here you have pointed out the problem, copying up with the subject of Economics. Action was told, learn time management, go back to basics, short health and result was told 83% in final year. So such answers show that you are the one who accepts challenges, you are the one who finds solutions. It turns out that your decision making skills, your thought process, your commitment, your determination, everything is revealed. If you answer this with PAR approach. Let us take another example. So that PAR approach should come completely into us. During my third year I was captain of the college basketball team. While we were preparing for one of our important matches, the team was demotivated, and was losing hope as our next match was with a very strong team. Who has tall players and they were winning almost every match. And as motivation can do wonders, I took it as a challenge to everyone and ensure victory of ours despite all the factors which were against us. I draw charts to highlight all our strengths. I refer to all our past victories and the reasons behind them. I converted entire worry time of team into performance time. And as a result the match went very competitive. We gave our best and won that match. So tell the problem that the team was demotivated and tell the action that highlighted the strengths. Referred to past victories and converted Worry Time to Perfection Time. And the result that you won is the match you made it possible. You are a champion. So, in many such behavior questions you can use the PAR approach and prepare your answers. Next question, tell us about your college and why you chose it? And in case of experience candidates, it can be tell us about your last organization and why you chose it. Many times when I ask this question to the candidates, I get to hear such answers. My last organization was not very supportive. Many times students cover up their shortcomings by blaming the college. I can't stand people who are egoistic. I hate people who do not respect time. Such answers do not give you any positive image. Because everyone knows that there are all kinds of people in every company and only the person who can deal with all kinds of people can grow. At times people who do not stay by their equipment are tough to deal with, but I always try to find ways to ensure that deadlines are met without any personal disagreement. So, in this you answered , showed your flexibility and also showed your team spirit. Next question, what makes a true manager? This may change according to your profile or degree. It can be what makes a true engineer, what makes a true journalist, what makes a true doctor? I am using manager for example because this is the most common question. Because everyone has some manager or the other. A true manager is someone who can guide and direct an entire team in a result- oriented business environment. They Understand How to Motivate, Delegate, and Follow Up to Get the Job Done. Give a clear perception of what it is that they want and how to deliver it to meet and exceed expectations. Again, give your answer according to your degree, your profile, your comfort level and your language. Next question, have you ever failed in any of your decisions? How and why? If so, this is your answer which I heard recently in an interview. I have never failed. I have been successful in all I do. Answering like this and remaining silent are the same thing. Because if you have never failed, it means you have never done anything new, never learned anything new , never tried anything, never taken any risk. You can also answer this question with PAR approach. As students of journalism we organised a news reading competition in our college. We managed to get good entries, sponsors, logistics and everything was in place. But on the day of the event, the audience for football was 20% off what we expected. We have the responsibility of not promoting it well. And try to learn lessons from it. We Did Not, Not, Not, Not, Not Give Up and Tried Again Next Year. We used active promotion strategies to create trust and increase football while making the event successful. So you told me that you failed. You told me how you failed. But I also told you what I learned from it and how to improve it next time. Remember Quitters Never Win and Willers Never Quit. This is the second time in the entire video that the gentleman has threatened. Well done. Last question. What did you learn from your internship? For experienced candidates, this could be: What did you learn from your previous job? This question is last but not the least. This is my one of the most important question which I definitely ask to freshers. Because the internship of freshers is a kind of trailer of the movie of your entire upcoming corporate life. So, from this trailer of yours, i.e. your internship, the interviewer will get an idea about how well you go in a corporate environment. So, let's take an example of a hotel management interview. The practical training about customer service and kitchen operations was very helpful for me. That means your internship is on. Prepare this well also. - Extractables & Leachables: Overview: Kind: captions Language: en when pharmaceutical companies seek new drug regulatory approval it isn't just the drug itself that undergoes scrutiny impurities that may arise from sources external to the drug product are also a key Focus the packaging the drug is stored in is a potential source of impurities that may migrate to the product and is therefore an important component that studied as part of the approval process the drug product container closure system manufacturing process components food contact material and medical devices such as those used in the storage and administration of the drug should not release chemicals that can accumulate in quantities sufficient to present a risk of toxicity to the patient consumer user or affect the drug stability or efficacy consistent with regulatory expectations a comprehensive evaluation should typically include a controlled extraction study and leechable assessments controlled extraction studies are material based and are used to understand the extractable's profile of the material for the purposes of predicting potential leachables aggressive organic solvents or solvent mixtures acids and bases are used with oxalate or solvent reflux and other techniques to extract materials oxlet and reflux are both time consuming manual processes at risk of cross-contamination also important to note is that the extraction conditions should not be so harsh as to deform or degrade the material of Interest which would affect the extractable profile typically the extracts are then analyzed using various techniques such as headspace gcms direct injection gcms LC uvms LC CAD Ms and or ICP MS2 for volatile semivolatile non-volatile and inorganic species the compounds that are present in the resulting extracts are called extractables we study them to create a worst case scenario and prediction of potential leachables to assisted material selection and early risk assessment leachables are product-based compounds that have migrated into the drug product Matrix under normal application or storage conditions leachables are some Times classified as a subset of the extractables we can perform forced or accelerated migration studies to assess for likely potential leachables that migrate under simulated environmental conditions by analyzing drug formulations or simulation solvents after exposure to evaporated temperature we can perform forced or accelerated migration studies to assess for likely potential leachables that migrate under simulated environmental conditions by analyzing drug formulations or simulation solvents after exposure to elevated temperature however if the leachable interacts with the drug product or packaging materials new compounds can be present these are termed secondary leachables drug formulations often contain buffers surfactants fillers and other excipients this complexity means that there are sometimes secondary leachables that only can be identified after long stability studies over the typical drug shelf life which necessitates the requirement for untargeted analysis and the ability to retrospectively evaluate data taken together other this is known as extractables and leechables Analysis there are four main challenges you're likely to face when performing this type of analysis confident detection and quantification of unknowns lowering limits of detection and limits of quantification reducing analytical uncertainty factors and being able to increase sample throughput while reducing manual errors and sources of contamination thermoscientific offers a variety of tools that provide operational Simplicity through hands-free operation and increase throughput capability to provide the right solution for enl analysis see how thermoscientific makes enl analysis easier at thermofisher.com extractables and leachables PPD part of Thermo Fisher Scientific is a leader in supporting enl Consulting study design and testing for the pharmaceutical industry and can help design and conduct studies that support your product submission needs

- Top 20 Interview Questions & Answers || Analytical Balances ||: Kind: captions Language: en hello everyone welcome back to yet another learning video in this video we are going to decode 20 interview questions and their answers related to balances before we begin today's video let me tell you one thing that those who have seen our previous videos on the balance calibration for them it will be very easy to answer all these questions which we are going to discuss in today's video so let's get started without any further delay [Music] question number one what are the USB Jana chapter numbers for the balances so the answer to the question is there are two USB Channel chapter numbers related to the balances and those are USB Channel chapter number 41 and USB Channel chapter number 1251 question number two what are the balance television parameters as per USB Journal chapter number 41. so answer to the question is there are two balanced television parameters as per USB Channel chapter number 41 and those are repeatability and accuracy question number three what is the formula for repeatability calculation so answer to the question is formula for the repeatability is 2 into standard deviation divided by smallest net weight in 200. question number four why repeatability test is performed so answer to the question is repeatability test is performed to check whether Valance is able to give the same results for the same object under the same conditions every time question number five what is the smallest net weight so answer to the question is smallest net weight means the minimum weight below which weighing is not acceptable for example if you have a calculated smallest net weight as 10 milligram then you are not supposed to take any weight below 10 milligram on the balance question number six what is the formula to calculate smallest net weight so answer to the question is smallest net weight is equal to 2000 into standard deviation so this standard deviation value will be taken from the repeatability test where we will take the 10 measurements of a test weight sometimes standard deviation value is less than the 0.41 D in that case formula for the smallest net weight will be 2000 into 0.41 d question number seven what is D in the formula small as net weight you can see the formula 2000 into 0.41 D so what is D here so answer to the question is D is called as scale interval or the readability which means the least value which your balance can read question number eight how the accuracy question is to perform accuracy test a single test weight is selected and its weight is measured after that accuracy is calculated with the help of this formula certificate weight minus observed weight divided by certificate weight into 100. question number nine what is the acceptance limit for the accuracy test so answer to the question is acceptance limit for the accuracy test is not more than 0.10 percent question number 10 how much weight should be taken for the accuracy test so answer to the question is to perform accuracy test weight should be between five to hundred percent capacity of the balance question number eleven what are the percent question number 15 what is the difference between the accuracy test and the sensitivity test well friends if you see the calculation for the both the tests accuracy test and sensitivity test selecting test weight between five to hundred percent capacity of the balance whereas sensitivity test is performed by selecting test weight near to the balance capacity or I can say that sensitivity test is carried out by selecting the maximum weight whereas accuracy test is performed by selecting the test weight between five to hundred percent capacity of the balance so I hope the difference is measured on the all these four corners of the balance after that eccentricity will be calculated with the help of this formula weight at the center minus weight at individual corner divided by the weight at the center into 100 the obtained value should not be more than 0.05 percent question number 17 what is the formula to calculate eccentricity so answer to the question is wait at the center We don't want to say bad things about the employer and focus on things that are a bit more neutral. Give an example of a goal you set and how you achieved it. Or another version is what is your greatest accomplishment? Depending on your profession, you can usually predict a more specific version of this question such as if you're an event planner, they might ask what is the greatest event you've planned or if you're a salesperson, what is the best deal you landed? Basically, they are opening up the floor to you and saying, "I'm giving you a moment to shine. Show us what you got, kid." For this answer, you need to pick an answer that has two components. The first is relevancy. Too many job seekers pick their favorite accomplishment of theirs, such as, "I've heard job seekers pick that they are proud to be the first person in their family to get an MBA." But that doesn't set you apart, nor does it share the value you're actually going to deliver in the role. And then the second thing is to focus on it being impressive. Choose an accomplishment where you did something really well and the results were great. Here's an example. In my role as VP of talent acquisition, I was given the goal to reduce time to hire for our critical technical roles which had been sitting at more than 70 days and causing major delays for engineering and our product teams. And so I set a target to bring that number below 45 days within two quarters. Now the first step was diagnosing where the process was breaking down and I found inconsistencies in how hiring teams were evaluating candidates. There were long gaps between stages. There was lack of clarity around what a great candidate looked like. And so I partnered with the engineering leadership to rebuild the competency profiles, created structure interview guides and I trained every interviewer on how to use them. And then from there, I implemented a new scheduling workflow that removed bottlenecks and introduced a simple dashboard so leaders could see the real-time progress. Then within 4 months, we reduced time to hire to 43 days and increased the number of offer acceptances by more than 15% due to this coordinative, more responsive process. And those improvements allowed the company to accelerate some of their product initiatives. And several of those frameworks we built ended up becoming the standard process across other locations. Tell me about a time you failed or tell me about a time you made a mistake. When picking your answer, look for a story in your past where it truly was a mistake or a failure. We don't want to dodge this question, but the most important part is how did you react to that failure? How did you rectify it? A story with a strong lesson is the one you should choose. Here's an example answer. In my last role as supply chain coordinator, I was responsible for placing weekly replenishment orders with one of our key suppliers. And one week, I misread the demand forecast and I placed an order that was significantly lower than what was needed. And I didn't catch the error until the warehouse team notified me that we were about to run short on a high volume item which put production at risk. And immediately I took ownership. I called the supplier to request an urgent shipment. Because it was my mistake, I also worked with our logistics partners to secure expedited transport and reorganize the production schedule so that the team could prioritize work that didn't rely on that item. We avoided a full shutdown, but it was definitely a stressful thing that I put the team through that didn't have to happen. Afterward, I met with my manager to walk through what went wrong, and I realized I had been relying too heavily on a single forecast view. And so, I built a simple crosscheck process that compared the last four weeks of demand and open orders and safety stock before submitting anything. And since putting that system in place, we didn't have another replenishment issue. And that experience taught me the importance of validating data from multiple angles, slowing down, and making sure that you are protecting accuracy. Do you have experience with specific tool or software like Salesforce? This is a quick check of your skill set. So, answer briefly and confidently, and they will ask follow-up questions if necessary. If you don't have that skill, share how you're working on it or share how you've built similar skills. Your answer can sound like, "Do I have experience with HubSpot? Don't talk them out of it by pointing out how you might not be the perfect fit. Now is the time to dive into your past experience. Touch on your most recent work experience, give a couple of highlights about it, and then move to your role before that, and then speak more briefly about roles farther back in time. You don't need to walk through your whole work history, just the important roles. And you can round it out by saying something like, "And I held several junior level roles and internships prior to that." What's so important here is to not explain your past roles super accurately, but instead explain them where you're only focusing on tasks and accomplishments that are most relevant for the opportunity you're interviewing for. Such as if you're going for a program manager role, but your biggest accomplishment from your last role was closing a big business development deal. Now, Mr. Wolf of Wall Street. That is impressive, but don't mention it because that's not going to help you land this role. If you're a recent grad or switching fields, pull from class projects, volunteer work, or even hobbies where you've used similar skills when you share your highlights. You don't have to pull exclusively from official work experience because once more, say it with me, focus on what's most relevant. Also, brevity is your best friend here. So, keep your answer to under two minutes. The resume already gives them your entire discoraphy. This is about making your greatest hits playlist. Part three, why this role? This third part is the secret to ending your answer with a bang and keep from trailing off at the end of your answer trying to figure out how to tie it up with a bow. Share why this role at this company is the right next career step for you. The reason why this is so powerful is it focuses the interviewer now on the future. a future where you are in it. Because interviewers genuinely want to know, are you truly interested in this role? Are you likely to stay in it? You shouldn't recite the company's mission statement like a robot. Instead, share some things that interest you about the job description and the company to show that this role fits with where you see your career going. Maybe you're interested in the product. Maybe the role has a global scope that excites you. Maybe your friend works there and says, "This company is simply the best. >> Whatever it is, connect the dots." This final part of your answer tells them, "I know what I want, and it's this." Here's an example of a seasoned finance professional. I am a finance leader focused on operational forecasting and strategic planning in the health tech industry. In my current role as director of FPNA, I manage planning for a business unit that grew from $90 million to $250 million in three years. I've led budgeting cycles, built forecast models, and partnered with product and sales teams to align investments to company strategy. Before that, I spent several years in corporate finance where I improved reporting processes and led a team responsible for monthly close, variance analysis, and board level presentations. Earlier in my career, I held several analyst roles where I developed my foundation in modeling and business partnering. And what interests me the most about this senior finance manager role is the focus on building forecasting discipline during a period of a rapid scale. And that aligns very well with what I've done in the past of creating that clarity, reducing those surprises, and helping the leadership make informed decisions. And so I feel like I could make an impact right away. What are your salary expectations? Simply ask, "What is the salary range for this role?" And you could tell them whether or not you're comfortable with it. If they don't have a range, you can say that you're flexible and looking for the best overall package. If they really want a specific number, you can say based on the other similar roles I'm seeing, companies are paying X to Y, does that fit in your budget? Okay, sure we can play that game. So knowing this, I would say something that connects to your values and shows something about your character. Describe a situation where you had to deal with a difficult coworker or another version is where you had to resolve an interpersonal conflict. Raise your hand if you've been personally victimized by this question. Oh, this is a very common question and one that I've had to help my clients with a lot because this answer could make you come off the wrong way like you're talking smack on your co-workers. Essentially, the interviewer is asking you this because we've all worked with difficult people in our day and they're trying to understand your ability to communicate and adapt. So, pick a scenario where there was a bit of tension and where you were the bigger person. Don't share that you avoided them or you had any sort of attitude about it. A good answer would sound something like this. When I worked as a staff nurse, I had a co-worker who often changed patient care tasks without communicating it to the rest of the team. For example, she would adjust medication timing or move a dressing change earlier in the shift, and the rest of us wouldn't find out until we checked the chart, and it led to duplicate work and more importantly, confusion to the patients. I asked if we could talk in a quieter moment, and I approached it from the angle of patient safety and teamwork, not blame. I said something like, "I've noticed a few times the care plan gets adjusted without the rest of us knowing and this makes it harder for us to coordinate and I just want to make sure that we're giving the patients the most consistent care. Is there a way that we could sync more clearly?" And she explained in her previous unit, the nurses were encouraged to update tasks on the fly to move quickly and she didn't realize that our team relied so heavily on verbal confirmation. We agreed to do brief check-ins at the start of each shift to go over any planned changes and to send a quick message through our internal system whenever adjustments were made. Very quickly, within days, our whole workflow smoothed out. The whole team communicated better, and she later told me that she appreciated me coming to her with explicit expectations rather than everyone having to guess. If you were an animal, which animal would you be? or really any offshoot of these wacky questions that have nothing to do with the role or your competency. And they likely have some convoluted pseudo psychology rationalization of what different answers mean. But really, this question is a bit of a power trip where they are watching you squirm. [screaming] >> For any of these types of questions, a special trick to think of a really good answer is to think of the trait you want to exhibit for this role and then build your answer back from that. An example is if you were a chief of staff and a core skill you would have is anticipating needs. And so I'm thinking, okay, what animal is good at anticipating needs? Um, how about a seeing eye dog? So you might say, well, if I was an animal, I'd be a seeing eye dog because they are great at watching people and anticipating needs. Or another version of this question is, if you were a superhero, what would your superpower be? And so again, we're thinking, okay, anticipating needs, what superpower would that be? You could say, "Well, if I had a superpower and I would be the power to see into the future because I can predict what's going to happen and I can anticipate needs for the team." So, that's the trick to these questions and it is exhausting, but here we are. Describe yourself in three words. Going into the job interview, you have deeply studied the job description and reviewed your notes from your previous interviews where they gave you additional information about the role. Right. Right. So, when you answer these questions, use three words from the job description. Here's what you're going to do. So your answer would sound something like >> the first word I would use to describe myself is coach because as a director of sales over the past two years I've helped five reps move into senior roles by giving them structured training and opportunities to stretch and then do an answer like that for two more words. When answering why do you want this job? Why do you want to work here? You should touch on three potential areas. The first is the role. This is the most important thing to talk about because they aren't looking for someone who simply loves the company and is trying to get their foot in the door. No, they want someone who is allin on this specific role, the duties, the projects, the goals. They want you to be a master of it and see yourself in this role for years to come. So say specifically why this role is the best next career move for you. Geek out about what you like about the duties, how you're eager to jump in on the projects that you'd work on, and how you feel like you could make a great impact. Next, you can talk about the team. Talk about the people you've met in the interview process. If this question comes up a few interviews in, and say their darn names, okay? It's going to sound like you're already a member of the team. Say specifics of what stood out to you in the conversations with them, how you could learn so much from them and vice versa. How you could see you all working well together and how working with great people makes all the difference. You know, you're lobbing on that sweet praise of true things that you noticed about the team. Finally, you can talk about the company. Discuss what you like about the product, what you're looking forward to accomplishing, why you're excited about the future of this industry, and how you feel that the company is aligned with your values. Now, warning, I would say stay away from an answer that includes things like the location, commute, salary, benefits, because a lot of that is quite similar across roles and companies. such as a huge mistake I see people making is they answer this question with one of the reasons being I'm really interested in this role because it is remote. Okay, millions of jobs are remote. It's like asking someone why are they interested in dating you and they say it's because you have brown hair. Not really a reason with any sort of depth that you would feel good about. Here's an example. I'm excited about the product marketing manager role because it's the blend of work I want to be doing next. I am specifically interested in patientf facing products where I can own the go to market strategy. I also really enjoyed meeting Marcus and Sam. They were honest about the challenges and clear about the direction they want the team to go in. And I'm excited about the company because I've been following your past few releases and the way you're building those AIdriven features. It really shows a long-term vision. It feels like a place I could be for the long haul. The first question is, are you familiar with our company? This is usually asked in the first round recruiter call. This is not a test. They're really trying to figure out how long they should make their explanation of what the company does so that you truly understand. It is impressive to know some things about the company, saying things like, "Yes, I downloaded the app." Yes, I was on your website last night. Yes, I saw the news article about the new product that launched. That will instantly put you in an advanced bracket above other candidates. And then say, but I'd love to hear more about the company and role from your perspective. Here is the two-part formula for answering what is your strength. Part one, name a strength or two that matches a key competency of the role. So take a look at the job description and pay special attention to the prominent skills you see. Take for instance this job description for the VP of business affairs and legal. What are some of these skills that they emphasize? It says here skilled in complex negotiations and handling conflict resolution. What you could emphasize is your strength being excellent at negotiation. Of course, if this isn't one of your strengths, don't say so. But I'm guessing if you're going for this role, I have a feeling you're likely pretty strong at it. Part two, we've got to give an example. A major mistake I see people doing with this answer is they list descriptors and only descriptors. Naming cliche adjectives that you think your interviewer wants to hear is not going to set you apart. Here's an example for a creative director. One of my key strengths is my ability to translate ambiguous ideas into clear creative concepts that teams can execute on. In my last role as creative director at a techdriven wellness brand, we were preparing to launch a new product category with very little creative direction from leadership. And so I led a discovery sprint. I pulled insights from re user research and I built three distinct creative territories that all aligned with broader brand strategy and this gave crossf functional teams a concrete vision to rally behind and we produced a campaign that generated the highest engagement of the year and it helped drive a strong first quarter launch. Now let's move into the second part of the question which is what are your weaknesses? If talking about your weaknesses intimidates you that tracks because I warn you that you can say a weakness here that could be detrimental and they'll kick you to the curb. Don't necessarily view this question as them trying to bamboozle you. Every human has weaknesses and a good manager wants to understand what are your weaknesses so that they can best manage you and set you up for success and put you in a role that plays to your strengths and doesn't rely on your weaknesses. Here's the three-part formula for answering what are your weaknesses. Part one, name something that you're legitimately not great at, but don't choose just any weakness. Choose one that you're already working on and improving. and make sure it is not a core component of the job. Such as a salesperson should never say a weakness that they're working on is cold calling or facing rejection. Those are too important to the role. Here are weaknesses you can say when you're asked the question in a job interview. What is your greatest weakness? A lot of roles require some element of doing a presentation, but it's usually not a huge part of the role. So, this is a really good one to say and it's super relatable. Public speaking is the top fear of humans besides death. Another weakness could be not taking enough risks. You can talk about how in the past you regretted not making bolder moves and decisions in your jobs. You can talk about how you might feel uncomfortable initiating conversations at networking events. This is again very relatable. A lot of people feel like they're not good at this. Part two, explain how you're working on it. Describe the steps that you are already taking to improve that weakness. Part three, articulate the results you've seen from improving. It's not about the weakness going away completely because then they may ask you for another weakness that currently exists, but this shows that you are working to better yourself. The best answers are ones that showcase your ability to take feedback well or demonstrate your self-awareness and they prove that you take the initiative to level yourself up. Here's an example of a weakness also for a creative director. A growth area I've been actively improving is resisting the urge to jump into execution mode too early. I am a hands-on creative by nature and earlier in my career I sometimes found myself adjusting designs or copy instead of guiding the team to explore the solution themselves. And so to address this I've built a practice of asking more coaching style questions in the early reviews and establishing clearer creative principles at the start of each project. And I've also done some leadership training focused on creative empowerment. So now my team definitely feels more ownership. I do like to dabble sometimes, but it's been a lot better now that I've been able to take a little bit of step back.

And I created digital tracking sheets because it was too much to like sanitize the markers and sanitize the colored pencils and get out the stickers. And um, you can also digitally data track and just show your students um, this is the digital goal. This is this is where we're performing. you know, how can we help get there next time? And I think that something really cool about that is just helping our students um understand like one, you can do it, and two, if you're doing it here, how can I help you do it there as far as generalization goes? So, maybe we're we're really good at doing it in the therapy session, but maybe we need to move our therapy sessions into the cafeteria or talk to support staff about like what that looks like in the classroom setting. So just kind of um breaking down those barriers, making it obvious, brightening the lines of like did I reach reach my goal and did I make successful you know um demonstrations during this session. Hi, I am Katie Arnold. I am a speech language pathologist about 18 19 years now. Um I have worked in a variety of settings. Um I'm a spouse to a military service member and so I've gotten a chance to travel around and um work in early intervention and private practice in schools. Um and so yeah, I've I've always been in pediatrics and um kind of have found myself in the specialty area of uh focusing on um gestalt language development and um I came into the content probably around um 2011 and I found um Marge Blanc's article finding the words to tell the whole story um because I was a little bit frustrated with looking at progress over time, seeing a lot of the same goals. And then again, I was moving from like one place to another, going from very little ones to my older high school age, young adults, and I'm like, what is going on here? There has to be more. Um, and so thankfully, I came across um Marshblanc's content later. um devoured her book, Natural Language Acquisition on the Autism Spectrum, um once that was published in 2012 and um found my way into working with um Alex Ekos of meaningful speech um in 2021 and really been spending the last few years of my career supporting clinicians and educators in understanding more about Kashal development. And you know, last season we did the entire season on Gustalt language processing and what that looked like and we couldn't just nail down an interview, but this season Katie is all about carryover. And I was like, who could I have on here to talk about carryover and what that looks like? and you were the first person to pop into my head because I just think you have such a well-round because of what you just mentioned, you have such a well-rounded view on the ages, the different types of of um like communication styles that someone might have and then how that all relates to gestalt language development. So, we keep using this term GLP, gestal language development. If someone has never heard that term before, could you just give a little brief overview of what that is? >> Sure. So, when we think about language development, we often think about our little ones that um start with individual words um and they point and they reference it and then they get two words together and they combine those two words and three words and beginning grammar emerges. And that is one way that we develop language. That's considered analytic language development. There is another way to develop language and that is gestalt language development. And for our gestalt language processors um who are going through that natural process of gash language development their first stage isn't a single word. It is a intonationally defined unit of meaning or a whole chunk. Um and they're tuning into the sound stream of our language. They're the rise and the fall of our language. And so for our gashalt language processors, their first stage of gestalt language development is delayed echalleia or scripts or gestalts. Um and so they are listening to the language around them and then during meaningful experiences they are tuning into that language and storing it for later use. So, um, you might hear very rich inonation, um, because they're using the same intonation as the original speaker. Um, and so, in in stage one of Gashall English development, they're acquiring all of this these whole chunks of language around them. And then they can move through the stages of of gashalling English development, moving into stage two, which is mitigation, a breaking down and trimming down of those gestalts, stage three, isolating those individual single freed words. Um, and so at that stage, it's they're processing those words for kind of the the first time as a one for one meaning. And that those individual words have flexibility, and then they move into stage four, where those individual words are now put together flexibly, and they're starting to begin to develop grammar. Um, and then they can move into more complex grammar as they move into stage five and six. >> That's such a great little like summary of everything. [laughter] I think I might even throw in the show notes maybe some one of our amazing handouts or graphs related to that development chart because I love how you kind of summarized in how it is just a natural way to develop language. It's not a diagnosis. It's not a treatment method. It's just how a child might develop language. Um the only other thing I was going to add for someone who's an AAC user and using creative communication, you mentioned that inonation piece. Sometimes that even for our creative communicators looks like using their body >> or using manipulating media and showing you that that's how they are processing. They're processing in chunks. They're processing in in meaningful intonation whether it be music or audio clip or a video clip. And they might be showing you um replayment of that within their natural their natural environments. Right. >> Yes. >> So speaking of Oh, go ahead. >> No, you're good. So, speaking of natural environments, what would you say or would be some good environmental supports in in whether homes, classrooms, clinics, um you know, thinking about some tangible takeaways that staff or families could use to support a gustalt language processor? Well, we've talked about how it's a natural process of developing language. And I think when individuals are delayed in a process, whether it be an analytic language processor or gal language processor, it seems that from my observations, and I've done it before, we almost strip it down our interactions to be very bland and very um robotic. And so my number one strategy would be don't do that. [laughter] keep it natural. Um, making sure that we are communicating with the child in front of us. We're not um we're thinking about them as as a child or as an individual. or thinking about them um and the the exchange in and wanting to communicate with them um in a way that is meaningful and that is connected and that has an authentic relationship to it. Um because who wants to who wants to talk with somebody who is correcting them all the time, telling them what to say, how to say it, when to say it. Nobody does wants to do that. And so if we can really keep things natural and keep a nice flow to the conversation using kid-friendly language um that can be really helpful um and just continuing the spontaneity um of of language in the exchanges. That would probably be my top broad tip there. Um, on the topic of kid-friendly language, um, using language, knowing that a gestal language processor in particular in stage one or is acquiring whole chunks of language, um, thinking about shifting our language from questioning um, and prompting um, to more of just declarative language um, modeling language about, you know, that's more commenting or I really like self-narration. Um like if I'm talking about, you know, I almost like want to give a child a choice. Do you want this or that? And that becomes very again kind of um I don't know, very clinical. It kind of becomes very much of a of a teaching language moment. But I still kind of curious. I'm I do a lot of selft talk like ah I wonder what we should eat. Hm. How about apples? And usually the child will jump right in and you know if they want apples they'll let you know they want those apples and if not um they can always you know be able to to show us and tell us in other ways. Um but a lot of selft talk can be really helpful because if that language is acquired by a gashalt language processor and you stored and used later in that meaningful moment they too might say how about some apples um and so that is um a one way to be able to support their their language and what I love about the examples you're giving Katie is that they're natural so you know there's a natural opportunity for flow and for acknowledgement and versus like that systematic clinical approach you're talking about, right? Like if I pull a flash card out and I have a picture of an apple on it and then I say apple, like that's not natural, right? So like what how does that really relate to my environment? How does that relate to my day? Like there's not that >> that natural piece of processing and understanding it versus how about some apples is if we're pulling out an apple cuz it's lunchtime. Like there's that natural ability to to learn and make a connection. And then you know research supports that too, right? There's research that says if you learn something as a child naturally in play, it only takes 16 around 16 opportunities, 16 to 20 to be able to embed that and have that in your inventory versus 200 plus opportunities in some kind of rigid context, right? Um I'll see if I can find that that [clears throat] citation for for the notes here, but I think that makes that makes sense to us, right? We're wanting our kids, even our gestalt language processors, we're wanting to make natural connections. We don't want them to just have like a bank of things that they can recite or um pull on command, right? That what what you and I would call survival language, right? That robotic like, oh, I can pull that word, >> right? And I think that you can hear it too in the exchanges. You can hear what is naturally acquired from a child um because it sounds natural when it when they use it. Um, and versus a more taught language approach, it can sound very robotic. It can be very rigid. It doesn't feel authentic to the child themsel. Um, it's not as joyful even as as natural language that can be naturally acquired. Um, so >> and it's that easily carried over. >> Right. Right. And that was, you know, one of the things I was thinking about when you were talking about carryover. Um, one of the things that I enjoy most about supporting gasholing processors through like through a very natural um interaction, a very child-led interaction is there isn't really need for carryover. Um, you know, you >> like systematic carryover, >> right? Right. you're modeling in in the moment during natural interactions during those meaningful experiences. And so as you're looking at um you know across you know their school day or the home environment or you all of all of that happens a lot more naturally where you don't have to think about okay now I've taught this skill and now I have to figure out how can I generalize this skill. Okay. Now, I'm gonna create a new um curated situation in this other environment and try to teach the skill that in this environment. Um it the carryover happens just so much more naturally whenever you're approaching it from um a very naturalistic, you know, interaction. Um, so that would be I mean that kind of leads me into another I guess tip if you will is thinking about in some environments I think having child-led interactions. That means um taking sensory preferences into account um thinking about um you know gestalt thinking like a whole part thinking um allowing for the child to lead in both play and in their language. um not really having a plan, being kind of the supporter of we know what the child has in mind as opposed to being the keeper of it. Um, so when we're working with a gashol language processor and wanting to support some environments, I will say are easier to be childled more often than others. But I would also encourage others to not say, "Oh, I can't do it. You know, I'm in a school setting." School settings can be challenging. There is a lot of the day that is very structured and very adult- driven, but I think you can still find moments to be child- led. And those small moments can build to some really big things for a child. It can allow for them to feel safe in their environment and not judge. They can take more risk. Um, when we're thinking about why be child-led, um, I go to like self-determination theory where it's like, you know, you want to be able to build, um, you know, autonomy, competency, and relatedness. And through those things, you know, the child becomes intrinsically motivated. We all do, not just the child. I I feel more motivated when I have autonomy, competency, and relatedness as a part of my workplace, a part of my relationships. Um, and so when we can just find those small moments to be more child-led, um, and build intrinsic motivation, um, some really great things can happen in in the classroom, in your therapy sessions, in the home environment. Um, and I think that's one thing that can really just support carryover, if you will, across all environments. >> And I'm actually thinking back like because you were the moderator and I think still are for the meaningful speech forum and some of those questions. - Balance Calibration || Guideline References || Part-1 ||: Kind: captions Language: en hello everyone welcome back to yet another learning video do you know that what are the various calibration parameters of a balance what is the acceptance criteria for each parameter which guideline we should refer to learn about the balance calibration so all these questions will be answered in next few minutes so let's get started without any further delay [Music] a typical balance is calibrated with the help of five calibration parameters those are number one repeatability number two accuracy number three sensitivity number four linearity and number five is eccentricity so these are the five main calibration parameters of a balance which are used globally by all the pharma companies now comes the acceptance criteria for each parameter first comes the repeatability the acceptance criteria for the repeatability is not more than 0.10 percent then comes the accuracy the acceptance criteria for the accuracy is plus or minus 0.10 percent then comes the sensitivity the acceptance criteria for the sensitivity test is not more than 0.05 percent then comes your linearity and the acceptance criteria for the linearity is not more than 0.05 percent and finally comes your eccentricity and the acceptance criteria for the centricity is not more than 0.05 percent so here you can see that for these two parameters reproducibility and accuracy these two parameters have the same acceptance criteria that is not more than 0.10 percent and for these three parameters sensitivity linearity and eccentricity for these three parameters acceptance criteria is not more than 0.05 percent so this was all about your acceptance criteria for all the calibration parameters of a balance now comes the last learning of today's video that is which guideline we should refer to know about the balance calibration so answer to this question is guys u.s pharmacopoeia channel chapter number 41 and 1251 along with that one more guideline you can refer to know about the balance calibration that is european pharmacopoeia journal chapter number 2.1.7 if you will read usp channel chapter number 41 and 1251 then you will come to know that few of the calibration parameters are covered in general chapter number 41 whereas few calibration parameters are covered in jannah chapter number 1251 so let us see what are the calibration parameters are given in usp journal chapter number 41. so guys repeatability and accuracy test parameters are given in journal chapter number 41 whereas in jannah chapter number 1251 four calculation parameters are given and those are sensitivity linearity eccentricity and repeatability if you will see these two journal chapters then you will come to know that repeatability test is covered in both the journal chapters so guys this was all about today's video so let us quickly rewind today's learnings so today we have learned three things about the balance calibration the first thing which we have learned today is that there are five calibration parameters of balance number one repeatability number two accuracy number three sensitivity number four linearity and number five is eccentricity the second thing which we have learned today is that about acceptance criteria for all the calibration parameters and we came to know that for repeatability and accuracy tests are having same acceptance criteria that is not more than 0.10 percent whereas linearity eccentricity and sensitivity tests are not more than zero point zero five and third thing which we have learned today is that there are two journal chapters are given in the u.s pharmacopoeia which talks about the balanced calibration those are usb 41 and usb 1251 along with that we also came to know that in european pharmacopoeia general chapter number 2.1.7 talks about balance calibration hope you have learned something new today before winding up let me tell you one - Qualification in pharmaceutical industry l Interview Questions: Kind: captions Language: en here are the top 12 frequently Asked interview questions about qualification in pharmaceutical industry subscribe to channel for more informative content we will start with the very basic question what is equipment qualification wh in anex 3 defines qualification as documented evidence that premises systems or equipment are able to achieve the predetermined specifications when properly installed and or work corly and lead to expected results second very frequently Asked question when we should perform equipment qualification answer is in three cases we should perform equipment qualification first at the initial introduction of new equipment premises or system to the facility secondly periodic requalification and third when major changes done to the qualified equipment premises or system next important question which qualification documentation are done prior to introduction of new equipment system to the facility answer is preparation of URS that is user requirement specification then fat that is Factory acceptance test sat site acceptance test purchase procedure and change control procedure are done for new introduction of equipment or system to the facility next question at which stage of qualification equipment specific Sops shall be ready answer is W anx 3 states that all relevant Sops for operation maintenance and calibration should be prepared during qualification however it is minimum requirement that at least draft sop shall be ready at operational qualification stage and shall be attached with respective qualification protocols next important question at which stage of qualification calibration of component shall be done answer is calibration shall be initiated during installation qualification to ensure that the system operates according to the described specifications and because the calibration status could have been affected during transport and installation thereafter it should be performed at regular intervals in accordance with the calibration program and Sops this one is very frequently Asked question why three batches or three test Cycles are considered for equipment qualification answer is consecutive three batches or test Cycles data gives enough statistical information to conclude on the repeatability of results one or two batches data May found insufficient whereas obtaining data from more batches probably will give repeative results next question which guidelines instruct about equipment qualification first one is usfd guidance for industry q7a good manufacturing practice guidance for for active pharmaceutical ingredients second wh anex 3 and third PDA technical report number 31 validation and qualification this one is a tricky question which document shall be prepared before URS or change control answer is URS that is user requirement specification document is prepared for understanding the expectation from proposed equipment or system only after complete and and approved URS we will be in state of proposing Change Control hence after agreement from all cross functional teams and approval of URS Change Control shall be initiated in qms next question is can we combine installation and operational qualification protocol as IQ answer is depending on your needs and the complexity of the system qualification protocols can be combined provided that all key qualification test are covered during Protocol preparation and its execution next question which qualification test are done after introduction of equipment in facility answer is design qualification installation qualification operational qualification and performance qualification next very commonly asked question is what is difference between qualification and validation answer is qualification is directly related to equipment systems or softwares and validation is directly related with the processes the term qualification essentially referred to equipment utilities and systems and it should be completed before process validation last important question whether deviation shall be raised during Yes, >> that's the question we want. >> That's the question. >> Um, and this is probably one of the most common questions, if not the most common question that we get both from patients and from healthcare providers. And at the crux of it that the the baseline is obesity is a chronic disease. It's a chronic disease. It's a lifelong disease. So we need to treat it chronically. We need to treat it lifelong. And this is also why we don't call the medicines weight loss medicines. Right? It's not about weight loss per se. It's about maintenance. And what the medicines do is not weight loss. They recalibrate. They lower the enough point so that you your body wants to carry less weight, wants to carry less fat. So that's exactly what happened to you when you lost all that weight. Your enough point came down and the weight followed. Now what do we want to do? We want to maintain. We want to maintain that new enough point. So we want to maintain that new weight. What would happen if we would stop it? Well, the enough point, your enough point would go back up. And what happens when the enough point goes back up? Guess what? You chase it. You go right back up. So you regain the weight. So for most people on average, they regain the weight. You mentioned blood pressure. Okay. So let's say you were taking a blood pressure medication and your blood pressure came down beautifully, wonderful. What would happen if you stopped taking the blood pressure medicine? Blood pressure would go back up and we're not surprised. It's the same thing with these medications. So right now what we're finding is in order to maintain that weight reduction. In order to maintain that weight, you have to keep taking the medicine. In terms of the dose, you know, we're working all these things out. We're trying to figure out what the best thing is in terms of maintaining. right now. Just like for other diseases, the medicine that worked for you, the dose that worked for you is likely to maintain, but there could be some variability. >> Yeah, I tried I tried to beat this, remember? So, uh these medications GLP-1 receptor agonists and and GLP-1 GIP receptor GLP-1 receptor agonist have been used for the treatment of diabetes for 20 years. So, we've been monitoring for side effects for long-term side effects for 20 years. Can I say we know what's going to happen after 30, 40, 50 years? No. With millions of people taking medicines, will everything always be good? No. There's nothing in life that is all good. So, we have to keep on monitoring and looking for things, but right now we're not seeing any long-term detrimental side effects. Now, there are things that come up. In terms of the most common side effects of these medications, they are gastrointestinal. Um, so nausea, diarrhea, constipation, and and sometimes vomiting. Those are the most common. But the best way that we can not have those side effects or at least mitigate those side effects, lessen them, we mentioned before, which is to start at the lowest dose and go up very very slowly. We have to start at the lowest dose. So for you, so your body gets used to it. Your body gets used to it. And that also means that let's say you were going to pause, right, for any reason or stop them for any reason um because of shortage or cost or anything else. If you if you pause or stop them for any reason at any time, you have to start back at the lower doses. >> More than two weeks, right? >> It and it depends on the medicine, but yes, two to three weeks. Um because because of the >> I stopped for a year and then >> you got to start at the lowest dose. You got to start back at the lowest dose again because otherwise if you go up too quickly, you're more likely to have nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation. So start low and go slow is really the best way to go. And that takes communication with your provider. and your provider should know that. There are also things that that that patients can do to help mitigate those side effects. So, they're they're fairly simple. So, it's not eating past the point of fullness, watching out for which foods may exacerbate your symptoms. So, for a lot of people, it's fatty foods. And then the last one is eating smaller but more frequent meals. So, those three things really help in terms of mitigating side effects like nausea. Additionally, drinking enough water really helps. If we're dehydrated, one, our kidneys are not happy, and two, it can actually make nausea worse. And then the fifth thing that I think is so so so critical is if you're having side effects, tell your provider. They're there to help you. They're there to work with you and they're going to figure it out. They're going to say, "Maybe you need a lower dose for a little bit longer and then we'll go up. Maybe we can try this, you know, another way of eating for a little while and that will help you." So, they're there to help you with those side effects. I'd say if you're on the medications, and listen, I'm not just trying to promote your book. Uh, but if you're on the medications, this book is really going to help you. If you're thinking about the medications, this book is really going to help you because I have found that a lot of the providers, they're just getting used to the medications themselves. They really don't know how to advise people. That's what I've discovered. And and what I'll say is, you know, when I went to medical school, it was the late 1990s. We didn't have one lecture about obesity. Uh these medicines didn't exist and we didn't know about the biology of obesity. Leptin had just been discovered, the the hormone that your fat makes that communicates with the brain. So, we didn't have that education. So, in a way, we're playing catchup. We're playing catchup to educate ourselves to then be able to help our patients. And again, these highly effective medications have brought this on.
