chapter 2 a history of the ideal face who decides what's beautiful the concept of the perfect face has always been a reflection of the values and priorities of the time Beauty has never been about personal preference alone it has been shaped by social structures class hierarchies economic conditions and technological advancements what we consider beautiful is often tied to deeper cultural anxieties and aspirations meaning that the definition of the ideal face is always in flux in many historical periods facial Aesthetics were links to class distinctions before modern cosmetic interventions a person's appearance often signified their social economic status in the Renaissance for example round plump faces were associated with nobility Leisure and access to an abundance of food these features indicated wealth because the lower classes who performed physical labor and had fewer resources often had thinner more angular faces due to undernourishment and exposure to harsh environments the softness of a woman's face was directly connected to the ideas of fertility health and privilege making facial fullness and indicator both status and desirability as the world industrialized in the 19th century the association between thinness and refinement began to take hold particularly in the upper classes during the Victorian era a more delicate slightly hollowed face became fashionable particularly among women there was an increased emphasis on ethereal Beauty pale almost translucent skin paired with refined angular features Victorian women went to extreme lengths to maintain this look arsenic to achieve a sickly ghostly paler cons complexion that had unintended side effects of hollowing out their cheeks this reflected the cultural ideal of the time women were expected to be fragile passive and demere and their faces need to reflect this aesthetic stepping into the 1920s the flapper era marked another major transformation and beauty standards particularly in facial Aesthetics for the first time in modern history women actively sought to reduce facial fullness as a form of rebellion the rise of jazz culture Urban night life and the increasing independence of women led to a rejection of the traditional roundface ideal in favor of a more androgynous angular look women cut their hair into short bobs darkened their eyes and aimed for a thinner more streamlined face this shift was tied to changing gender roles women were fighting for the right to vote entering the workforce and gaining social autonomy and their beauty ideals reflected this departure from traditional femininity and by the time the 1950s arrived however the pendulum swung back the post-war era brought a return to Domesticity and beauty standers once again favored a soft full youthful face women were encouraged to embrace their femininity and Hollywood icons like Marilyn Monroe Elizabeth Taylor and Grace Kelly epitomize his look their radiant rounder cheeks symbolize warmth fertility and a return to traditional gender roles this shift was also influenced by the booming economy food was more abundant beauty products were widely available and cosmetic enhancements such as early versions of fillers were emerging allowing women to stly maintain their youthfulness and fullness the 1970s and 80s saw yet another change the rise of Fitness culture and the growing influence of Hollywood's workout craze which led to an emphasis on a more sculpted face the introduction of aerobics weightlifting and fad diets encouraged women to strive for a leaner more defined look both in the body and the face this was the first time that actively chiseling the face through exercise contouring and Skin Care became widespread in Beauty celebrities like Jane Fonda and Christy Brinkley represented this ideal thin but not gaunt full face but not soft embodying a careful balance that required intense discipline then came the 90s an era that introduced one of the most extreme beauty standards in modern history the heroin Chic look the grunge aesthetic fueled by fashion music subcultures glorified a sunken gaunt and almost malnourished appearance Kate Moss became the face of this trend with her hollowed out cheeks sharp jawline and almost skeletal frame and this was a direct reaction to the over-the-top glamour of the and it was meant to look raw and effortless and rebellious I honestly kind of hate this look it's kind of depressing and but this trend also had a dangerous consequences as it normalized extreme thinness and disordered eating as aesthetic choices rather than health concerns and moving into the 2000s and 2010s saw a reversal of the heroin Chic era and the rise of injectables plastic surgery and social mediad driven beauty standards instead of glorifying thinness at all costs the focus shifted to creating the perfect balance structured by soft chiseled but youthful features celebrities and influencers turned to cosmetic procedures to enhance their facial structure without the side effects of weight loss high cheekbones full lips and a sculpted jawline became the new ideal giving rise the ER of contouring and facial Uh there are plenty of free calculators out there on the internet, and you just plug in your gender, your height, your weight, and your age, and it spits out a list of calorie requirements based on your activity level. Now, for my level, I chose sedentary or little to no activity. You should choose whatever activity level best matches your lifestyle. And you'll notice the minimum calorie requirements for your body increases with each activity level. I do want to point out though that as you're losing weight, you should go back in and readjust the settings because as you lose, your body requires less calories to functions. - Metabolic Health and Body Systems at the Intersection of Nutrition and GLP-1 Drugs | Discovery Forum: Kind: captions Language: en [music] This session [music] is going to focus on what else is going on in the body. So, as these drugs course through your body for, you know, a week at a time, they're obviously not just going to be acting at the level of the gut or the level of food intake. There are a lot of different body systems that are going to be involved, and we're going to cover that with four wonderful experts. So, with that, we'll start with um Dr. uh Housing, who will be talking to us about the pancreas as one of our first body systems. Mark. Uh, thank you to the organizers for having us up here. My name is Mark Hing. I am a faculty member at UC Davis. My lab is on across the causeway. And what we do in my lab is we study we study the original target of Inkerton drugs which is here. This is an actual 3D image of a group of eyelets that we labeled, we stained for the different hormones that they produced. Most of the cells here, that pinkish red color, those are your beta cells. Those release the insulin that most of us care about. But there's also other cell types there in green and in white. And those are two different cell types that uh are well known to many of us but sometimes forgotten as to the role they play. We study how they work. Um these alpha cells and these delta cells really play a big role in shaping that insulin secretion that most of us care about. And most of us will know from our textbooks that the beta cells that release insulin uh don't do that when glucose levels are low, which is appropriate. But when glucose levels start to rise over a certain threshold, boom, your beta cells kick in. They release insulin. Um, and that's when you get insulin talking to your peripheral tissues and taking away that insulin and storing it back into your liver or your skeletal muscle or your fat. We also know from our textbooks uh that alpha cells that release glucagon are really important as a counter regulatory hormone. Whenever your blood sugar levels drop dangerously low with your alpha cells that kick in, talk to the liver and make your liver produce more glucose and restore your glucose levels. Um, however, your textbooks have are still teaching us that these two hormones work in this kind of yinyang fashion. They are functionally antagonistic hormones. And it's actually really exciting to be in this field because I think we're relearning that that's that's not the case. Yes, it is true that on the low glucose levels, alpha cells produce insulin and prod produce glucagon and that's a really important life-saving physiological response. But during the panal phase, when we're busy digesting a meal, these alpha cells, they don't oppose the beta cells. They're glucagon, as is indicated by that green arrow pointing from that alpha cell down to the beta cell. That green arrow is indicating a cooperation between alpha cells and beta cells. Alpha glucagon or sometimes alpha cell GLP-1 is actively stimulating the beta cell to produce more insulin. And it's not just glucagon that does this. It's also in human eyelets corticotropen releasing hormone and acettooline. There are multiple signals coming from the alpha cell that activate the beta cell during the prenal phase. Meaning this is a feature not a bug. Um and then there is delta cells. Uh Sean mentioned them in his slide. Delta cells make satastatin. Somatastatin is an inhibitory hormone and like anything in biology, you need to have some kind of feedback inhibition. Delta cells do that. They kind of keep the whole system from going off the rails and going off the track uh to make sure that once insulin levels are successful in bringing glucose levels back down, your delta cells are one of the mechanisms that make sure that we don't overshoot and produce too much insulin and get into hypoglycemic uh territory. So, The hierarchy, the cross talk that happens here, I think is important to summarize and it all starts with the beta cell. The beta cell itself is a glucose sensor. It's really good at sensing glucose and transmitting that in an appropriate amount of insulin secretion and as I'll show you in the next slide, those beta cells are actually tightly coordinated almost like a cardomyioite uh like a heart muscle um and they do that by gap junctions and layered on top of that are these local layers of crosstock. We call this paracrine cross talk where the alpha cell glucagon and the alpha cell GLP-1 and delta cell smatin kind of modulate beta's output and then the hormones and nutrients and neurotransmitters some of the nutrients and and precision nutrition that we're going to talk about today. They layer on top of that. They can talk and affect beta cells directly but they can also affect this local layer of cross talk that is baked into how an eyelet works. Now this is all abstract and schematic. What we do in the lab is to actually take a deep dive and a closer look as to how this works in real time. I'm going to show you this one simple vignette of that where we're looking at eyelets where we engineered them so that they are lighting up in real time as these processes take place. I'm not going to bore you with the details. I'm going to show you simply a video where you're going to see on the right in green calcium, an important second messenger, and on the left you see changes in another second messenger called cyclic AMP. Both of these things things are really important and it's changes in these second messenger levels that really are underlying the insulin that comes out of these islands. See and scare them to respond real quick. [music] And in doing so, we can get insight into the coordination between the cell types, the alpha cells and the delta cells along with the beta cells. And in doing so and in measuring what happens when incrutrin hormones are activating these cells, we can actually understand [music] which incrutin hormones are the best at driving a robust stimulation of secretion and which ones are less import less so. And I think by doing so, we're learning that some of the ways that these increin hormones that we all know and love and know work so well, some of the ways they work so well is because they are better at activating these signaling cascades I've just shown you. And so these aspects of age and obesity are reducing the heart's ability to resiliently respond to these aspects and in order to kind of take damage and overall reduce its ability to function and that leads to uh this syndrome of heart failure. Well, what that means is about 30, almost 40 years ago at this point, there was a group of scientists who discovered that there was this hormone essentially in your body, in your stomach, that signaled to your brain and it said, "I'm full." Oversimplification, not a scientist, not a doctor. And for the longest time they didn't even really know what to do with that. And adapting that natural one to make it last longer because the way a GLP-1 works is you eat some food, your body generates the GLP-1 and it and a couple of minutes later it stop the GLP-1 stops working. Yeah, the GLP-1 says, "I'm full." Then it stops working. They're like, "Well, if we could extend that out, maybe we could make you feel full longer." They could never figure out a way to make the natural one extended out longer and she talked about this whole process of the development. The scientists around the world working on it to where eventually they created a medication, a drug and people like say, "It's not a drug." It is actually a drug by FDA standards because it's not the natural GLP-1 peptide that's in your body. They didn't take that out, right? That's not what they did. They created a a medication that mimics that, sort of behaves like that, but it extends the half-life out to 5 days. So, you do the injection and these by and large are injections. We'll talk about some of the other ones later. You do the injection that first day and it goes into your body and it gradually diminishes over the course of 5 days until the fifth day, these are averages, when it gets to about half strength. Not the headlines, not the Tik Tok simplifications, the actual 2025 science that made me say, "hm, this is bigger than weight loss." So, if you're new to the term GLP1, it stands for glucagon like peptide 1, a hormone your body already makes. Pharmaceutical versions mimic or amplify it. But here's what blew the doors off in the field in 2025. GLP1s are hitting multiple pathways that influence human aging, not just weight. And the deeper we go, the more interesting it gets. So, let's unpack this in four parts. Part one, metabolic signaling. For years, people thought GLP1s worked because they made you eat less. That's true, but that's the shallowest part of the pool. The 2025 multic-enter metabolic phenotyping study changed everything. Researchers discovered that GLP1 agonist increase metabolic flexibility, normalize insulin and glucagon rhythm dynamics, re-calibrate EMPK and mTor nutrient sensing pathways, reduce hippatic glucose output, and shift mitochondrial substrate preferences towards fatty acid oxidation. This is a system reset, not a diet drug. And one of my favorite pieces of 2025 data came from a team at Karolinska Institute. They showed trazepide users improved metabolic switching, the ability to shift from burning carbs to burning fat even without weight loss. So why does this matter for aging? It matters because impaired metabolic switching is one of the earliest signs of mitochondrial dysfunction, inflammation, insulin resistance, and accelerated epigenetic aging. GLP1s in a very real sense help the metabolism act like it's younger. And yes, that's not immortality, but it is meaningful. Part two is inflammaging. Aging scientists talk endlessly about inflammaging, the chronic low-grade inflammation that drains resilience and accelerates disease. In 2025, three major studies, one from Oxford, one from UCSF, one from Kyoto, found that GLP1 agonist cut IL6, a inflammation marker, by approximately 20 to 35%. lowered TNF alpha another inflammation marker by around 18% reduced CRP another inflammation marker even when weight is stable reversed inflammatory macrofase infiltration and visceral fat and improved atapose tissue cytoine signaling and here's the wild part these anti-inflammatory effects showed up within 10 to 14 days way before meaningful weight loss this is why the 2025 5 Kyoto paper called GLP1's metabolic anti-inflammatory regulators and inflammation is at the root of many things like Alzheimer's, atherosclerosis, psychopenia, cancer risk, insulin resistance, immune aging and even depression. So when I say GLP1s may function as longevity drugs, this is the mechanism that I'm talking about. They calm the internal noise that accelerates aging. Part three is the organ specific benefits. This is the part of the science I find most compelling because it shifts GLP1s from cosmetic weight drugs into whole body protective agents. Let's go organ by organ. So for the heart, two major 2025 cardio metabolic outcome trials showed approximately 25 to 30% reduction in major cardiovascular events and around 25 to 30% reduction in hospitalization for heart failure. There were measurable improvements in endothelioeno production and it lowered arterial stiffness which is a key for biological aging. And lastly, showed improved left ventricular remodeling. Nothing, and I mean nothing, drives lifespan more than cardiovascular health. And when cardiologists start using phrases like disease modifying, you definitely pay attention. For kidneys, a 2025 German data found slowed EGRF decline in diabetics and non-diabetics and lower renal inflammation. Kidney aging is one of the strongest predictors of mortality as well. So slowing kidney deterioration is definitely a massive win for the liver. A 2025 Cleveland Clinic trial found that GLP-1 agonist reduced liver fat by approximately 60%, reduce fibrosis markers, and lower the ALT significantly. Why does this matter? Well, because metabolic liver dysfunction accelerates systemic inflammation, hormonal dysregulation, cardiovascular disease, and mitochondrial dysfunction, improving liver health is longevity 101. And lastly, the brain, which I think is slightly underrated when it comes to GLP1s. 2025 gave us two major breakthroughs in brain aging and GLP1s. First was microal inflammation suppression. Think of micro gia as your brain's cleanup crew. When they're chronically activated, they damage surrounding neurons instead of protecting them. And second was improved insulin signaling in the hippocampus. And observational data from over 1.2 million patients in 2025 showed that GLP-1 users showed lower rates of cognitive decline in observational data even after adjusting for weight. I'm not claiming GLP-1 cures Alzheimer's, but they are clearly affecting brain resilience and that makes them at minimum neuroprotective candidates. And lastly, body composition. Some podcasts, I think, talk around this and we're not going to do that. And here's the truth. Rapid weight loss always risks muscle loss and GLP1s don't change that physiology. The 2025 Mayo Clinic body composition study was the wakeup call. On average, 25 to 35% of weight loss on GLP1s was lean mass. And the range was huge from 10 to 55%. And what does that mean? That some people maintained muscle beautifully while some unfortunately accelerated their aging. And there were three variables that the data pointed to that decided that huge gap. First was protein intake. People with higher protein intake lost far less muscle. Second was resistance training. This almost completely prevented psychopenic weight loss. And lastly was the V2 max maintenance. Cardiorespiratory fitness protected the mitochondrial function during rapid weight loss. So when people say GLP1s destroy muscle, that's not accurate. It's the under fueling, undertraining that destroy the muscle. The drug didn't change the physiology. It just magnified it. All right, I know that that was a lot of science, but here's why it really matters in the real world. These mechanisms don't mean anything if you don't have a strategy to preserve what matters most. And before we head into the real world application of what we just discussed, let me tell you where this is all heading. because the 2025 2026 pipeline makes this story even more exciting. Here are some of the most important compounds coming down the line. The first is retatrutide. 2025 data showed unprecedented body recomposition, drastic visceral fat reduction, stronger inflammatory improvements, and improved fatty acid oxidation. This drug doesn't just reduce caloric intake. It rewires metabolic pathways across multiple organs. Expect approval for this drug for 2026 or 2027. Second are AMOLIN and GLP-1 combinations. 2025 trials showed smoother appetite regulation, less nausea, and more stable metabolic effects. This might become the next WGOI class. And third are quad agonist with GLP1, GIP, glucagon, and amalin. Very early 2025 data suggests strong synergistic metabolic control, better organ protective effects, and possibly less muscle loss due to glucagon modulation. These are very early, but they're the compounds longevity researchers are watching. So, speaking of the future, here is the big question that we're trying to answer. Are GLP1s really longevity drugs? And here's what we know for now. They are not rejuvenation therapies. They don't reverse biological aging like reprogramming might someday and they are not lifespan extending in healthy weight individuals just yet. No data is really showing that. But in people with metabolic dysfunction, which is most adults, they do meaningfully reduce the biological drivers of aging. Summarizing what we've been talking about, GLP1s improve inflammation, insulin signaling, mitochondrial function, cardiovascular outcomes, kidney resilience, liver health, and cognitive risk profiles. GLP1s also then lower visceral fat, oxidative stress, fibrosis markers, microal activation, and organ aging burden. GLP-1s are not miracle drugs, but they are the first mass adopted medications to meaningfully influence the pace of age related decline indirectly but powerfully. They are leveling the playing field for millions of people locked in metabolic dysfunction. They are reducing disease risk in ways lifestyle alone often couldn't reach. When paired with resistance training and adequate protein, they do become a legitimate tool for extending health span. All right, my favorite part. Let's clear out the noise, unpack the misconceptions, and bust the myths that make GLP1s either wildly overhyped or unfairly dismissed. Because truth is nuance, and honestly, the nuance is the interesting part. Myth one, GLP1s melt muscle. We talked about this already, but the muscle loss is common, but muscle preservation is absolutely possible. The drug isn't the issue. It's the undereating and inactivity. Loss of appetite leading to reduced protein intake leading to inconsistent training is the recipe for lean mass loss. Myth two, GLP1s fix your metabolism permanently. The truth is that they do correct dysfunction while you're on them. If you stop the medication without new habits in place, metabolic drift often returns. Not because GLP1s failed, but because the underlying behaviors weren't rebuilt. Think of GLP1s as the scaffolding. You still have to build the house. Myth three, everyone should max the dose for best results. The truth is that high doses are often worse for aging goals because high doses correlate with excessive appetite suppression. difficulty eating enough protein, greater risk of lean mass loss, more GI issues, and faster weight loss than physiology can handle safely. Longevity dosing is the opposite of weight loss dosing. Start low, go slow, and stop early. Think about it this way. The best dose for you is the minimum dose that supports health, not the maximum one that crushes hunger. Myth four, GLP1s work the same for everyone. Well, body composition, age, training history, protein intake, baseline insulin, genetics, and even microbiome all influence response. This is why personalization matters. The best GLP-1 protocol is not a downloadable PDF. It's a tailored plan. And last, myth five, GLP1s are a shortcut. This is the big one. GLP1s aren't shortcuts. They're stabilizers. They create metabolic conditions that make long-term change more achievable. But the change still has to be built. There is no drug that replaces the fundamentals of exercise and nutrition because nothing else gives the same magnitude of benefit. GLP1s don't skip the work, but they do help you make the work more effective. So, here we are at the end of an episode that reflects one of the biggest shifts happening in longevity right now. What started as a diabetes drug then became a weight loss phenomenon is now stepping into a much more nuanced much more important role. A metabolic stability tool that can either support your health span or work against it if you don't use it wisely. So let's come back to the three big questions we started with. Why is it a disease? It's a disease because your body weight is under tight regulation by lots of hormones in the body. What hormones are we talking about? You know, leptin. Leptin is a hormone discovered in 1995 by Rudy Liel and colleagues. Comes from the comes from fat tissue goes to the brain and tells the brain, "Okay, you're full. There's enough fat. We have enough fat for survival." Okay. [snorts] If you lose weight by diet and exercise, you lose fat tissue. Less fat tissue, less leptin is produced. And so less leptin goes to the brain and you get the other side of the coin coming out which is ooh we're we don't have enough fat to survive we're get we get really hungry. Okay that kind of hunger when you lose weight and when you lose weight with diet and exercise I don't know if you've ever felt that kind of hunger I have. Medications are, you know, obesity medications, the older ones or fentamine. Uh, FDA approved in 1959 when we thought that obesity was not a disease. So we approved it was approved for 3 months use only still out there for 3 months. Um fentamine is a like a crude appetite suppressant. Goes to the brain shuts off appetite. It's a real drug. It had it can it has side effects like high blood pressure, palpitations, insomnia. We also have the combination of bupropion which is an anti-depressant and nrexone which is an anti-addiction agent. The brand name is Contrave. It can really help with cravings. Um, we also have cucia which is a combination of fentamine and tyroate. Top pyramate is an antiscychotic anti-convulsant agent. Um and then we have the three that I mentioned araglatide, simaglletide, tzepatide, the GLP1s which have changed the uh really been game changers in the field of obesity medicine. If your BMI is over 40, meaning you have class 3 obesity or over with diabetes or sleep apnea, you are a candidate for beriatric surgery. We like to call it metabolic beriatric surgery because it's really a metabolic surgery. We do two the the two operations we most usually do in the United States are the sleeve gasterectomy and the ru gastric bypass. The sleeve gasterectomy. It's less than an hour in the operating room. take a piece, take the lower curvature of the stomach out and uh you're out of the hospital in one night and you lose, you know, 25% of your body weight over a year on average and you keep it off. The RUI gastric bypass is a little more complicated. You create a small pouch instead of the the stomach. You bring up a piece of jigunum and that average weight loss is 33 to 35%. Again the wa the weight comes off in a year 9 to 12 months or maximum 16 months and you keep it off indefinitely. There is some weight regain but guess what there is some weight regain with medications as well because this is a chronic disease that we're talking about. There are other more more um there are other procedures that that uh are are reserved for very very high BMIs like BMI over 50 over 60 and those are the bilo pancreatic diversion uh and dadinal switch procedures and those just bypass a little bit more of the intestine. So how do how does beriatric surgery work? It works by changing the gut hormones the mill the gut hormone millure. So when food goes down the new um tract, it hits the cells that produce those satiety hormones earlier and you have you reduce your body weight set point. So we learned all about our new GLP1 medications from studying why metabolic surgery works. We used to think metabolic surgery worked by restricting the pouch, making it really small so you couldn't eat a lot of food and malabsorbing nutrients with the bypass. You bypass a piece of intestine. That's not how it works. It it it works by changing the gut hormone millure. And so our new medications are at attempts at at getting to a medical gastric bypass. So doing what the what the surgery does medically. We're certainly not there yet because metabolic surgery gives you much much more weight loss. Um, but maybe in the future with with new medications and combinations of of increin hormones coming out, we may get there. >> It almost feels full circle, you know, getting to the the mechanism of these drugs from prior weight loss strategies. Whereas why are the incrretin hormones so different? They're agonists of naturally occurring hormones. I tell patients, you know, it's not really a drug. It's a vitamin. And so it's that, you know, when you think about your body's survival instinct and when you start drastically limiting your calories, your body starts slowing down its metabolism so it can hang on >> to it. And that's just that. So when you come off of these drugs after you've been starving yourself, yeah, you're in a situation where your metabolism is very downregulated and then you have this huge appetite that comes back and so it's like this double whammy. But going back to what we said when if you do it properly and you're doing like low doses and you're still maintaining good eating habits and using as a tool, >> you don't lose your metabolism. Um and most people would ill. But the GLP1s are groundbreaking. Obesity is the same. And I think the shame around obesity and weight bias is really the big problem. It's it's more than just medicine. It's a cultural problem. What do you mean by food noise? >> So, I'm so glad you asked because John, I had never heard of it before either. So, food noise and and maybe because I've always I'm going to out myself. Okay, John. I've always been skinny. I've never had a weight management problem. So, to be a weight management doctor, never had the problem. I had to learn a lot. Food noise is that dialogue that patients that struggle with obesity or overweight have with food, right? Did I eat? Should I not? Should I eat the one more donut? Should I not eat the one more donut? Should I eat the carbs? Not eat the carbs. Do I need an extra salad? No. That thing is food noise. And a lot of people that struggle with obesity talk about the food noise. The constant dialogue they are having with food. And it is a a syndrome, I guess, or a part that's very indicative to people that have this disease process. And it's something that if you don't have the disease process, you're unaware of. Well, what we have learned is that this medication, the GLP1s, reduce food noise. So, there's no longer that dialogue that they're having with food. And I think a lot of my patients love that part of it more than anything else because it is very consuming to have this constant noise and narrative in the back of your head and it makes you feel defeated is what they explained to me. So, you're in this conversation and it feels like you keep losing cuz you're eating the donuts, right? It feels like you're losing cuz you're taking the extra slice of pizza. And I feel like that is going to widen as things progress in healthc care because the numbers are growing, the need is growing, the baby boomers are getting older, and if you're not speaking up for yourself, you know, the squeakiest will get the oil. So GLP1 is abbreviation for glucagon like peptide agonist or some people will call it glucagon like peptide receptor agonist and what it does is it mimics glucagon in our body and glucagon is a hormone that's produced by the small intestine and it has several things that it does. It triggers insulin release from the pancreas. So an insulin, we all know about that, an essential hormone that allows your body to use food that you eat for energy. It lowers the amount of glucose or sugar in the blood. And if you don't have enough insulin, your blood sugar increases and you can have diabetes. Okay? But it also blocks glucagon secretion and that's another hormone in the body and that raises blood sugar levels. So GLP1s prevent more glucose from going into your bloodstream. And you start on a very, very low dose because if you don't, the side effects would be bad and your body has to get used to it. And then periodically, you move up on that dose. And it gets a little bit stronger each time you titrate, each time you move up, such that you're trying to maximize weight loss and minimize side effects. That's why working with your doctor is so important. It is possible if you move up too quickly, if you say, "I want to lose weight faster." and you move up too quickly, that will make you sick. Turns out moving up faster doesn't actually make you lose more weight, but it might make you sick. So, work with your doctor, but it's there's nothing wrong with you.
