- Ozempic's Origin Story is Insane: Kind: captions Language: en In late 2022, it seemed like the entire&nbsp; internet was talking about Ozempic.&nbsp;&nbsp; [various speakers say “Ozempic”] Google searches skyrocketed, the hashtag Ozempic&nbsp; got hundreds of millions of views on TikTok,&nbsp;&nbsp; and pharmacies across the United States struggled&nbsp; to keep the drug in stock. By the end of the year,&nbsp;&nbsp; the FDA formally declared shortages on&nbsp; most of the major GLP-1 receptor agonists,&nbsp;&nbsp; the class of drugs that includes Ozempic.&nbsp; But most people don’t realize that&nbsp; the origin story of GLP-1 analogs was&nbsp;&nbsp; one of the wildest science stories&nbsp; in modern drug development. Like,&nbsp;&nbsp; I read about venomous lizards, and deep and&nbsp; sea creatures, and amphetamines for this story. So in today’s video, we’ll explore how researchers&nbsp; discovered the GLP-1 hormone in the body,&nbsp;&nbsp; then how two totally different research groups&nbsp; turned that hormone into a therapeutic tool,&nbsp;&nbsp; and finally, how these medications turned&nbsp; into the blockbusters we know today. But to understand how we got here, we need&nbsp; to understand the history of diabetes care. Because after World War 2, Type 2 diabetes started&nbsp; getting more common. And it was clear that doctors&nbsp;&nbsp; would need a new pharmaceutical strategy&nbsp; to treat this growing number of Type 2s.&nbsp; &nbsp; See, usually when&nbsp;&nbsp; blood glucose is elevated — like after you eat —&nbsp; the pancreas releases a hormone called insulin.&nbsp;&nbsp; That insulin binds to receptors on cells all&nbsp; throughout your body, which starts the process&nbsp;&nbsp; of bringing glucose into the cells so they can use&nbsp; that glucose for energy or store it for later.&nbsp; In type 1 diabetes, the body’s immune&nbsp; system destroys the insulin-producing&nbsp;&nbsp; cells in the pancreas called beta cells. As&nbsp; a result, their blood glucose stays elevated,&nbsp;&nbsp; unless they’re given insulin some other way.&nbsp; Type 2 is different. It’s characterized by&nbsp; something called insulin resistance — the&nbsp;&nbsp; pancreas does release insulin, but different&nbsp; tissues don’t take enough glucose into the&nbsp;&nbsp; cell even when insulin is present. As a&nbsp; result, blood glucose stays elevated.&nbsp; But the pancreas can still sense that blood&nbsp; glucose is high, so it compensates by pumping&nbsp;&nbsp; out more insulin. This cycle continues and&nbsp; over time, the insulin-producing Beta cells&nbsp;&nbsp; can fail, which means impaired insulin&nbsp; production on top of insulin resistance.&nbsp; That’s why, while insulin can&nbsp; be used for type 2 diabetes,&nbsp;&nbsp; doctors usually try other treatments first&nbsp; like lifestyle modification or other drugs. We’ve had insulin since the 1920s, but with&nbsp; the rise in type 2 diabetes after the war,&nbsp;&nbsp; doctors desperately needed new medicines to treat&nbsp;&nbsp; it. This is what made an observation in&nbsp; the 1960s so important. And it was this: Glucose taken by mouth raised insulin production&nbsp; and lowered blood glucose more than intravenous&nbsp;&nbsp; glucose did. That meant that something was&nbsp; happening when the gut processed glucose,&nbsp;&nbsp; and that was signaling the&nbsp; pancreas to secrete insulin.&nbsp; That signal turned out to be a&nbsp; hormone secreted by the gut itself;&nbsp;&nbsp; it became known as an incretin&nbsp; hormone, a term coined back in 1932. So scientists started to wonder if these incretin&nbsp; hormones might make for good diabetes treatments,&nbsp;&nbsp; so they started testing proteins in the gut to&nbsp; see if they could stimulate insulin secretion.&nbsp; The first one they isolated was called&nbsp; Gastric Inhibitory Polypeptide, or GIP.&nbsp;&nbsp; It did stimulate insulin production and lower&nbsp; glucose, so it qualified as an incretin. But&nbsp;&nbsp; early studies showed that it didn’t lower&nbsp; blood glucose in people with diabetes.&nbsp; So GIP went on the back burner… at least for&nbsp;&nbsp; a while. We’ll come back to&nbsp; it by the end of the video.&nbsp; But one of the other potential&nbsp; matches was a hormone called glucagon,&nbsp;&nbsp; which I like to think of as insulin’s buddy cop.&nbsp;&nbsp; They both come from the pancreas and work&nbsp; together to keep blood sugar in check.&nbsp; Insulin lowers blood sugar when it’s too high&nbsp; while glucagon raises it while it’s low. Fun fact,&nbsp;&nbsp; the name glucagon means glucose agonist,&nbsp; meaning it promotes glucose production.&nbsp; Scientists in 1965 found that injections of&nbsp; glucagon led to increased insulin production.&nbsp;&nbsp; And at first that seems non-remarkable —&nbsp; after all, glucagon raises blood glucose,&nbsp;&nbsp; and higher blood sugar usually&nbsp; triggers the release of more insulin.&nbsp; But here’s the counterintuitive part:&nbsp; these scientists in the 60s found that&nbsp;&nbsp; insulin rose before blood glucose went&nbsp; up. That suggested that glucagon itself,&nbsp;&nbsp; not just the elevated blood sugar,&nbsp; was stimulated insulin secretion. So glucagon seemed like a good target&nbsp; for a diabetes treatment. Unfortunately,&nbsp;&nbsp; studying how these hormones work is&nbsp; tricky — especially in mammals since&nbsp;&nbsp; the pancreas is such a crowded and complex organ.&nbsp; As you can see from this sample, most&nbsp; of the pancreas is made of acinar cells&nbsp;&nbsp; which make the enzymes we use to digest food.&nbsp; Scattered throughout the pancreas are small,&nbsp;&nbsp; lighter colored clusters of cells called the&nbsp; Islets of Langerhans. This is where insulin&nbsp;&nbsp; and glucagon come from — insulin from the&nbsp; beta cells, glucagon from the alpha cells.&nbsp; So instead of studying the&nbsp; crowded mammalian pancreas,&nbsp;&nbsp; researchers turned to animals like the anglerfish.&nbsp; Because unlike humans, angerlfish have separate&nbsp;&nbsp; organs for making digestive enzymes and&nbsp; for making hormones insulin and glucagon.&nbsp; This finally brings us to a group of&nbsp; Massachusetts-based scientists in the&nbsp;&nbsp; early 1980s. Around that time, researchers had&nbsp; discovered that in order for certain animals&nbsp;&nbsp; to make glucagon, they start by making&nbsp; a larger protein called a precursor.&nbsp;&nbsp; Then the body chops up the precursor into pieces,&nbsp; and one of those pieces becomes glucagon.&nbsp; The Massachusetts group decided to focus on the&nbsp; genes for this precursor in anglerfish. Simply&nbsp;&nbsp; put, a gene is section of DNA that provides the&nbsp; instructions for building specific proteins that&nbsp;&nbsp; we use in our bodies. More specifically,&nbsp; it tells the cell’s machinery how to put&nbsp;&nbsp; together sequences of amino acids — the&nbsp; building blocks of peptides and proteins. So by studying the gene for the glucagon&nbsp; precursor, the scientists hoped to learn more&nbsp;&nbsp; about how the peptide was built, and if anything&nbsp; interesting happens to the rest of the precursor.&nbsp; And one thing they noticed was that the precursor&nbsp; had these little regions called cleavage sites.&nbsp; I think of them like the perforations of notebook&nbsp; paper— they exist to be broken. Likewise,&nbsp;&nbsp; cleavage sites on a protein make&nbsp; it easier for the cell to clip the&nbsp;&nbsp; bigger protein into smaller pieces like glucagon. But the scientists found multiple&nbsp; cleavage sites on this precursor,&nbsp;&nbsp; so the natural follow up question was what&nbsp; does the rest of the protein break into? Well in 1982 they published an article&nbsp; showing that the precursor, preproglucagon,&nbsp;&nbsp; broke into a few different peptides.&nbsp; One of these peptides was a previously&nbsp;&nbsp; undiscovered peptide that they&nbsp; called glucagon related peptide.&nbsp; And while it might’ve been a potential incretin&nbsp; hormone, and thus, a new diabetes treatment,&nbsp;&nbsp; this was in anglerfish. We’d need to find&nbsp; out if mammals had something similar. So in 1983, a different group of scientists used&nbsp; hamster DNA to identify the mammalian version of&nbsp;&nbsp; the glucagon precursor gene. But it wasn’t&nbsp; an exact match with the anglerfish version.&nbsp; Where the fish had a 34 amino acid sequence,&nbsp;&nbsp; the mammalian version had one that was 37&nbsp; amino acids long, and then another that&nbsp;&nbsp; was 33 long. They called these glucagon-like&nbsp; peptide 1 and 2. Or GLP-1 and GLP-2 for short. Were these incretin hormones? Well,&nbsp; when researchers used the full 37&nbsp;&nbsp; amino acid version of GLP-1, it didn’t&nbsp; stimulate insulin secretion. So at first,&nbsp;&nbsp; it seemed like GLP-1 wouldn’t&nbsp; lead to the incretin effect. But then we meet Svetlana Mojsov, a&nbsp; scientist born in what is now North&nbsp;&nbsp; Macedonia. After grad school, she read&nbsp; about GLP-1 and teamed up with doctor&nbsp;&nbsp; Joel Habener, one of the authors of the&nbsp; anglerfish paper, to study it further. During their research, Mojsov noticed something:&nbsp; if you removed the first few amino acids from&nbsp;&nbsp; the full-length GLP-1 molecule, the remaining&nbsp; peptide looked even more like glucagon. And&nbsp;&nbsp; that made her wonder if the cell might actually&nbsp; cleave off the first 6 amino acids from GLP-1,&nbsp;&nbsp; and the remaining portion was&nbsp; the biologically active form. In the conclusion to her 1986 paper,&nbsp; she proposed that GLP-1 (1-37),&nbsp;&nbsp; so the full 37 amino acid peptide,&nbsp; might be a precursor to GLP-1 (7-37),&nbsp;&nbsp; a version of the peptide lacking the&nbsp; first 6 amino acids. So the shorter&nbsp;&nbsp; version might actually be the hormone&nbsp; that has an effect on insulin secretion. Which meant that maybe GLP-1 had a&nbsp; sneakier incretin effect than expected.&nbsp; To test this, she measured how well GLP-1&nbsp; (7-37) raised insulin in a rat model and&nbsp;&nbsp; compared it to the full-length peptide.&nbsp; And in 1987, they confirmed that yes,&nbsp;&nbsp; GLP-1 (7-37) is insulinotropic, meaning&nbsp; it stimulates the release of insulin. And they weren’t the only ones. Within the year, a&nbsp; research group in Denmark showed that GLP-1 (7-37)&nbsp;&nbsp; had a much stronger insulin-raising effect than&nbsp; the full-sized precursor. So the big takeaway was&nbsp;&nbsp; that (at least in test animals) the 7-37 portion&nbsp; of GLP-1 was the active form of the hormone.&nbsp; Later that year, research published in The Lancet&nbsp; confirmed that GLP-1 was made in the human gut,&nbsp;&nbsp; and that GLP-1 levels went up after&nbsp; participants consumed glucose. This meant that earlier scientists stood&nbsp; corrected, GLP-1 could induce the incretin&nbsp;&nbsp; effect after all. But like they saw with GIP,&nbsp; not&nbsp; all incretin hormones become useful medicines.&nbsp; It turned out that GLP-1 only raises insulin&nbsp; secretion when blood glucose was elevated. And&nbsp;&nbsp; that would make it a useful diabetes drug — since&nbsp; GLP-1 depended on glucose, it reduced the risk of&nbsp;&nbsp; hypoglycemia, or dangerously low blood sugar.&nbsp; GLP-1 also inhibits excessive glucagon release,&nbsp;&nbsp; which, prevents it from triggering glucose&nbsp; release from the liver even further. But the big question in the 80s was whether&nbsp; GLP-1 itself could be used as a diabetes drug. Tests in the early 90s showed that when you&nbsp; gave type 2 diabetics an IV infusion of GLP-1,&nbsp;&nbsp; insulin rose and glucose fell. So it worked&nbsp; as expected. But there was one big problem:&nbsp;&nbsp; GLP—1 cleared from the blood extremely quickly.&nbsp; When given by IV, half of the GLP-1 was&nbsp; broken down after about two minutes. So&nbsp;&nbsp; GLP-1 by itself wouldn’t be a useful drug unless&nbsp; the patient was constantly hooked up to an IV.&nbsp; These days, we know the peptide breaks&nbsp; down so quickly because an enzyme called&nbsp;&nbsp; DPP-4 cuts it up and then it’s cleared out&nbsp; by the kidneys. This discovery led to a&nbsp;&nbsp; class of drugs called DPP-4 inhibitors,&nbsp; but that’s a story for another time.&nbsp; So to sum it up — we desperately needed&nbsp; new treatments for type 2 diabetes,&nbsp;&nbsp; and GLP-1 was a promising&nbsp; candidate. The challenge then,&nbsp;&nbsp; was figuring out how to make this&nbsp; hormone to last longer in the body. One of the strategies was to find a molecule like&nbsp; GLP-1 that naturally lasted in the body longer.&nbsp; And by the early 1990s, scientists had a&nbsp; lead thanks to unrelated research into the&nbsp;&nbsp; venom of the Gila monster. These guys&nbsp; are muscular, venomous lizards found&nbsp;&nbsp; in the American southwest known for their&nbsp; brutally painful bite and slow metabolisms. As scientists learned more about the chemistry of&nbsp; the lizard’s venom, they noticed that something&nbsp;&nbsp; in the venom triggered inflammation in&nbsp; the pancreas of bite victims. And that&nbsp;&nbsp; made scientists wonder if the venom might have a&nbsp; chemical that interferes with pancreas function. Researchers publishing in 1984 found two&nbsp; potential culprits — what were called&nbsp;&nbsp; Helospectin and Helodermin. Helo referring to&nbsp; Heloderma suspectum and Heloderma horridum,&nbsp;&nbsp; the scientific name for the Gila monster&nbsp; and its relative, the Mexican beaded lizard. Helospectin and Helodermin are also known&nbsp; as exendin 1 and exendin 2. And it turned&nbsp;&nbsp; out that they both had similar&nbsp; amino acid structures to glucagon,&nbsp;&nbsp; which might explain the pancreas involvement. So by the late 1980s, start of the 90s, a New&nbsp; York based scientist named Doctor John Eng&nbsp;&nbsp; picked up on this line of research and analyzed&nbsp; venom samples from the Mexican beaded lizard.&nbsp; And in 1990, his and his team published a&nbsp; paper describing a new peptide that they&nbsp;&nbsp; called Exendin-3. But while exendins 1 and 2&nbsp; were more structurally similar to glucagon,&nbsp;&nbsp; Exendin 3 looked more like human GLP-1.&nbsp; So they ran the experiment again, but this time&nbsp; with venom from the Gila monster, and found&nbsp;&nbsp; another new peptide — Exendin-4. And just like&nbsp; Exendin-3, it was structurally similar to GLP-1.&nbsp; That raised the question — it looks kind of&nbsp; like GLP-1, but does it behave like GLP-1? And it sure did. When they gave Exendin-4 to&nbsp; dogs, it caused insulin levels to rise and&nbsp;&nbsp; glucose levels to drop. This meant that Exendin-4&nbsp; was binding to some kind of receptor in mammals;&nbsp;&nbsp; it’s just that nobody knew which receptor.&nbsp; Until 1993, where Eng and a team of other&nbsp; researchers found an answer. Exendin-4 was&nbsp;&nbsp; binding to GLP-1 receptors. That meant that his&nbsp; new peptide from lizards, Exendin-4, was binding&nbsp;&nbsp; to GLP-1 receptors in mammals and activating&nbsp; the same physiologic pathway as native GLP-1.&nbsp; But Exendin-4 had one very important feature that&nbsp;&nbsp; made it different from GLP-1. That effect on&nbsp; blood glucose lasted for hours, not minutes.&nbsp;&nbsp; Exendin-4 has a half life of close to two and&nbsp; half hours, which would make it a useful medicine. To quote that 1993 paper: “the possible clinical&nbsp;&nbsp; value of the GLP-1-agonist properties of&nbsp; exendin-4 should be seriously considered.&nbsp; Luckily, Eng was already thinking ahead. That&nbsp; same year, he filed for a patent for exendin 4,&nbsp;&nbsp; and started looking for pharma companies to help&nbsp; bring the drug to market. He started by presenting&nbsp;&nbsp; his findings at medical conferences, but he&nbsp; didn’t get much traction right away. But then,&nbsp;&nbsp; one day in 1996, he caught the attention of a&nbsp; rep from a small biotech company called Amylin.&nbsp; So Eng licensed Exendin-4 to Amylin who&nbsp; began clinical trials with it. And the&nbsp;&nbsp; results were promising. They found that two&nbsp; injections a day of exendin-4 lowered HbA1c,&nbsp;&nbsp; which is a marker for long term blood&nbsp; glucose control. So this lowered HbA1c&nbsp;&nbsp; was a desired outcome. It also led&nbsp; to a little bit of weight loss.&nbsp; Amylin developed this new synthetic version&nbsp; of exendin-4 under the name exenatide,&nbsp;&nbsp; and got FDA approval for the treatment&nbsp; of type 2 diabetes in 2005. Then,&nbsp;&nbsp; through a partnership with Eli Lilly, they&nbsp; launched the drug under the trade name Byetta.&nbsp; It was the first in a class of drugs that&nbsp; goes by a few different names. Usually,&nbsp;&nbsp; they’re called GLP-1 receptor agonists,&nbsp; which means they bind to the same receptor&nbsp;&nbsp; that our bodies’ GLP-1 does. They’re also&nbsp; called GLP-1 analogs, which means they’re&nbsp;&nbsp; structurally and /or fuctionally similar&nbsp; to GLP-1. And sometimes they’re referred&nbsp;&nbsp; to as incretin mimetics, because they&nbsp; mimic the job of incretin hormones. Whatever you call them, by 2005,&nbsp; Dr. Eng and the American companies&nbsp;&nbsp; had one solution to GLP-1’s short half&nbsp; life problem: they’d found a different&nbsp;&nbsp; molecule that has the same effect as GLP-1&nbsp; but naturally lasted longer in the body.&nbsp; But, that wasn’t the only solution.&nbsp; Because meanwhile, over in Europe,&nbsp; scientists were working on the&nbsp;&nbsp; same problem but with a totally different&nbsp; approach. Something a little more designer. Now, I’ve spent the last two videos talking&nbsp; about Denmark’s biggest pharmaceutical company,&nbsp;&nbsp; Novo Nordisk, so I won’t go into&nbsp; much depth here. The main thing you&nbsp;&nbsp; need to know is that during the 1990s,&nbsp; they were the biggest insulin producer&nbsp;&nbsp; in Europe and the second biggest in&nbsp; the world, right behind Eli Lilly.&nbsp; But for this story, we need to meet a new&nbsp; character at Novo: Lotte Bjerre Knudsen. As a Danish scientist, Lotte knew all about Novo’s&nbsp; work. She wasn’t particularly into academia, she&nbsp;&nbsp; was more interested in developing real medicines&nbsp; that could improve people’s lives. And while she&nbsp;&nbsp; initially got a job in Novo’s enzyme division, she&nbsp; started working on diabetes treatments in 1991. On the day to day, Lotte and her team would&nbsp; screen a bunch of potential drugs for diabetes,&nbsp;&nbsp; including compounds that bound to the GLP-1&nbsp; receptor. But then something unexpected happened.&nbsp;&nbsp; Lotte went on maternity leave in 1993,&nbsp; and while she was away, management at&nbsp;&nbsp; Novo Nordisk restructured certain teams, including&nbsp; the diabetes team. And when she came back in 1994,&nbsp;&nbsp; she was the only scientist left who&nbsp; was familiar with their GLP-1 project.&nbsp; And at this point, Novo Nordisk&nbsp; was more focused on insulin,&nbsp;&nbsp; and their GLP-1 research didn’t seem&nbsp; like it was going anywhere. So the CEO,&nbsp;&nbsp; Lars Rebien Sørensen, gave Lotte an ultimatum.&nbsp; In a podcast interview from 2024 he says “We&nbsp;&nbsp; did give her 12 months to say you got to&nbsp; come up with something more convincing”. Meanwhile, other scientists at Novo had&nbsp; come up with something exciting. They’d&nbsp;&nbsp; developed a method for attaching fatty acids&nbsp; to proteins, which caused the protein to bind&nbsp;&nbsp; to albumin — the most common protein in human&nbsp; blood. This created a bigger, chunkier molecule&nbsp;&nbsp; that wouldn’t get filtered out by the kidneys as&nbsp; quickly, causing it to last in the body longer.&nbsp; In 1995, they published a paper describing&nbsp; their technique, and mentioned that it would&nbsp;&nbsp; “prolong the action profile of peptide&nbsp; drugs”. And that’s exactly what it did. At first, they used their&nbsp; method on insulin molecules,&nbsp;&nbsp; creating longer lasting insulin analogs.&nbsp; The 1995 paper describes an insulin that&nbsp;&nbsp; looks like insulin detemir, which&nbsp; I talked about in the last video.&nbsp; If Knudsen’s team was able to&nbsp; apply this new technique to GLP-1,&nbsp;&nbsp; it might be the “something more&nbsp; convincing” the CEO was looking for.&nbsp; So they started by attaching fatty acid&nbsp; chains to different positions on the&nbsp;&nbsp; GLP-1 peptide with a goal of finding a&nbsp; variation of GLP-1 with a half life of&nbsp;&nbsp; about 10 hours. That would make the resulting&nbsp; medicine&nbsp; appropriate for a once-a-day dose.&nbsp; And after comparing multiple GLP-1 analogs&nbsp; in pigs, they found several compounds that&nbsp;&nbsp; hit the 10 hour target. A massive leap&nbsp; from GLP-1’s original 2-minute half life. In 2000, they published their results&nbsp; in the Journal of Medicinal Chemistry.&nbsp; One of their most promising analogs had a&nbsp; fatty acid tail 16 carbons long attached&nbsp;&nbsp; to a lysine residue towards the middle of&nbsp; the peptide, and an arginine substituted&nbsp;&nbsp; for the usual lysine towards the end. They&nbsp; called this molecule liraglutide. It was&nbsp;&nbsp; structurally similar to native GLP-1 and had&nbsp; high potency, so it went on to clinical trials. During preclinical testing, rodents given&nbsp; liraglutide showed an increased risk of&nbsp;&nbsp; developing thyroid tumors. But fortunately that&nbsp; wasn’t the case in monkeys nor later human trials. So by the time liraglutide made&nbsp; it to phase three clinical trials,&nbsp;&nbsp; it looked both safe and effective, other than&nbsp; some participants reporting nausea. Luckily,&nbsp;&nbsp; Knudsen’s team found they could minimize&nbsp; the nausea if they started with a small&nbsp;&nbsp; dosage of liraglutide, then ramped it up over&nbsp; time. Which is a strategy still used today. Novo Nordisk published phase 3 trials in&nbsp; December 2007. They showed that liraglutide&nbsp;&nbsp; helped regulate blood sugar, reduced blood&nbsp; pressure, and led to modest weight loss.&nbsp;&nbsp; It also hit the goal of an 8 to 10 hour half life,&nbsp; which made it appropriate for a daily injection. They applied for approval in Europe and in the&nbsp;&nbsp; US at the same time — May of 2008.&nbsp; It got approved in Europe in 2009,&nbsp;&nbsp; and in the US in January of 2010. Novo&nbsp; Nordisk sold it under the brand name Victoza.&nbsp; And it was clear right away that Victoza was going&nbsp; to hit blockbuster status, or a billion dollars&nbsp;&nbsp; in annual sales, faster than expected. Right,&nbsp; patients who had been taking Lilly’s twice-daily&nbsp;&nbsp; exenatide, now had a more convenient alternative&nbsp; with once daily liraglutide. So in the US at&nbsp;&nbsp; least, Victoza started eating into Lilly’s Byetta&nbsp; sales in the first few weeks of its release.&nbsp; Now, at the time Lilly was developing a&nbsp; product called Bydureon, a once a week,&nbsp;&nbsp; extended release version&nbsp; of exenatide. But in 2010,&nbsp;&nbsp; the FDA asked them for more clinical&nbsp; data, which delayed its approval. So, and stay with me here — Lilly sold the global&nbsp; rights for both Bydureon and Byetta to Amylin,&nbsp;&nbsp; that small biotech company from earlier. Amylin&nbsp; was then acquired by Bristol Myers Squibb,&nbsp;&nbsp; whose diabetes division was bought by AstraZeneca.&nbsp; So by 2013, Astra-Zeneca had inherited exenatide.&nbsp; Meanwhile, you’ve got all these other&nbsp; GLP-1 analogs coming out at the same time.&nbsp; Sanofi’s lixisenatide, another exenatide&nbsp; based analog got FDA approval in 2016.&nbsp;&nbsp; But they later withdrew it because&nbsp; it wasn’t selling well. Likewise,&nbsp;&nbsp; GlaxoSmithKline’s once a week albiglutide,&nbsp; basically 2 GLP-1s attached to albumin,&nbsp;&nbsp; got FDA approval in 2014 but was&nbsp; withdrawn in 2017 also due to poor sales. Either way, by the twenty tens, there was&nbsp; an established market for GLP-1 analogs as a&nbsp;&nbsp; treatment for type 2 diabetes and scientists&nbsp; had solved the short half life problem. The&nbsp;&nbsp; Americans were inspired nature, while the Danes&nbsp; introduced a brand new molecular technology.&nbsp; But during those early preclinical&nbsp; studies, Knudsen’s team noticed that&nbsp;&nbsp; animals on liraglutide were eating less than&nbsp; their counterparts. And earlier studies had&nbsp;&nbsp; even shown rats starving themselves to death&nbsp; due to suppressed appetites. So as early as&nbsp;&nbsp; the mid 1990s, Lotte wondered if GLP-1&nbsp; analogs could be weight loss drugs too.&nbsp; GLP-1’s viral moment was coming,&nbsp; but it wasn’t always a given.&nbsp; In April of 2005, the CEO of Novo&nbsp; Nordisk is quoted as saying “Obesity&nbsp;&nbsp; is primarily a social and cultural problem.&nbsp; It should be solved by means of a radical&nbsp;&nbsp; restructuring of our society… There is no&nbsp; business for Novo Nordisk in that area”. Now, I agree with Sorensen that social and&nbsp; environmental factors contribute to obesity,&nbsp;&nbsp; but the “business in that&nbsp; area” deserves a closer look.&nbsp; Because weight loss medications have their own&nbsp; backstory. it probably deserves its own video. The first drug approved by the FDA specifically&nbsp; to treat obesity was cleared in 1947. This was&nbsp;&nbsp; before drugs had to show safety and&nbsp; efficacy data prior to approval. That&nbsp;&nbsp; drug was desoxyephedrine…. which is&nbsp; better known today as methamphetamine. That led to more amphetamine-based weight&nbsp; loss drugs in the 1950s and 60’s. But after&nbsp;&nbsp; the FDA started requiring drug&nbsp; efficacy studies in the 1960s,&nbsp;&nbsp; many of these kinds of drugs struggled to&nbsp; show that they actually caused weight loss.&nbsp; The FDA allowed some of&nbsp; them to stay on the market,&nbsp;&nbsp; but only for short term use and they needed&nbsp; to come with a black box warning for safety.&nbsp; As a result, amphetamines got less popular by&nbsp; the late 20th century. But then all of a sudden,&nbsp;&nbsp; in the 1990s, a combination of two&nbsp; older drugs exploded in popularity:&nbsp;&nbsp; Fenfluramine and phentermine, or&nbsp; what became known as FenPhen.&nbsp; These drugs caused more eight loss together than&nbsp; individually. But then came Redux, a new variation&nbsp;&nbsp; of fenfluramine. And it got super popular,&nbsp; peaking 85,000 prescriptions a week at one point. Media covered it favorably at first. But&nbsp; things changed after a paper came out in&nbsp;&nbsp; 1997 pointing to a possible link between&nbsp; FenPhen use and heart valve issues. And&nbsp;&nbsp; further studies confirmed the link — people&nbsp; who took FenPhen for extended amounts of time,&nbsp;&nbsp; especially more than 24 months, had a&nbsp; higher risk of heart valve problems.&nbsp; Knowing this, the FDA recommended taking&nbsp; the drugs off the market in September of&nbsp;&nbsp; 1997. The company that manufactured&nbsp; FenPhen dealt with some high profile,&nbsp;&nbsp; very expensive lawsuits at the end of&nbsp; the 90s as a result. This was around&nbsp;&nbsp; the time Novo Nordisk was beginning&nbsp; clinical trials for liraglutide. So fast forward to 2005, and I can see why&nbsp; the Novo Nordisk CEO wasn’t enthusiastic&nbsp;&nbsp; about pursuing obesity treatments. Historically,&nbsp; weight loss drugs were more risk than reward. At the same time, if you could get a drug approved&nbsp; for obesity, the potential market was massive. By&nbsp;&nbsp; 2011, over half a billion people worldwide were&nbsp; living with obesity. And that number was growing. Knudsen’s group had been looking into&nbsp; liraglutide’s effects on weight loss&nbsp;&nbsp; before it was approved for type 2 diabetes.&nbsp; They published their first paper on&nbsp; in 2001. And over the next few years, they kept&nbsp; publishing research comparing liraglutide effect&nbsp;&nbsp; on weight loss to placebos, as well as&nbsp; against other GLP-1 receptor agonists. Working in Knudsen’s favor was that, in the&nbsp; early 2000s, Novo Nordisk didn’t have much&nbsp;&nbsp; else in development, so the company changed&nbsp; course and pursued liraglutide for obesity.&nbsp; But where Victoza was&nbsp; 1.2-2.8&nbsp; milligrams of liraglutide,&nbsp;&nbsp; the weight loss formulation was 3 milligrams&nbsp; and went by the not-so-creative name of lira3mg. Phase two trials started in 2007, followed&nbsp; by Phase three in 2011. At which point,&nbsp;&nbsp; the interest in liraglutide starts picking up.&nbsp; A set of studies published that year in the&nbsp; Journal of Obesity showed that liraglutide&nbsp;&nbsp; was not only safe, but it led to an average of 6%&nbsp; weight loss in certain patients.&nbsp; By this point,&nbsp;&nbsp; the CEO was starting to come around&nbsp; to the potential of obesity drugs.&nbsp; And the timing couldn’t have been&nbsp; better. In the summer of 2013,&nbsp;&nbsp; the American Medical Association released&nbsp; a position statement that officially&nbsp;&nbsp; recognized obesity as a diseasethat&nbsp; required multiple interventions.&nbsp; The stage was set for weight&nbsp; loss drugs to make a comeback. Novo Nordisk submitted liraglutide for approval&nbsp; as a weight loss drug in December 2013 and&nbsp;&nbsp; got FDA approval a year later. They sold it&nbsp; under the brand name Saxenda in the States.&nbsp; Real quick clarification, liraglutide is the&nbsp; active ingredient in both Saxenda and Victoza.&nbsp;&nbsp; The difference comes down to what the drug&nbsp; is treating and the dosage prescribed.&nbsp; Victoza is prescribed for type 2 diabetes,&nbsp; typically at 1.2 to 1.8 milligrams per day,&nbsp;&nbsp; while Saxenda is approved for obesity and ramps&nbsp; up to a higher dose of 3 milligrams a day. Now, it took some time to figure out how it&nbsp; worked though. As Knudsen has pointed out,&nbsp;&nbsp; their new drug application for liraglutide as&nbsp;&nbsp; a diabetes treatment says that they&nbsp; didn’t see GLP-1 uptake in the brain. See, while GLP-1 is primarily a hormone, it&nbsp; can also activate certain nerves in the gut&nbsp;&nbsp; and bind to receptors in different parts of the&nbsp; brain stem that lead to a feeling of fullness. The body responds by slowing down gastric&nbsp; empyting, meaning food stays in your stomach&nbsp;&nbsp; for longer, which contributes to that fullness.&nbsp; So whether it’s the natural GLP-1 hormone or a&nbsp;&nbsp; GLP-1 analog like liraglutide, the response is&nbsp; the same: reduced appetite and less food intake. But this mechanism might also explain some&nbsp; of the gastrointestinal side effects that&nbsp;&nbsp; people experience while on GLP-1&nbsp; receptor agonists. High levels of&nbsp;&nbsp; GLP-1 signaling to the brainstem can&nbsp; trigger the brain's vomiting center.&nbsp;&nbsp; Fortunately, patients typically&nbsp; build a tolerance to the drug,&nbsp;&nbsp; which tamps down the GI side effects over time.&nbsp; That’s why dosages start small and ramp up. After liraglutide’s approval for weight loss, Eli&nbsp; Lilly’s introduced dulaglutide, a GLP-1 analog for&nbsp;&nbsp; diabetes treatment. And unlike liraglutide which&nbsp; is a single GLP-1 molecule hooked up to a fatty&nbsp;&nbsp; acid, dulaglutide consists of two modified GLP-1&nbsp; analogs hooked up to fragment of human antibody.&nbsp; This design lets it resist breakdown by the DPP-4&nbsp; enzyme, which allowed for once a week doses. Lilly sold it under the brand name Trulicity.&nbsp; But Novo Nordisk wanted a once a week injection&nbsp; too. And to do this, they decided to build on&nbsp;&nbsp; their albumin binding strategy from liraglutide’s&nbsp; development, but they needed to change something.&nbsp; Because they’d learned that while longer fatty&nbsp; acids increased the peptide’s ability to bind&nbsp;&nbsp; to albumin, which increases its half life,&nbsp; that came at the cost of affinity to the&nbsp;&nbsp; receptor, or how well the drug binds to its&nbsp; receptors. And that led to worse potency.&nbsp; The challenge then, was to find a molecule that&nbsp;&nbsp; would bind firmly to albumin,&nbsp; but also to GLP-1 receptors. Their best candidate was semaglutide. It had a&nbsp; GLP-1 (7-37) sequence, modified in two different&nbsp;&nbsp; amino acid positions, attached to an 18 carbon&nbsp; fatty aid chain, via a very important linker.&nbsp; But even before it hit the market,&nbsp; semglutide got attention. In a Novo&nbsp;&nbsp; Nordisk sponsored trial published in&nbsp; the New England Journal of Medicine,&nbsp;&nbsp; patients with type 2 diabetes treated&nbsp; with semaglutide had fewer heart attacks,&nbsp;&nbsp; fewer strokes, and died from cardiovascular&nbsp; events less frequently than the placebo group.&nbsp; This paper has been cited over 6,000&nbsp; times, which means it’s super influential.. Now, this was 2016, 2017,&nbsp; which was around the time&nbsp;&nbsp; Novo Nordisk’s patent on liraglutide&nbsp; was about to expire. This meant that&nbsp;&nbsp; other drug makers would be able to start&nbsp; making their own versions of the drug.&nbsp; And in 2017, a giant Israel-based&nbsp; generics&nbsp; producer, Teva Pharmaceuticals, applied for&nbsp;&nbsp; FDA approval for a generic liraglutide. Novo&nbsp; Nordisk saw this coming, but they wanted a&nbsp;&nbsp; little more time to transition their customers&nbsp; to their /new/ drug. So they reached a settlement&nbsp;&nbsp; with Teva where Novo Nordisk would extend their&nbsp; market exclusivity on liraglutide through 2023.&nbsp; That would give Nordisk time to ramp&nbsp; up marketing for their newly approved&nbsp;&nbsp; GLP-1 drug semaglutide, what they&nbsp; sold under the brand name Ozempic. And market it they did. In 2018, they launched&nbsp; a massive campaign for Ozempic. This is when&nbsp;&nbsp; I remember hearing that commercial&nbsp; with the “ohh ohh ohh Ozempic” jingle. Now at this point, Ozempic was still only&nbsp; approved for type 2 diabetes. But during its&nbsp;&nbsp; clinical trials, researchers noticed significant&nbsp; weight loss in diabetic patients, like with a 2&nbsp;&nbsp; milligram dose a week, 40% of participants&nbsp; lost at least 10% of their body weight. So while diabetes trials were ongoing, Novo&nbsp; Nordisk started testing higher doses of&nbsp;&nbsp; semaglutide for obesity. Phase 3 clinical trials&nbsp; were expected to finish by the end of 2019.&nbsp; But even before official approval, doctors were&nbsp; prescribing Ozempic for weight loss anyway. See,&nbsp;&nbsp; in the US, doctors are allowed&nbsp; prescribe a drug off-label, meaning,&nbsp;&nbsp; for a condition it doesn’t have FDA approval for.&nbsp; This is when hype for GLP-1s really&nbsp; starts building in the general public. Because among other things, the clinical trials of&nbsp; semaglutide for weight loss were looking extremely&nbsp;&nbsp; promising. Participants lost about 12-17% of&nbsp; their body weight over a year of treatment,&nbsp;&nbsp; compared to about 8 percent with liraglutide. Yes,&nbsp; there was the issue of weight loss plateauing,&nbsp;&nbsp; or gaining the weight back. But as long&nbsp; as the trial participants were able to&nbsp;&nbsp; stay on semaglutide, they tended&nbsp; to see the intended weight loss.&nbsp; There was also still some nausea and GI symptoms,&nbsp; which sometimes caused people to stop treatment.&nbsp; In June 2021, the FDA officially&nbsp; approved semaglutide for weight&nbsp;&nbsp; loss. Novo Nordisk put it&nbsp; on the market as Wegovy.&nbsp; And since they were already producing&nbsp; semaglutide at a large scale,&nbsp;&nbsp; they had it for sale in American&nbsp; pharmacies within the month. Now remember, by 2021, doctors had already been&nbsp; prescribing Ozempic for weight loss for years,&nbsp;&nbsp; and at the same time, some major&nbsp; American insurance companies&nbsp;&nbsp; formally decided they’d reimburse for&nbsp; Wegovy, which made it more accessible.&nbsp; The result was that Wegovy got more prescriptions&nbsp;&nbsp; in its first five weeks of sales than&nbsp; Saxenda did in its first four years. And while Ozempic and Wegovy&nbsp; were exploding in popularity,&nbsp;&nbsp; Eli Lilly was working on something new. They&nbsp; already had a weekly injection with Trulicity,&nbsp;&nbsp; but they wanted to experiment with&nbsp; something a little different.&nbsp; The result was called tirzepatide — a dual&nbsp; agonist&nbsp; that stimulates two different&nbsp;&nbsp; receptors. GLP-1 receptoprsand receptors for&nbsp; that incretin hormone I mentioned earlier:&nbsp;&nbsp; GIP, although by 2022 it had been renamed&nbsp; glucose-dependent insulinotropic polypeptide.&nbsp; In the spring of 2022, Eli Lilly&nbsp; released&nbsp; tirzepatide under the names Mounjaro for&nbsp;&nbsp; diabetes and Zepbound for obesity.&nbsp; Some of the trials with tirzepatide&nbsp;&nbsp; showed over 20% body weight loss&nbsp; in participants with obesity.&nbsp; So! We’ve got this pent up demand from&nbsp; years of off label use as well as FDA&nbsp;&nbsp; approval for obesity, a condition&nbsp; that millions of people have. It&nbsp;&nbsp; was already prime conditions for a&nbsp; shortage. And then TikTok happened.&nbsp; From 2021 to 2023, information about GLP-1&nbsp; analogs hit the general public hard. Celebrities,&nbsp;&nbsp; influencers, and everyday people started&nbsp; sharing their experiences online.&nbsp; This became a big enough phenomenon that&nbsp; academics wrote journal articles about it. According to a non-academic article on&nbsp; trackdrugs.com, the Ozempic hashtag on&nbsp;&nbsp; TikTok grew from around 2 million views&nbsp; in 2021 to well over a billion in 2023.&nbsp; But just as demand was exploding,&nbsp; the supply chain hit a snag.&nbsp; In October of 2021, the FDA visited&nbsp; one of the facilities that Novo Nordisk&nbsp;&nbsp; used to make Wegovy. According to&nbsp; the 2023 book about Novo Nordisk,&nbsp;&nbsp; the visit resulted in “very harshly&nbsp; worded criticism” and a shut down of&nbsp;&nbsp; the production plant. That plant probably&nbsp; wouldn't start up again until late 2022. So in 2022, between the social media hype and&nbsp; factory shut down, Demand for semaglutide outpaced&nbsp;&nbsp; supply. As a result, the FDA declared a shortage&nbsp; of Wegovy in March and Ozempic in August.&nbsp; Lilly’s tirzepatide followed onto&nbsp; the shortage list in December,&nbsp;&nbsp; and Novo’s older liraglutide&nbsp; followed in July 2023. Now, the FDA defines a shortage as “a period&nbsp; of time when the demand or projected demand&nbsp;&nbsp; for the drug within the United States&nbsp; exceeds the supply of the drug.”.&nbsp; And while I couldn’t find the&nbsp; exact quantity of the shortage,&nbsp;&nbsp; the important thing to know is that&nbsp; new rules apply in this situation.&nbsp; Because as long as a drug is in shortage,&nbsp; FDA rules allow special pharmacies called&nbsp;&nbsp; compounding pharmacies to manufacture&nbsp; the drug. These are facilities with the&nbsp;&nbsp; ability to make bespoke medicines,&nbsp; usually for individual patients.&nbsp; For instance, if you have an allergy to one&nbsp; of the ingredients or need a modified dosage,&nbsp;&nbsp; compounding pharmacies can make that happen&nbsp; in a way that a large scale factory can’t. Importantly though, the compounded&nbsp; version does not need FDA approval.&nbsp;&nbsp; Novo Nordisk has highlighted this&nbsp; fact in advertisements, like this one,&nbsp;&nbsp; which I’m pretty sure is meant to mimic the&nbsp; style of online semaglutide seller, Hims. Speaking of the name brands, semaglutide&nbsp; and tirzepetide were both under patent&nbsp;&nbsp; at this point, so what are the&nbsp; compounders actually selling? The short answer is that compounding pharmacies&nbsp; are allowed to make patented drugs when the FDA&nbsp;&nbsp; declares a shortage. But the compounders&nbsp; couldn’t actually buy pure semaglutide or&nbsp;&nbsp; tirzepetide from the original manufacturers, so&nbsp; they had to resort to less-regulated sources. Some compounders made salts of semaglutide,&nbsp;&nbsp; like semaglutide sodium or semaglutide&nbsp; acetate. Some used research grade semaglutide,&nbsp;&nbsp; which isn’t held to the same purity standards&nbsp; as its pharmaceutical grade version.&nbsp; It even got to the point where the&nbsp; FDA issued a warning saying “Hey,&nbsp;&nbsp; just reminding y’all that these are different&nbsp; from the active ingredient we approved”. As you can imagine, both Novo Nordisk&nbsp; and Lilly wanted to fix the shortage,&nbsp;&nbsp; and take back their market share. So&nbsp; both companies spent 2023 and 2024&nbsp;&nbsp; building factories dedicated to GLP-1 production. Tirzepatide came off the&nbsp; shortage list on October 2,&nbsp;&nbsp; 2024. On February 21, 2025, semaglutide followed.&nbsp; In both cases, the FDA gave compounders a&nbsp;&nbsp; few months’ grace period to wing&nbsp; down production without penalty. Liraglutide was a little different though.&nbsp;&nbsp; Novo’s patent extension settlement with&nbsp; Teva Pharmaceuticals finally came to an end,&nbsp;&nbsp; and allowed Teva to manufacture an “authorized&nbsp; generic” version of Victoza in June of 2024.&nbsp; By the end of the year, a British drug maker named&nbsp;&nbsp; Hikma Pharmaceuticals followed&nbsp; with their own generic version. But even during the shortage, drug&nbsp; companies were still working on new GLP-1s.&nbsp; Like Eli Lilly is developing retatrutide a triple&nbsp; agonist that binds to receptors for GLP-1, GIP,&nbsp;&nbsp; and good old glucagon. As of the time of&nbsp; filming, it’s in phase 3 clinical trials. Lilly also acquired an /oral/ GLP-1 receptor&nbsp; agonist they’re calling orforglipron. It’s&nbsp;&nbsp; a small molecule, not a peptide, so unlike&nbsp; Novo Nordisk’s oral semaglutide, Rybelsus,&nbsp;&nbsp; it makes it through digestion fairly intact.&nbsp; Early clinical trial data in type&nbsp; 2 diabetics showed weight loss and&nbsp;&nbsp; blood sugar reductions on par with semaglutide.&nbsp; An effective GLP-1 pill would also be useful&nbsp;&nbsp; because it’s easier to get people&nbsp; to take pills than injections.&nbsp; Now, a ton of the hype around GLP-1s is based&nbsp; on what people expect them to do in the future.&nbsp;&nbsp; They’ll end obesity and cure Alzheimers and&nbsp; become the miracle drug of the 21st century. And&nbsp;&nbsp; maybe those will all happen, maybe they won’t,&nbsp; but we can still take away some lessons now. Takeaway number one, the early GLP-1 research in&nbsp; the 80s and early 90s was truly creative science.&nbsp; Like once we knew that GLP-1 and GLP-1 receptors&nbsp; existed, scientists used different routes to find&nbsp;&nbsp; agonists for that receptor. John Eng’s team&nbsp; used gila monster venom to make exenatide,&nbsp;&nbsp; while Lotte Knudsen’s team used that&nbsp; special albumin binding strategy.&nbsp; And both of them benefitted&nbsp; from the recent accessibility&nbsp;&nbsp; of recombinant DNA to make hormones.&nbsp; Takeaway number two, timing was everything&nbsp; in this story. This is pure speculation now,&nbsp;&nbsp; but had Novo Nordisk tried to market&nbsp; liraglutide for weight loss so close&nbsp;&nbsp; to the fallout from FenPhen, I doubt that&nbsp; patients would’ve swarmed to it like they have.&nbsp; Even though they work totally differently,&nbsp;&nbsp; FenPhen and Redux spooked a lot of folks&nbsp; away from the idea of weight loss drugs.&nbsp; So instead, by waiting and studying the product&nbsp; for a few years, the memory of FenPhen faded,&nbsp;&nbsp; and Nobo had a global customer base who was&nbsp; ready to give weight loss drugs another chance.&nbsp; But the timing of TikTok might’ve&nbsp; been even more impactful. Right,&nbsp;&nbsp; Americans understand the typical pharma&nbsp; advertisement at this point. It’s enough&nbsp;&nbsp; of a trope that SNL has a parody drug&nbsp; commercial every other week it seems.&nbsp; But when you hear people talk about Ozempic on&nbsp; TikTok, it doesn’t feel like an advertisement,&nbsp;&nbsp; it feels personal. It feels like that person&nbsp; is sharing their experience with a product;&nbsp;&nbsp; like it’s a restaurant or a book they’re&nbsp; recommending. TikTok feels like word of mouth,&nbsp;&nbsp; but has the reach of the entire internet.&nbsp; Plus, semaglutide is unique within&nbsp; the world of pharmaceuticals. It’s&nbsp;&nbsp; a drug that can be sold like a regular old&nbsp; consumer product since obesity is so common.&nbsp; Althouh, it would be wild to think&nbsp; that the TikTok phenomenon could&nbsp;&nbsp; apply to an antibiotic or a bronchodilator&nbsp; or something with a more niche audience.&nbsp; Takeaway number three, the financial&nbsp; success of GLP-1s changed the pharma&nbsp;&nbsp; industry’s appetite for weight loss drugs.&nbsp; These days, whether it’s a GLP-1 analog,&nbsp;&nbsp; a triple agonist, or something&nbsp; entirely different. just about&nbsp;&nbsp; every major pharma company is working&nbsp; on some kind of weight loss drug.&nbsp; So regardless of what happens&nbsp; with GLP-1s in the future,&nbsp;&nbsp; they’ll go down as the drugs that&nbsp; put weight loss back on the table.&nbsp; This video has been one of my most requested&nbsp; videos for literally years, and I’m thrilled to&nbsp;&nbsp; finally share it with you. But before you go, I’ve&nbsp; got some exciting news. From now on, you can find&nbsp;&nbsp; my videos early and ad-free on Nebula, the indie&nbsp; streaming platform made by creators for creators. You probably sat through, what, 4 or 5 ads to&nbsp; get here? Well on Nebula, there are no ads. You&nbsp;&nbsp; can watch videos uninterrupted for quite&nbsp; literally hours. My catalog is on there,&nbsp;&nbsp; plus bonus videos you won’t find on YouTube. Like&nbsp; one about a Nobel Prize winning scientist who&nbsp;&nbsp; thought Vitamin C could cure cancer, and another&nbsp; about how nobody knows where Neosporin comes from. But Nebula is home to hundreds of other creators&nbsp;&nbsp; you probably already love. Medical creators&nbsp; like Medlife Crisis and Chubby Emu,&nbsp;&nbsp; as well as folks you’ve seen on this channel&nbsp; before like Knowing Better and Mia Mulder. cases but many people are now saying that all of this has changed we finally have drugs that can do this effectively and they're called glp1 receptor agonists like ozampic glp1 stands for glucagon-like peptide and this is basically a hormone released by the gut in response to food consumption and what it does is when you eat and that glucose level goes up in your blood it tells your pancreas to make more insulin which then lowers that sugar level it also inhibits the release of another hormone called glucagon which normally acts to release sugar into the bloodstream so by increasing insulin and decreasing glucagon glp-1 is very effective at reducing blood sugar levels and that's why it was initially developed as a drug for people with diabetes and multiple Studies have shown that these drugs very effectively lower sugar levels and they reduce cardiovascular events in people with diabetes but these trials also showed a very interesting side effect and that is that people taking the drug at lost weight and the reason for this is that the drug also does something else because your intestines use it as a feedback mechanism to regulate how much food you're eating glp-1 actually slows down what's called the motility of the stomach and the intestines which basically means that it slows down digestion so food stays in your stomach for longer which keeps you feeling full for longer and that means you eat less and that's how you lose weight and drug companies are very opportunistic so they took advantage of that side effect and then they designed trials to see if these drugs can actually help anyone including people who don't have diabetes to lose weight and the first major trial was published in the New England Journal of Medicine in March of last year and I'd say it was a game changer they took almost 2 000 adults who were either obese meaning they had a body mass index or BMI of 30 or higher or they had a BMI of 27 or higher and at least one complication related to their weight such as high blood pressure high cholesterol sleep apnea or cardiovascular disease and what's important is that none of these people had diabetes two-thirds of them got the drug in this case a glp-1 receptor Agonist called semi-glutite orozepic and one-third got Placebo and they all got weight loss counseling along with a diet plan and an exercise plan and over 68 weeks this is what happened so the top line is how much people in the placebo group drop their weight in this case 2.4 percent of their body weight and the bottom line is how much those who took the drug lost in this case it was 14.9 percent of their body weight by week 68. and that difference was 12.4 percent of body weight or on average 28 more pounds lost in people who took ozampic along with that there was also lower blood pressure better physical functioning better mental functioning and lower blood sugar in fact over 80 percent of people who had pre-diabetes went back to being normal when they took the ozempic and they've repeated this trial in different groups of patients and different parts of the world and the results are pretty consistent over about 16 months people lose about 10 percent more of their body weight with the drug compared to a placebo and the weight loss actually continues on this drug for over a year after you start it in one study it didn't actually Plateau until about week 60 and in another study the weight stayed off even two years into taking the drug there just haven't been any drugs in the past that have come even close to this level of impact and ozempic isn't the only option although it's the best study drug there are recently published Trials of other drugs in the same class and we will probably see many other drugs like this approved in the next few years but there are drawbacks first of all most of these agents have to be administered subcutaneously which means that it's a tiny needle just under the skin so if you don't like needles this isn't for you also what happens when you stop the drug we know from a recent trial that people who stopped the drug did gain back some of the weight they had lost over the next few months so it's not a sustained benefit it helps you while you're on it but it does seem to wear off when you stop it and the other question is side effects so the good news is that we've been using these drugs and diabetes for over 15 years so we have a pretty good understanding of their safety and side effect profile the most common side effects are things like nausea diarrhea vomiting and constipation the other thing to keep in mind is that weight reduction is great as long as it's mostly fat that you're losing as opposed to muscle and bone so we still need to understand the If we're all swimming in the exact same sea of dopamine, ultrarocessed foods, and hyperpalatable engineered snacks, why is one person's brain hijacked, leading to severe treatment resistant obesity, while another person seems completely immune? Why does one nervous system treat a donut as a simple passing treat while another nervous system treats it as a powerful necessary sedative? Why does one body naturally burn off excess energy while another body holds on to it? Today we're going to discuss the single most overlooked, misunderstood, and stigmatized factor in the global obesity epidemic. We're talking about adverse childhood experiences and the permanent biological rewiring of your metabolism. I'm Dr. Amin Hayad, triple board certified physician, assistant clinical professor, and pathologist. I appreciate you subscribing to my channel if you haven't already and turn on the notifications so you don't miss the videos as they get released. Before we go any further, here's a note of care. If you have experienced early life trauma, whether that's abuse, neglect, or extreme household dysfunction, some of the concepts we discussed today might be difficult to hear. It may bring up the past, but please understand the intention. This is not about blame, not about labeling you as broken. My goal today is to show you the rigorous, peer-reviewed science of your own resilience. I want to show you that your body did exactly what it needed to do to survive an unsafe environment. Your weight is not a character flaw and it's in fact a brilliant biological adaptation. In our last episode, we mapped out the neuroscience of hijack. We discussed how the modern food environment hacks your dopamine pathways, overriding your prefrontal cortex. We talked about the difference between wanting and liking, and how sugar and fat can physically downregulate your reward system, leaving you in a chronic state of dopamine deficit. But that explanation, as neurologically accurate as it is, leaves a big question unanswered. Today we're going to look at the hardware and the software of your stress response. We'll explore how early life stress can permanently change the set point of your cortisol, alters physical architecture of your fat cells, and change the way your DNA is read through a process called epigenetics. We're going to explain why for a traumatized nervous system, obesity is not a metabolic dysfunction. And in fact, it's a necessary, calculated, and mathematically precise adaptation for survival. To understand this, we'll travel back in time to 1985, and we'll go to a clinic in San Diego, California, that accidentally discovered the root cause of the modern obesity crisis. Dr. Vincent Feliti was a highly respected preventive medicine physician running a successful weight loss clinic at Kaiser Permanente. His program was the gold standard of the 1980s. Strict liquid diets, intensive medical monitoring, and rapid dramatic weight loss. And clinically, looking at the charts, it was working great. Patients were losing hundreds of pounds. Their blood pressure was dropping. Their type 2 diabetes was reversing. But then Dr. Feliti noticed a deeply disturbing patterns in his data. The patients who were succeeding the most, the ones rapidly shedding the weight and hitting their target BMIs, were suddenly dropping out of the program. Surprisingly, they were quitting because they were succeeding. Dr. Feliti was baffled because it made no medical sense. He tracked down one of his patients, a woman who had lost a massive amount of weight, looked fantastic on paper, and then suddenly regained it all back and abandoned the clinic. He sat her down and asked, "What happened? Your blood work had improved and you were doing so well." She told him a story about her childhood. It was a story of abuse. A story where being small, unnoticed, and attractive was deeply physically dangerous. And being big, heavy, and imposing felt safe. Her weight was acting as a physical shield. When the shield disappeared, the terror returned. I want to pause here. Does this resonate with anyone watching? Have you or anyone you know ever successfully lost weight, started receiving compliments, and suddenly felt a wave of unexplained anxiety that led you straight back to your starting weight. Drop a yes in the comments if you've experienced this so-called self-sabotage. I want you to look at the comments and see that you're not alone. This single conversation sparked a hypothesis that led to one of the largest and consequential public health studies in history. The adverse childhood experiences study. The Centers for Disease Control and Kaiser Permanente partnered to survey over 17,000 middle-class Americans. They asked them 10 specific uncomfortable questions about their childhood environments before the age of 18. Did you experience physical abuse, emotional neglect? Was there a parent with active addiction at home? Was there a violent divorce? Was there incarceration? Was there sexual trauma? They gave everyone a score from 0 to 10. The results they found were medically surprising. They found a dose dependent relationship between the number of trauma categories a person experienced and their risk of chronic disease in adulthood. Dose dependent means the higher the dose of trauma, the higher the rate of disease. According to the data, if you had an A score of four or higher, your risk of severe obesity didn't just go up a few percentage points. It in fact doubled. Your risk of developing type 2 diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and severe autoimmune disorders also went up. But here's a critical pivot of our story. When the study was published, the medical community and the general public looked at this data and drew an overly simplistic and biased conclusion. They said, "Oh, well, these people are stressed out, so they eat comfort food to cope with their feelings. It's a behavioral issue. They just need better coping mechanisms." But as a physician who studied the pathology of these diseases, I can tell you that this view is scientifically incomplete and dare I say, medically negligent. What we now know thanks to 30 years of advancements in neuroendocrinology is that severe early life trauma can fundamentally disrupt your HBA access, which is beyond just making you sad or stressed. When a developing human nervous system is exposed to chronic unpredictability, whether that's physical violence, emotional neglect, or the chaos of the disregulated household, it learns one specific overriding lesson. The environment is hostile and the world is unsafe. To survive an unsafe world, the body must constantly be ready for war. It initiates a physiological threat response. It recruits a neuroendocrine superhighway known as the HPA axis, the hypothalamus, pituitary, and adrenal glands. Think of the hypothalamus located deep into the brain as the radar system. When it detects a threat, it sends a chemical signal to the pituitary gland, the commander. The pituitary then sends an urgent message down to the adrenal glands, which sits right on top of your kidneys. The adrenals then secrete a hormone called cortisol. Cortisol is the master stress hormone. It's designed to save your life in the short term. When that cortisol floods your bloodstream, it does three very specific things to help you survive a famine or a physical fight. First, it immediately raises your blood sugar. It signals your liver to dump glucose into your veins so your muscles have instant fuel to run or fight. Second, it raises your blood pressure to forcefully pump that oxygenated sugar rich blood to your extremities. Third, it shuts down all non-essential long-term projects. It halts digestion. It suppresses the reproductive system. It pauses immune repair. You don't need to digest a sandwich or fight off a mild cold if you're about to be eaten by a predator. In a normal healthy life, stress is an acute spike. You see a tiger, cortisol spikes. You run, you hopefully survive, the tiger leaves and cortisol drops back to baseline. The parasympathetic nervous system kicks in and you rest and digest and the system resets. But what happens when the tiger lives in your house? What happens when the life-threatening danger is a chaotic, unpredictable parent? Or the chronic stress of deep poverty or the silent terrifying fear of abandonment? You can't fight them and you can't run away from them. The spike never drops. The radar never turns off. The developing nervous system is bathed in a toxic wash of cortisol 24 hours a day, 7 days a week for years. This chronic, unrelenting exposure creates a physiological condition called HPA access dysregulation. The internal thermostat for stress breaks. Decades later, when you're an adult, when you're living in a safe neighborhood, when the threat is entirely gone, your baseline cortisol remains pathologically high. Your radar is still spinning, looking for a threat that no longer exists. or in some cases the system becomes so exhausted that it burns out completely leaving you with a blunted cortisol curve resulting in a profound paralyzing fatigue where you can't mount the energy to simply face the day. Now why does this specific dysregulation cause obesity? This brings us to the concept of endocrine reprogramming. We have to stop thinking of cortisol as just a stress hormone in the context of metabolic psychiatry and obesity medicine. Cortisol acts as a highly potent fat storage hormone and it's incredibly specific where it builds its reserves. As a medical doctor, when I look at patients with Cushing disease, a condition where a tumor causes the body to produce massive unregulated amounts of cortisol. I see a very specific recognizable body shape. These patients have thin arms and thin legs, but a massive dense buildup of fat in the abdomen and in the back of the neck. This is because cortisol specifically targets visceral atapose tissue. Here we need to differentiate between fat types. Subcutaneous fat is the soft fat that sits right under your skin of your arms, hips or legs and it's mostly inert metabolic storage. Visceral fat cells possess a significantly higher density of gluccocorticoid receptors which chemically speaking this means visceral fat is sticky for cortisol. Why would the body do this? Why would the biological system forcefully packs fat tightly around your vital organs whenever you're stressed? Because visceral fat is biologically easier and can readily break down and release free fatty acids directly into the portal vein. The portal vein acts as a direct superighway straight to the liver where those fatty acids are instantly converted into glucose. If your traumatized nervous system genuinely believes you're living in a permanent war zone, it doesn't want your energy reserves stored far away in your thighs or arms where it takes time and effort to access. It wants the fuel stored right next to where it needs to get processed. It wants instant energy ready to dumped into the bloodstream at a millisecond's notice to fuel the fight orflight response. So that stubborn belly fat that you hate, that's a tactical bunker. And your body built a strategic, highly efficient fuel reserve to survive a war that ended 20 years ago. This physiological reality is exactly why traditional weight loss advice feels like gaslighting to a trauma survivor. Some people even in the medical community tell you to just cut calories. But to a disregulated HBA access, a calorie deficit is not a path to health. A calorie deficit is in fact a biological threat. Calorie restriction is a severe biological stressor and it naturally raises cortisol which leads to more fat storage. Let's say you listen to the advice. You go on a strict thousand calorie a day diet, which translates to you actively starving yourself. Your brain, which is already hypervigilant, already scanning the horizon for the next disaster, senses the sudden lack of incoming food. It sounds the alarms that the famine is here or the war has escalated. What does your body do in response? It fights back. It spikes your cortisol even higher. It downregulates your thyroid function to aggressively slow your resting metabolic rate. It clutches onto that visceral fat with a grip of iron, refusing to burn it because your deep survival brain believes that fat is the only thing standing between you and death by starvation. You're fighting an ancient biology that's desperately trying to save your life. This isn't fighting lack of willpower. But the mechanism of trauma goes much deeper than just circulating hormones and fat storage. It goes down to the microscopic level. It goes to the very blueprint of your cells. To fully grasp the magnitude of the wound, we need to talk about the epigenetics. For a very long time in medicine, we believed that your genes were your absolute destiny. You were born with a fixed script inherited from your parents and you simply played it out. If you had the obesity gene, you would be obese. We now know definitively that this is false. Think of your genes as the hardware of your computer. Epigenetics is like your operating system and early life trauma can write the code. Think of it as a study of how behaviors, environment, and severe psychological stress cause physical changes that affect the way your genes work. These epigenetic changes don't mutate or alter underlying DNA sequence. Instead, they change how your body reads that sequence. They turn certain genes on, and they forcefully switch other genes off. There is a highly specific heavily studied gene called FKBP5. This gene plays a critical role in your stress response and acts as the negative feedback loop. It regulates the sensitivity of the gluccocorticoid receptors, the microscopic ears on your cells that hear cortisol and tell your body to calm down after a stressor has passed. Recent studies have shown that experiencing severe early life trauma causes a process called methylation. A methyl group, a tiny chemical tag, physically attaches itself to the regulator region of the FKBP5 gene, effectively putting a padlock on a vital piece of machinery. This epigenetic attack fundamentally changes the sensitivity of your entire stress response system. It hardwires your cells at a molecular level to be hyper reactive to any future stress. Let me give you a clinical example of what this looks like in real world. This means that if you with your history of childhood trauma and the resulting epigenetic methylation and your best friend who had a perfectly safe, highly regulated childhood both experience the exact same modern stressor, the biological outcome is vastly different. Let's say you both get stuck in severe gridlock traffic on the way to a highstakes meeting or you both receive a vaguely threatening rude email from your boss. Your friend's brain registers a threat. She gets a mild feeling of annoyance. Her cortisol goes up slightly to make her feel alert. She takes a deep breath and within 10 minutes her cortisol drops back to baseline. The event is practically over for her. You on the other hand experience a total physiological cascade. Your unmethylated disregulated system can't apply the brakes. You get a massive disproportionate cortisol dump. Your heart raises. Your blood pressure elevates. Your liver dumps glucose into the blood causing a massive blood sugar spike. And your body receives a deafening chemical signal to store visceral fat. Same traffic, same email, vastly different biological response. Why is that? Because of the epigenetic software installed in your DNA decades ago when you were, let's say, just 5 years old. This epigenetic scarring creates what we call a pro-inflammatory phenotype. Your body is locked in a state primed for inflammation. And as we'll learn, inflammation is the ultimate enemy of a healthy metabolism. This profound dysregulation leads us directly to the self-medication hypothesis. In episode one, we talked about the dopamine hijack. We established how highly engineered food acts like a drug on the brain. But now with the context of trauma, we can connect the final dots. We can answer why the hijack happens so fiercely in some people. Why do we relentlessly crave highfat, high sugar, ultrarocessed foods when we're stressed, anxious, or triggered? Is this just because they taste good, or is it merely a pursuit of empty pleasure? Dr. Mary Dolman, a pioneering researcher at the University of California in San Francisco, discovered something profound in her extensive research on the stress response network. She found that when a mammal ingest what we call comfort food, specifically the combination of high sugar and high fat, it effectively puts the biological brakes on your system. It actively chemically dampens the stress response. When you eat the donut or the pizza or the sleeve of cookies, it causes a surge of insulin and alters gluccocorticoid signaling in the brain. Your cortisol lowers. You feel a transient genuine wash of physiological calm. The loud anxious noise in your head temporarily stops. The tight panicky feeling in your chest loosens. For a person living with severe HPA dysregulation and an overactive fear center, food is more than just nutrition or calories. It's the most readily available, socially acceptable pharmacological tool to downregulate a screaming emotional center of the brain called amydala. You're essentially eating to forcefully lower your cortisol. You're utilizing food as an anzolytic, but like any powerful drug, you build a rapid tolerance. Because your baseline cortisol is chronically high, your brain requires more and more sugar and more and more fat to achieve the same dampening effect. Think about your own intense cravings for a moment. When you reach for those ultrarocessed foods, are you truly physically hungry or are you trying to turn down the volume of your stress? If you feel comfortable sharing, let me know in the comments. Do you tend to use food as fuel or as a sedative? Please be honest. Identifying this mechanism is a crucial step to healing it. This creates a devastating self-perpetuating vicious cycle. The emotional stress drives the binge eating. The binge eating drives the storage of visceral fat. The expanding visceral fat creates systemic inflammation. And the systemic inflammation acts as a physiological stressor to the brain which creates more stress. Finally, I want to address the most painful sensitive but perhaps the most clinically important part of this biology, the concept of the armor. In Dr. Felit's original thousands of patient interviews and in the clinical experience of obesity medicine physicians worldwide we have observed a heartbreaking pattern among survivors of sexual abuse physical boundary violations and severe childhood bullying for many of these individuals. The excess atipose tissue serves a highly specific protective function. It desexualizes the body and creates a physical barrier. a thick layer of insulation between the vulnerable inner self and a dangerous outer world. It makes them feel invisible to the predators or conversely it makes them feel big enough and imposing enough to never be victimized again. Biologically, we see this physiological paradox mirrored in the hormone leptin. We typically think of leptin as our primary satiety hormone, the signal that tells us we're full. But from a strict biological perspective, leptin acts as a permission hormone. High circulating leptin levels, which come from having high body fat, signals to the hypothalamus that the body has enough energy to safely reproduce. But if the traumatized nervous system has deeply tagged reproduction, intimacy, or unwanted physical attention as a severe life-threatening danger, the psyche and the biology enter a brutal war. Your conscious logical mind looks in the mirror and says, "I want to lose weight. I want to feel lighter. I want to be healthy." But your subconscious primitive survival brain also says if you get small you can get hurt. If you get attractive you may get attacked. And in the battle between the logical mind and the survival brain the survival brain controls the metabolic levers. So let's say you start a new diet. You lose 20 pounds. People start noticing. Co-workers comment on how great you look. Someone gives you an unsolicited physical compliment and suddenly absolute panic sets in. The central nervous system alarm rings. The insomnia starts. The anxiety spikes to unbearable levels. And the subconscious sabotage begins. You find yourself mindlessly binging in the middle of the night and quickly gain the 20 lbs back, probably plus an extra 10 lbs of buffer. And you sit there devastated, blaming your willpower. You call yourself weak, undisiplined, and broken. But you weren't weak. You were terrified. Your body sensed a massive vulnerability, the sudden loss of your protective armor, and it rushed to build the wall to keep you safe. This is exactly what I mean when I say severe obesity is a neuromabolic adaptation. Your weight is not the core problem. Your weight is the solution your body brilliantly created to solve the much deeper problem of feeling fundamentally unsafe. So where does this reality leave us? If your fat cells are structurally programmed by your childhood trauma and your DNA is epigenetically tagged by unhealed stress, are you doomed? Is this a permanent life sentence? Are you destined to fight your weight forever? Absolutely not. Epigenetics is like a switch. And the great thing about switches is that they can be flipped back. The human brain possesses extraordinary neuroplasticity, the ability to rewrite its neural pathways. The HBA access, no matter how disregulated, can be recalibrated. But, and this is the single most important clinical nuance I would give you in this entire series, you can't fix a trauma-induced metabolism issue with a calorie deficit alone. If you try to simply diet the weight off without actively addressing the underlying nervous system dysregulation, you're just aggressively poking a sleeping bear. You're adding physiological stress to psychological stress. you're triggering the exact survival mechanism that's keeping you heavy in the first place. The path forward, the true protocol for lasting biological healing, which will detail step by step in the final episode of this series, requires a complete reversal of that traditional order of operation. Most people and some medical professionals tell you to do this. diet first, exercise vigorously, and maybe try some stress management or meditation last if you have free time. For the trauma phenotype, this order is a recipe for catastrophic failure. The order must be entirely flipped. Safety first. Before we cut a single calorie, we have to convince the emotional center of the brain, your amygdala, that the war is definitely over. This involves active daily nervous system regulation. This means summatic experiencing targeted vagus nerve stimulation to increase parasympathetic veagal tone and aggressive sleep regulation to lower baseline cortisol. Regulation is second. We have to mechanically fix the broken cortisol rhythm. This means utilizing early morning sunlight viewing to set the circadian clock. implementing strict protein pacing to stabilize blood sugar and setting fierce interpersonal boundaries to stop new stress from entering the system. Nutrition is third. Only once the nervous system feels safe do we focus on the plate and we nourish the body specifically to lower systemic inflammation rather than starving it to forcefully induce weight loss. We have to methodically dismantle the bunker not by violently attacking it with starvation but by peacefully proving to the cellular biology that the war is over. We covered some heavy paradigm shifting biology today. But before we can fully engage in that repair, we have to deeply understand the biological cost of carrying this armor. Because when the human body stays in this high cortisol, high visceral fat survival mode for years and decades, something concerning begins to happen. The visceral fat tissue doesn't just sit there quietly. It's highly active. And over time, it starts to suffer from hypoxia, a lack of oxygen. It starts to change. And when visceral fat gets like this, it starts to send out powerful destructive immune signals called inflammatory cytoines. These cytoines travel through your bloodstream and they're capable of crossing the bloodb brain barrier, creating not only systemic inflammation, but also affect the optimal brain function.
