- Is Ozempic an anti-aging medication? Wegovy, Semaglutide: Kind: captions Language: en Can Ozempic or Wegovy, the drug that&nbsp; helped Elon Musk drop a ton of weight&nbsp;&nbsp; help you live longer? I’ll explain how&nbsp; this injectable diabetes and weight loss&nbsp;&nbsp; medication works and if it’s something&nbsp; to consider in your longevity journey. Welcome to House of Longevity.&nbsp; Now, let’s get into it. You’ve probably heard the names Ozempic or&nbsp; Wegovy, or at least the jingle on TV commercials. Ozempic and Wegovy are actually two brand&nbsp; names of the same drug – semaglutide. Ozempic&nbsp;&nbsp; is marketed for diabetes treatment and Wegovy&nbsp; for weight loss. For the sake of simplicity,&nbsp;&nbsp; I’m going to refer to them both as&nbsp; semaglutide for the rest of this video. So, how does it work? Semaglutide is injected once a&nbsp; week into the stomach, thigh,&nbsp;&nbsp; or buttocks. It’s a GLP-1 receptor&nbsp; agonist which means it mimics GLP-1,&nbsp;&nbsp; a hormone in the body that signals the brain&nbsp; that we’re full and should stop eating. Semaglutide lowers fasting and post-prandial&nbsp; (after a meal) blood glucose by stimulating&nbsp;&nbsp; insulin secretion in a glucose-dependent&nbsp; manner. This means that insulin is being&nbsp;&nbsp; released the same way it is for someone who&nbsp; doesn’t have diabetes. It also slows the&nbsp;&nbsp; emptying of the stomach, so you stay fuller&nbsp; longer. This prevents blood sugar spikes. Among the side effects of semaglutide are&nbsp; gastro-intestinal problems, nausea, diarrhea,&nbsp;&nbsp; and… weight loss. Around 70-80 percent&nbsp; of people with diabetes are overweight&nbsp;&nbsp; or obese, so weight loss as a side effect&nbsp; is generally seen as a good thing. There’s&nbsp;&nbsp; also a black box warning on semaglutide&nbsp; for a risk of thyroid c-cell tumors. A study published in early 2021 in the&nbsp; New England Journal of Medicine showed&nbsp;&nbsp; an average weight loss of 14.9 percent of&nbsp; body weight from baseline to week 68 of&nbsp;&nbsp; the trial compared to a 2.9 percent&nbsp; weight loss for the placebo group. And once people learned about this,&nbsp; everyone who was anyone wanted semaglutide. In June 2021, it was approved for&nbsp; use as a chronic weight-loss drug,&nbsp;&nbsp; and in December 2022, it was&nbsp; approved for children ages 12&nbsp;&nbsp; and up who are in the 95th percentile&nbsp; in terms of BMI for their age and sex. It’s been talked about a lot on social media and&nbsp; many famous people have either taken it or are&nbsp;&nbsp; rumored to have taken it, like Oprah Winfrey,&nbsp; Kim and Khloe Kardashian, Post Malone, and Mindy Kaling, to name just a few.&nbsp; There’s been simultaneously wide approval for its&nbsp; use and backlash against those who have taken it.&nbsp;&nbsp; And even backlash from those who&nbsp; took it and it worked for them. So, what’s the problem? Before it was approved for&nbsp; use as a weight-loss drug,&nbsp;&nbsp; doctors were prescribing it off-label to&nbsp; their non-diabetic patients, but at such&nbsp;&nbsp; a level that some people who really needed it&nbsp; couldn’t get it for their diabetes treatment. All that aside, what happens to people&nbsp; who have reached their desired weight&nbsp;&nbsp; using the drug and stopped taking it? This&nbsp; study showed that an average of 2/3 of the&nbsp;&nbsp; weight lost was gained back within a year&nbsp; after they stopped taking it. It might be&nbsp;&nbsp; why it’s referred to as a chronic weight loss&nbsp; medication, meaning you need to keep taking it. There’s another side effect people are&nbsp; talking about known as “Ozempic face,”&nbsp;&nbsp; which is the loss of fat in the face, giving it&nbsp; a droopy or gaunt look. But is it a real thing? So, like he said, that’s a normal side effect&nbsp; of extreme or rapid weight loss for almost&nbsp;&nbsp; any reason. And for someone suffering from severe&nbsp; obesity, this may be the least of their concerns. Finally, can it help you live longer? The simple answer is yes, if you suffer from&nbsp; type-2 diabetes and/or are obese and have&nbsp;&nbsp; struggled for years to lose a substantial amount&nbsp; of weight, this can extend your life. Here’s why: Comparatively, this drug is a safe, non-invasive&nbsp; alternative to bariatric surgery – like a gastric&nbsp;&nbsp; bypass, sleeve gastrectomy, duodenal switch,&nbsp; and adjustable gastric banding. Those surgeries&nbsp;come with risks and require extreme lifestyle changes that most&nbsp; people can’t maintain, so they should only be&nbsp;&nbsp; a last resort. But the positive effects&nbsp; of weight loss shouldn’t be downplayed,&nbsp;&nbsp; since obesity is a major risk factor for&nbsp; cardiovascular disease, hypertension, diabetes,&nbsp;&nbsp; and stroke. If you reduce your chance of getting&nbsp; any of those conditions, you will live longer. Two studies showed that treatment with&nbsp; semaglutide in people with type-2 diabetes&nbsp;&nbsp; “demonstrated consistent reductions in major&nbsp; adverse cardiovascular events (MACE) compared&nbsp;&nbsp; with a placebo, regardless of baseline blood sugar&nbsp; or kidney function.” I’ll link the study below. But if you are overweight and struggle with&nbsp; bad habits like binge eating or eating when&nbsp;&nbsp; you’re bored or you’re on a see-food&nbsp; diet (you see food and you have to eat it),&nbsp;&nbsp; then semaglutide is not for you. Exercise&nbsp; and self-discipline are for you. Maybe&nbsp;&nbsp; a shock collar that a loved one can&nbsp; trigger when you try to buy junk food. All jokes aside, my conclusion is that semaglutide is a great,&nbsp; innovative drug for diabetes and weight loss, and&nbsp;&nbsp; by extension, longevity. But it’s definitely not&nbsp; for everyone. I’m not a doctor, so do your own further research and talk to&nbsp;a medical professional if you think you might be&nbsp;&nbsp; a good candidate for either Ozempic or Wegovy.&nbsp; I’ll leave a bunch of links in the description&nbsp;&nbsp; for the full videos from which I showed clips&nbsp; and from the studies and articles I quoted. - The End of Ozempic? Why March 20th Changes Everything.: Kind: captions Language: en Right now, a single engineered molecule is driving one of the largest financial booms in modern history. Companies like Novo Nordisk and Eli Relly have added hundreds of billions of dollars to their market caps. But the celebrity obsession and the relentless stream of before and after photos on social media hide a much harsher reality. Millions of type 2 diabetics who rely on this exact same medication to survive are finding their pharmacy shelves completely empty. The global supply chain physically collapsed under the weight of this drug. This happened because the science behind it is incredibly complex to manufacture. But that bottleneck has an expiration date. March 2026 is the month the entire system flips. A highstakes pharmaceutical crisis has collided with a biological breakthrough, straining the limits of global manufacturing and leaving the world's supply at zero. GLP1 stands for glucagon-l like peptide 1. It is a hormone your gut produces naturally every time you eat. Its primary job is to travel to your pancreas and trigger the release of insulin which brings your blood sugar down. This is why it was originally developed as a diabetes treatment. But it does something else on its way through your system. It slows down what doctors call gastric emptying. Food physically stays in your stomach longer, sending a signal to your body that you are full for hours after a small meal. Then comes the neurological effect. The hormone crosses the bloodb brain barrier and binds directly to the satiety center in your brain. It shuts off that constant nagging internal voice telling you to snack, a psychological effect many patients call food noise. This chart shows the half-life of GLP-1 in your body. Natural GLP-1 breaks down in minutes shown by this steep drop. Scientists at Novo Nordisk engineered a modified version called saglutide that survives for a full week represented by this flat sustained line. By keeping that fullness signal turned on permanently, clinical trials showed patients losing 15% to 22% of their total body weight. This success shifted the drug's role from a diabetes management tool to a widespread medical intervention for obesity. The science worked. The demand was nearly infinite. Yet in 2024 and 2025, pharmacy bins sat empty all over the world. The bottleneck lies in the manufacturing. These are not simple chemical pills that can be stamped out by the millions. They are biologics, complex molecules grown in living cells. Producing biologics requires highly specialized pristine environments to synthesize the liquid and equally complex manufacturing lines to assemble the intricate auto- injector pens used to deliver them. When the FDA officially approved these biologics for widespread obesity treatment, the physical limits of production were instantly overwhelmed. Factories could not build new sterile lines fast enough to meet the massive surge in prescriptions. The sheer biological sophistication that made the molecule effective is the same factor that prevented its global distribution. This scarcity created immediate ethical friction at the pharmacy counter. Diabetic patients fighting for a medicine they needed for baseline survival were suddenly competing with off label users seeking a cosmetic fix. With the official supply stuck at zero, a massive vacuum formed in the market. A parallel largely unregulated pharmaceutical gray market expanded overnight to fill it. Independent compoundingarmacies exploited legal loopholes, manufacturing and selling unverified bootleg versions of the semaglutide molecule directly to consumers. The engineered molecule was so deeply desired by the public that its scarcity forced an entire parallel economy into existence. But this supply chain crisis has a hard end date. On March 20th, 2026, the primary patent for semiglutide officially expires in several major international markets. This shifts the balance of power directly to India, the pharmacy of the world. Generic manufacturers like Zidus and Dr. Ready's have been preparing for a massive day one launch. To legally export generics, Indian manufacturers had to clear severe global regulatory hurdles, ensuring facilities strictly adhere to good manufacturing practice and CH guidelines. This chart shows the economic shift ahead. We are moving from a monopolized landscape where a monthly injection costs $1,000 to an open market where generic versions could cost less than $50. These generics will also bypass the auto injector bottleneck entirely. The market is preparing for the rise of oral GLP1s, replacing complex sterile needles with a daily pill. The 2026 patent expiration ends the era of exclusive manufacturing rights. By opening production to more facilities and dropping prices, this molecule moves from a luxury clinical tool to a standard global pharmaceutical. However, doctors are raising red flags about long-term use. Clinical data shows side effects like significant muscle wasting and the reality that once a patient stops taking the medication, the weight often returns. This raises a difficult question. How will our society function when physical willpower is replaced by a cheap, widely available injection? The supply crunch is finally easing as manufacturing expands and patient choices increase. I want to hear from you. Would you trust a generic version of Ompic if it cost 90% less than the name brand? Drop a comment below and subscribe for more deep dives into the intersection of pharma and tech. I'll see you in the next one.

- What Ozempic Is Secretly Doing To Your Gut Bacteria (38 dDifferent Studies): Kind: captions Language: en Right now, tens of millions of people are taking Ozmpic, WGOI or Monjaro. Their gut bacteria, the trillions of microorganisms that regulate their immune system, their mood, their inflammation levels, and even how well the drug itself keeps working are being fundamentally restructured. A major systematic review published just this year analyzed 38 separate studies on exactly this question. And what the researchers found is more complicated than anyone expected. Some of it is genuinely good news, but some of it is a warning because if you're on one of these drugs and eating the wrong way, you may be silently dismantling the very biological system that makes the medication work long term. This is what GLP1 drugs are really doing to your gut. Before we go any further, this is not medical advice. If you're currently on any medication or thinking about starting one, please talk to your doctor before making any changes. What I want to do here is translate the research into plain language. The kind of information that rarely makes it into a 10-minute GP appointment. The study underpinning this video is a peer-reviewed systematic review published in a leading nutrition journal in April 2026. It synthesized findings from 38 studies examining how GLP-1 receptor agonists, the drug class that includes semiglutide and tzepide, interact with and reshape the gut microbiome. What it found challenges the way most people think about these medications because GLP-1 drugs don't just tell your brain you're full. They reach all the way down into your intestine and start rearranging the ecosystem that lives there. Let's make sure we're on the same page about what the gut microbiome actually is because it's far more than a wellness buzzword. Your gut contains roughly 38 trillion microorganisms, bacteria, fungi, archa. This ecosystem functions like a secondary organ. It produces vitamins, regulates your immune system, communicates directly with your brain through the vagus nerve, and determines how much energy your body extracts from the food you eat. When this ecosystem is diverse and balanced, it is actively working in your favor. When it becomes disrupted, when species are lost and diversity collapses, it is one of the most consistent markers in modern medicine for poor long-term metabolic health. Scientists increasingly talk about microbial diversity, the way cardiologists talk about blood pressure. It's a number that tells you something important about your future, not just your present. So, when a drug as powerful as saglutide reaches into that ecosystem and starts making structural changes, the question can't just be how much weight comes off. The question has to be what kind of microbiome are you left with once it does? Here is where the genuinely encouraging news begins. Across almost every GLP-1 drug examined in the review, laglutide, semiglutide, dulaglutide, researchers found one consistent pattern. A specific species of gut bacteria was significantly and reliably increasing. It is called acromancium mucinophila. Acrimania lives in the mucous layer that lines the inside of your intestine. Its primary function is to reinforce that lining to maintain the integrity of the barrier between your gut contents and your bloodstream. And this matters enormously. When that barrier is compromised, a state researchers call intestinal permeability. Bacterial toxins known as lipopolysaccharides can seep into the bloodstream. Once circulating, the immune system treats them as a threat and mounts a response. The result is lowgrade chronic systemic inflammation. That kind of inflammation is closely associated with insulin resistance, persistent fatigue, cognitive fog, and the type of metabolic stagnation that makes weight loss feel impossible regardless of what you eat. So, the finding that GLP-1 drugs are consistently raising acromancia levels is meaningful. It suggests these medications may be doing something beyond appetite suppression. They may be actively supporting part of the gut architecture that underlies metabolic health in the first place. But here's where it gets significantly more complicated. The same systematic review that identified those acromansia increases also flagged something that every person on a GLP-1 drug should understand. In studies focused on semaglutide specifically the active compound in both ompic and wiggoi researchers observed a notable reduction in overall microbial diversity. Yes, acrimansia goes up but a broader range of other bacterial species go down. And in the gut diversity is not just a nice feature. It is the foundation of resilience. A microbiome with hundreds of thriving and distinct species can absorb dietary changes, fight off opportunistic bacteria and maintain metabolic stability. A microbiome with collapsing diversity even if it has acromansia doing solid work becomes fragile. It loses its redundancy and critically it loses the range of species responsible for producing short- chain fatty acids. The compounds that feed your gut lining regulate inflammation and send sati signals to your brain. When diversity drops, those signals weaken. And the mechanism behind that diversity loss comes down almost entirely to what the person on the medication is eating. What the research actually shows is that the drug and the diet are inseparable. One without the other creates a lopsided microbiome capable of one thing fragile against everything else. One of the things GLP-1 drugs do very effectively is suppress appetite. People on semiglutide often describe simply forgetting to eat or feeling full after just a few bites. And on the surface, this sounds ideal. Fewer calories consumed, weight loss follows. But here's where the problem quietly develops. When people on these medications do eat, when they finally feel hungry enough to put something in their body, they tend to reach for the path of least resistance. soft, processed, easy to eat foods, white bread, crackers, ready meals, refined carbohydrates that go down without triggering the nausea that higher doses of these drugs can cause. Researchers now have a name for this pattern, the beige diet. And from a microbiome standpoint, it is deeply problematic. The beneficial bacteria that maintain your gut's diversity, species like bifidobacterium, foliacterium prownits, and rose buria, they do not feed on your food directly. They feed on the fermentable fiber that your food leaves behind in the colon. No fiber, no fuel. When those populations decline through lack of substrate, you lose the bacteria that produce butyrate, the primary energy source for the cells that line your large intestine. Butyrate production drops, the gut wall weakens, and the barrier work that acrimansia is doing gets undermined from the inside. The very protection the drug was helping build quietly starts to erode. And researchers point to this exact pattern. high acromansia, low overall diversity, poor diet quality. As one of the leading explanations for why a significant number of people regain weight rapidly after stopping GLP-1 therapy, the drug goes, "The microbiome isn't resilient enough to hold the metabolic gains. The weight comes back." Stay with me because this next part is where it becomes genuinely actionable. Based on what the research shows, there are three practical strategies that matter most for anyone on a GLP-1 medication who wants to protect their gut health alongside their weight loss. The first is gradual fiber titration. And the word gradual is doing a lot of work there because if you're on a GLP-1 drug and you suddenly increase your fiber intake significantly, you will likely feel terrible. These medications already slow down digestion considerably. Adding a large fiber load on top of a sluggish gut causes the kind of bloating and discomfort that many people mistake for a side effect of the medication itself. The researchbacked approach is to increase fiber slowly, roughly 3 to 5 grams per week, working toward a daily target of 25 to 35 gram. Focus particularly on soluble fibers. Oats, beans, lentils, chia seeds. These ferment gently and are far easier to tolerate than rougher insoluble fibers when your gut is already working at a slower pace. And stay hydrated. Fiber absorbs water and GLP-1 users frequently have blunted thirst signals, which means dehydration can compound the constipation problem significantly if you're not consciously keeping fluids up. The second is targeted probiotic support. This is not about grabbing a generic multistrain supplement from a supermarket shelf. The research points to specific studied strains for the specific problems that GLP-1 users most commonly face. For slowed transit and constipation among the most common complaints, a strain called bifidobacterium lactis hn019 has clinical evidence for measurably reducing whole gut transit time. For bloating specifically, bifidobacterium lactis bio7 has been studied and shown meaningful reductions in that pressure-like sensation in clinical trials. For bowel unpredictability, the pattern where things swing between too slow and too fast, sacroyces bulardi is a well-documented yeast-based probiotic that consistently appears in the research on gut stabilization during periods of microbiome disruption. These are the strains that appear most reliably in the evidence for GLP-1 adjacent gut symptoms and they are worth discussing with your doctor or pharmacist. The third is deliberate protein prioritization. One of the most under reportported consequences of GLP-1 therapy is lean muscle loss. When total food intake drops dramatically and the body is shedding weight quickly, research indicates that a significant portion of that weight can come from muscle rather than fat alone. This matters because less muscle means a lower resting metabolic rate, which means maintaining weight without the drug becomes significantly harder over time. Researchers recommend aiming for approximately 1.2 to 2 g of protein per kilogram of body weight spread across several small meals rather than one or two large ones. From a microbiome perspective, the key is pairing that protein with diverse plant foods. The goal is the combination adequate protein, diverse plant fiber, steady hydration. That is the nutritional environment in which a resilient microbiome can hold together through the metabolic changes these drugs create. The bottom line is this. GLP-1 medications are genuinely powerful and the occur findings give real reason for optimism about what they may be doing well beyond the scale. But these drugs are reshaping your gut microbiome in ways that the weight loss conversation almost never mentions. Whether that reshaping leaves you more metabolically resilient long term or quietly sets you up for rebound comes down largely to what you're feeding your gut bacteria while the medication is doing its work. The gut does not ask for much. Diversity, fiber, consistency. Give it those things and it becomes one of the most powerful allies your long-term health has. If this kind of research is useful to you, subscribe. We cover exactly this depth every week. And coming up next, the specific foods that research now links to amensia growth.

Based off of some of the valid criticisms that Oprah brought up in her special, I think we should take a look at how the media has historically talked about celebrities' weight and how that's contributed to this mess we're in right now. While she was a very prominent example of this issue, it's even sadder to say that she wasn't the only person who experienced it. Before more socially conscious attitudes started to creep into mainstream media, it was just seen as par for the course to pick apart every aspect of a celebrity's body. It's why Oprah asked the Olsen Twins what size they were on national television. This was obviously much worse for women, but it was pretty universal across the board. Like, snapping paparazzi photos of action stars and saying, "Look, their bodies are different now," was like an age-old Hollywood special at this point. And clearly those socially conscious attitudes are not creeping quickly enough because this is still happening. I remember just a couple of years ago, Jason Momoa was in the news cycle for not looking like Aquaman 24/7, which is literally absurd. I'm actually going to talk more about Jason Momoa in part three, but the point is people are obsessed with Hollywood's bodies. Now here's the thing. Part of this is cyclical. Hollywood is obsessed with specific body types, which in turn fuels everyone's fixation on bodies in general, and then this spills out and affects people in Hollywood even outside of their job in moments when they are not working hard to look unattainable. This in turn completely skews everyone's idea of what normal looks like in the first place, and it's just like a big catch-22 where everyone's a victim. But I think it goes even deeper than that though. Yes, body standards explain some of this, but I think the heart of the issue is just that people are obsessed with the concept of transformation in general. There's just something innately human about wanting to see something change. Think about how many YouTube videos featuring some sort of crazy transformation are insanely popular. When it comes to things like say, caterpillars with no rizz turning into dripped-out butterflies or a house getting renovated from a literal dump into a modern masterpiece, I totally get it. But when this transformation obsession manifests itself as photographing celebrities without their consent and then picking apart the pictures to prove that they've gained weight because they don't look like they do in a photoshopped or highly contrived photo shoot or video, it's safe to say that it's more of a negative in this case. I also want to talk about the flip side of this issue. Celebrity weight obsession doesn't somehow become better when you're talking about weight loss instead of weight gain. We seem to have at least figured out for the most part that someone's body getting bigger should not be a news report, but nobody seems to have gotten the memo about the reverse. There are so many articles anytime a celebrity dares to look somewhat smaller than they did the last time we saw them. And it neatly fits into our transformation mania, it's not as overt as outright fat shaming, and then people start doing a bunch of annoying speculation into how they did it and what they're eating, and it's just a lot. It is actually crazy to think how accepted it is for people to write entire articles dedicated to celebrity weight loss. You're still commenting on people's bodies. You do get that? Often when the people in question are not doing so themselves. I'd also like to point out that weight loss is not always good. Who remembers the show “The Biggest Loser”? I certainly do. I literally remember watching this show as a kid and thinking, "Wow, this is awesome." And then I wonder where this obsession with transformation comes from. But this show is a prime example of weight loss that is completely disconnected from health. Yes, the pretense of getting healthier is there, but frankly, the whole idea of rapidly losing weight so you can win a cash prize isn't something that should be encouraged under any circumstances when again, gradual weight loss has been shown to be more sustainable over the long term. And that's not even getting into the multiple other issues with that show and the unhealthy behavior that they were promoting. It's not a great sign when your Wikipedia page has “risks and criticism” as a section. But anyway, besides that, there are so many medical conditions one can get where the side effects include rapid weight loss, and in this case, losing weight is actually kind of awful. And frankly, unless someone tells you, you have no idea of knowing how they lost that weight in the first place, nor is it any of your business. You can't know if someone is healthier just because they are now smaller. You're not really celebrating their health, you're just glorifying their image because it happened to change to fit into your standard. But we'll get more into that when we start talking about the fatphobia of it all. And I personally think there is a fairly straightforward answer for how the media should be commenting on people's bodies going forward. Basically, they shouldn't. That's it. That's the solution. Notice how I haven't actually made any comments about anyone's body in this entire video, thin, bigger or otherwise? It's not hard to do, even in a context where I am constantly talking about weight loss and weight loss medication. A concept that I really strove for, and not just this video but my personal life as well, is body neutrality. WebMD defines it simply as, "The act of taking a neutral stance towards your body, both emotionally and physically. That means not supporting the hatred towards your body's limitations or investing the time and energy to love it either.” Literally, your body is just your body. It's not this holy temple that should be worshiped, and it's also not something to be despised. It is genuinely just there, and it is doing its absolute best to persist, and honestly, that can be enough. Learning how to let that be enough was a huge thing for me. And here's the thing, if that's not your personal stance on your own body, that's absolutely fine, but it should definitely be your stance on everyone else's body. We are not affected by how other people look. They are literally just there. Other people's bodies don't exist for us to have opinions on, but for the people in them to live their lives while we live ours. There's nothing wrong with being proud of your body, and it's okay to join in celebrating someone else's achievements if they are the ones bringing this to the table. But fixating on this when that person didn't mention it is just weird, it doesn't matter what you're saying. There are a lot more resources if you want to go more in depth about body neutrality, and how to apply it, but I really think it's going to be the way of going forward when it comes to media coverage. Lizzo has recently come forward as a proponent of the idea, and Jonah Hill made waves a couple of years ago after he lost weight and then asked fans not to comment on his body, even if it's positive, because it's, quote, "not helpful and doesn't feel good." Though I like how in this very same screenshot, we see actress Sharon Stone chiming in with, "Can I say you look good? Because you do." Clearly, it's going to take a hot minute before people figure out what body neutrality is all about, but honestly, whether the media catches up or not, maybe it's something we can implement in our own lives, so we can break free from this vicious cycle of celebrity weight loss obsession for ourselves. I think one of the most annoying things I've seen in a lot of traditional media when it comes to Ozempic is either overselling the drug as some sort of unparalleled miracle cure, or fear-mongering to the point of absurdity, or sometimes even both. It's usually both. With the overselling, I'm not exaggerating with the miracle thing. Are these drugs a miracle moment? I would be very hesitant to say that anything with obesity is a miracle. Semaglutide is obviously a huge advancement, and it's clearly working better for a lot of people than previous attempts at treating obesity. But at the same time, not every medication is right for everyone, and I think positing this as some sort of wonder drug is not only misleading, but potentially disheartening for people as well. We are still learning what is the best way to give patients these medications, follow them on it. We're learning so much, but nothing's a miracle. Besides outright over hype, I noticed a lot of media referring to it as a trend as well, which I did in this video too, and I have mixed feelings about this. On the one hand, it is literally a trend, we saw that in the video, especially among people who didn't need it in the first place. We've also seen the negative impact that this unnecessary trend has had. I think just breezily generating media copy about the phenomenon without explaining why this should never have been a trend in the first place tends to rub me the wrong way. But by far, the worst angle I've seen media outlets take is definitely the blatant fear-mongering. I've seen some stuff that makes Oprah's special look downright Emmy-worthy by comparison. The allure is life-changing weight loss, but does it come with life-ending risk? “Ozempic risk. Could weight loss injections be fatal?” Productions like this always follow the same format. They hype up the drug, again, seriously overselling it, and then they try to make it sound like the medicine is secretly super dangerous, and this whole phenomenon is a threat to humanity as we know it. They focus on cases of extreme side effects, or sometimes even unrelated things that just happen to happen to people on Ozempic. Speaking of side effects, there are two reasons I think that the discourse around them is completely overblown. A, the side effects make perfect sense, and B, none of them are a secret. Again, thinking back to this medicine, and how it works, i.e., slowing down your digestive process beyond its natural rate, it tracks that the majority of side effects in that case are related to digestion. Nausea, diarrhea, vomiting, constipation, stomach pain, etc. If Wegovy was causing people's hair to fall out, or turning their nails brittle or something, I think it would be pretty natural to question why this is happening, but it's pretty clear that's not the case here. Also, if you're wondering where I'm getting this list of symptoms from, it's literally the website for Wegovy. I don't get why some people treat the side effects like they are secret, or proof that the medicine is evil. Even the one side effect which is the most serious has a prominent section on the website. These drugs do carry a black box warning for a rare type of thyroid cancer. Do not use Wegovy if you or any of your family has ever had this type of thyroid cancer. I don't think it can get any more specific than this. Not everyone who brings up the side effects is doing so to scare people off. I do believe that this is valuable information, sharing it is not inherently bad. But, I also think you're more likely to get a balanced perspective when it's coming from a medical professional, like the doctor I just showed. These medications are generally well tolerated, but one of the ways in which they work is by slowing the emptying of the stomach. Saying, "Hey, this is how it works, and these are the effects it may have" is one thing, but saying, "These people literally died. We don't know if it was because of Ozempic, but they were on Ozempic, so it could have been," that is just super gross, and it seriously makes me question the intent behind the reporting. A coroner ruled Tim's cause of death was undetermined. Sue says that doesn't make sense. Obviously, it is tragic, and I feel nothing but sorry for the people in this special who have lost their loved ones, but to the 60 Minutes execs that have spun this into a, "Does Ozempic kill people" TV special, that is just unhinged behavior, and nobody responsible for this is seeing heaven. Speaking of feeling sorry, I don't want this to sound like I'm blaming people for their side effects, by the way. Actually, you know what? Quick caveat. If you're taking this stuff and you know don't need it, but it's just because you can afford to, and you're trying to keep up with your rich friends, I'm going to be honest, I'm absolutely blaming you for your side effects, and you deserve every one that you get. You are the Ozempic girly who deserves the vomiting. I went to lunch with a girlfriend a few days later, and she's like, "I'm not really eating anything. I'm so nauseous. I'm on Ozempic." I was like, "I'm nauseous, too." The idea of a bunch of rich people going out for lunch but feeling too bad to eat it because of choices they made that are disadvantaging poor people is some sort of strange poetic justice. For the average person who was using this medication for medicinal purposes, I don't want it to sound like I'm blaming them for their side effects, or I don't care that they happen. Everyone's body is different, and for some people, the stuff actually does get pretty serious. There are options for managing the side effects, but in the end, as I've said, not every medication works for everyone, and genuinely, my heart goes out to anyone who gets overwhelmed by these side effects. However, the side effects in question are still literally on the box, and it's bizarre that so much of the protest here is coming from people who don't even plan on taking this stuff in the first place, or from people who did take it but don't need it, and they're the main ones not following the instructions. There are people out there who are not using these properly, but in using them properly, we find that the vast majority of people tolerate them well. Instead of losing weight at a gradual pace, celebrities want it to happen as quickly as possible, and then they get blindsided when there are actual consequences. Who would've thought? It takes five months to go from the lowest dose to the highest dose, I've heard stories of people starting with doses that are in the middle or higher, and that is guaranteed to give someone side effects. People even say, "Did you know you have to take the medication forever? Otherwise, the weight comes back," as if that's some sort of gotcha. Wegovy, Zepbound, etc, these are long-term medications. The very first sentence on the Wikipedia page for Semaglutide says, "It's used for long-term weight management." The average person is not just going to be able to click a couple of pens and then never worry about their weight ever again. That would be amazing, but we're not there yet. It's not a conspiracy, it's just science that anybody's doctor would explain to them. Yes, you have to keep taking the medication. Speaking of conspiracies, another reason I really don't like this fear-mongering is, it leans into some pretty wacky fringe groups. I've noticed that Ozempic is very much the latest target of that paranoid group of people who believe that literally all science and medicine is dangerous and full of malintent, like vaccines are all just the work of the deep state, and Ozempic is just the latest firmware update in this clandestine agenda. These are the kinds of people who will accuse me of promoting weight loss meds just because of this video, and I'm literally just talking about them. Pandering to these people by playing dumb and hyper-fixating on side effects and usage instructions of a medication that is, again, not a secret, is just really an irresponsible way of approaching your coverage. At the end of the day, if you have questions about this medication, talk to your doctor, not to a dramatic TV special or a conspiracy theorist's YouTube comment section. Spoiler alert, medicine has side effects, a sentence I never thought would be shocking to anyone ever. All right, in this part I want to look at some of the social attitudes and dynamics that are driving this discourse. As we've seen, Ozempic is definitely a big deal, but why is it a big deal? I have a few observations that explain things, but they all show that, once again, it starts at the top and finds its way down to each and every one of us. No discussion about Ozempic or weight in general would be complete without a look into body image issues, and how they play into all of this in the first place. If the fact that Hollywood is commandeering literal medicine for aesthetic purposes shows us anything, it's that weight is at the crossroads of both health and beauty, and this overlap is absolutely disastrous on both ends. It has two main effects. The desire to be beautiful now manifests as extreme unhealthiness, and concern for other people's health is often just thinly veiled discrimination for not fitting into dangerous beauty standards. Now, I'll get more into the second one later, but I really want to focus on that first one, beauty at the expense of your health. I think that this conversation is usually very focused on beauty standards for women, and how quickly they veer into unattainability and serious health issues. Honestly, there are already a lot of great resources about these standards created by the women and femme-presenting people who are affected by them. Instead of adding my limited perspective there, I thought I'd actually explore this through the lens of something I experience every day, Hollywood's promotion of beauty standards and body image issues for men. With toxic masculinities, comical emphasis on logic and downplaying your emotions, men don't generally sit around unpacking male beauty standards and how it feels to pursue them or exist outside of them, at least not in my experience, but that doesn't make the effects any less drastic. Hollywood dictates what men should look like, and these ideal bodies go in and out of fashion just like they do for women. We may not typically identify with terms like slim, thick or curvy, but we do have Captain America, and The Rock, and any dude could tell you that those are two different things. But what neither Cap nor Mr. Dwayne Johnson would explain is that it's highly unlikely that the average person would ever wind up looking like either of them. Part of the reason that it took the comedians to expose the Ozempic craze behavior is that Hollywood actors in general work really hard to downplay the difference between us versus Watching The Avengers is no fun if you're just sitting there thinking the whole time about how any one of the actors you see on screen could just Thanos snap your rent, or student loans, or anything away without even blinking, but they won't. Yes, we want our stars to be in a different stratosphere from us, but at the same time, we don't really want them to admit it, at least not anymore. There was actually a time where stars were supposed to be completely out of touch. From Paris Hilton and Nicole Richie's entire reality show where they struggled to get their single shifts of everyday jobs to idiosyncratic artists like David Bowie, who were known for being larger than life, we weren't exactly supposed to be anything like these people. Therefore, these people weren't so quick to hide the differences. Take the father of male beauty standards, Arnold Schwarzenegger. He never actually hid the fact that his physique was attained not just through hard work, but through the usage of steroids as well. Even guys who went the normal dude to super ripped approach for a movie were more open about it, too. Charlie Sheen has talked about using steroids, and the results are pretty obvious, but over time, things started to change. Seemingly coinciding with the rise of the internet and our collective shared experience, there was a rise in relatability as well. Stars started being less and less open with the things that made them seem different from us. When Tom Hardy was asked if steroids played a role in his physique as Bane, he said, "No, I took Smarties. What do you think?" This is a funny answer, but its indirectness says a lot about how expectations started to change. By 2012. We knew the truth, but we didn't really want to hear it. Now, fast-forward to today, and I'd say this relatability has reached critical mass. Look, the guy who plays Superman builds PCs and plays tabletop games just like us. Yes, if he wanted to, Henry Cavill could literally purchase all of your internal organs individually, but he's also just a normal dude. Now, instead of the outright admissions of a Schwarzenegger or a Sheen, or even the half confirmations of a Hardy, when it comes to steroid usage, men in Hollywood give us absolutely nothing. Nothing at all. Sure, there's lots of talk about personal trainers, and live-in cooks, and there's a reluctant acknowledgement that this is still, at the end of the day, unattainable, but from the way this is discussed nowadays, you'd think the word steroids was some kind of curse. It's a huge lie of omission, unless of course we were to assume that nobody is using steroids, and it's just something that they used to do right up until it stopped fitting the image that we're expecting from them. Yes, thanks to Marvel movies, the ideal male physique is literally rooted in comic book illustrations now instead of real people, but nowadays, personal trainers are just so good we don't even need steroids. I guess that could be the case, but I think it makes more sense that there's a bit of an open secret situation going on here. Their job isn't acting anymore, it's going to the gym twice a day and taking steroids, really. Have we got an award for most ripped junkie? Remember what we said about comedians? Look, I'm not going to sit here, built like Jack Skellington, and say that you can never ever attain a muscular physique without steroids. People did stuff like this before steroids even existed, but looking at the insane timelines of the average MCU leading man transformation, and just thinking about the fact that these people have multi-million dollar paychecks that depend on them looking completely unreal, you almost wonder why they wouldn't go the performance enhancement route under careful medical supervision. Though not even the best doctors that money can buy are enough to ensure that there are no negative effects. Charlie Sheen encountered mood changes. Tom Hardy dealt with joint pain, saying, "I think you pay the price with any drastic physical changes." Arnold Schwarzenegger said, "Anytime you abuse the body, you're going to regret it." I just want young people to know that I've seen people getting kidney transplants and suffering tremendously from it. We've taken a perfectly good idea and just clouded it in 100 layers of things that are simply not real, and that is part of the reason this debate is so charged. Yes, we want to look like what we see, and when you think about it like that, Ozempic becoming a "Trend" makes perfect sense. The more I worked on this video, the more I started to wonder, why is this literally the most controversial topic I've ever talked about? Out of all the things I've covered on my channel, why does this one in particular seem to inspire so many strong opinions in so many different people? We've looked at things like body image issues, and celebrity weight obsession, but that wasn't enough for me. I felt like there had to be some sort of underlying force behind all of this, and there is. Spoiler alert, it's fat phobia. When it comes to any form of discrimination, the dictionary definition never goes quite as hard as the actual definition. Fat phobia, according to Macmillan dictionary, is an irrational fear of, or aversion to, or discrimination against obesity or people with obesity. Yes, I think we can agree on the separate parts of that. Fat phobia is irrational, it's discriminatory, that makes it wrong, but is the problem that fat phobia has with larger bodies just that they're not skinny? On one hand, technically yes, that is literally the problem, but that is just the tip of the iceberg. If someone asked me to write a definition, I think I would tack on a few extra words there. It's discrimination, fear and aversion due to preconceived notions about obesity. Once you start getting into some of these preconceptions, everything starts making a lot more sense, even though it's all still very stupid. Now, there are resources that go into more detail about this, but I would say the root of the insanity at the heart of all of this is the irrational belief that weight is a morality issue. Even if people think that they dislike obesity for health reasons, the health side of things is often rooted in or even overshadowed by the fact that they genuinely believe excess body fat makes you a bad person. People use terms like lazy or a bad influence, but they all just mean bad. You're bad and you should feel bad. It's a disease, not a character flaw. When it comes to any sort of irrational beliefs that drive bigotry, I find that it's never a good use of your time trying to figure out why people think this, because truthfully, there is never a valid reason. So because of this, people's whole personalities becomes trying to bin logic and reality into a reason, into something that justifies their belief. So let's unpack some of those justifications because they come up pretty frequently. People with obesity are bad because they're normalizing obesity. This one is usually directed towards celebrities or media representation, et cetera. But here's the thing, obesity is normal. This is a fairly common issue to deal with. Shout out globesity. And if someone is dealing with this, you know that they're still allowed to exist visibly. It's an absurdly paranoid and overly emotional response to see someone with a certain body type and say, "Look, there it is. They're trying to normalize it." A, it's normal, and B, you seriously need to touch grass. This is also why people hyper focus on celebrities when they lose weight as I've gone over. It's like a sick relief that they're no longer existing as a bigger person in the public eye. If anything, the ultimate proof of the media's brain rot is the fact that you are getting shocked and startled by what are frankly very common body types. Of the last 10 or 20 people you saw, how many of them are actually not on camera? And then when you start thinking about cameras and movie magic and how none of this is real in the first place, it all just starts sounding ultimately ridiculous because it is. Anyway, here's another one. "straining the healthcare system." This might be one of my favorite go-tos from fat folks because of how little sense it makes. First of all, if you're saying this and you're in America, what healthcare system? Where is this mystical healthcare system of which you speak? And secondly, you're just saying nothing. Obesity is a health issue. People generally care for their health via healthcare. So it's funny how I never hear this getting brought up with any other medical condition other than obesity. Are you mad at people who break their arms because casts cost money? Do you think that people who need antibiotics should feel bad for using resources? What is being taken away from you just because someone with obesity dared to exist and care about their health? People with obesity are bad because they chose to be obese or as they really love to reword it because they get to pretend it sounds better. People with obesity don't have enough willpower to lose the weight, which literally means the same thing. So I think this gets brought up a lot in response to obesity as a medical issue, but it's really not the got you they think it is. It's okay that, say deaf people need hearing aids because it's not their fault, but it's bad that some people need other forms of care because being fat is something you do to yourself. And the truth of the matter is this, if it was a willpower issue, literally nobody would be unhealthy. We would all just will ourselves to not have the issue. Hello? But the fact that this isn't the case is clearly indicative of a larger problem here. Say you have a hundred children, one of them fails. Well, that might be the child, they didn't study. What if 70 of them fail? Would you say it's each and every one of them's fault? Or would you say it's more likely that it's the teacher's fault? And while seeing as 2.5 million people are dealing with overweight or obesity according to the World Health Organization, I think it's safe to say that it's the teacher's fault here. The teacher of course being the multiple obvious factors in society that set people up for dealing with these issues. The problem is not willpower. The problem is not the people. The problem is the environment that they find themselves and the food environment that they find themselves in. As the World Health Organization says, "The obesogenic environment exacerbating the likelihood of obesity in individuals, populations and in different settings is related to structural factors limiting the availability of healthy, sustainable food at locally affordable prices, lack of safe and easy physical mobility into the daily life of all people, and absence of adequately legal and regulatory environment." If you can diet and exercise yourself past all of that. All of those roadblocks to being healthy in this environment, then just be happy for yourself and mind your own business. But if someone can't, why would you blame them for a problem they did not create? Not everyone is the same. People like that get really hung up on this idea of equality. If I did it, then so can everyone else. And equality is definitely a beautiful end goal, but these same people have no idea that it starts with equity, which is making sure that everyone has the resources they need to achieve equality in the first place. Spoiler alert, different people need different resources. Many people can lose weight with diet and physical activity alone. I don't want you to think that I think that it's not possible to do, but what do you do if that doesn't work? So as I mentioned towards the beginning of the video, I myself have lost a fairly significant amount of weight over the years, and it was definitely something I used to struggle with. Like I said, as a kid, I was pretty thin, so by the time I found myself putting on extra weight, I didn't even know what was happening or why it was happening or what I could do about it. And I was mostly doing it because I was proud of myself and I thought people would find the post interesting. As we established, we love a good transformation. I would say really vague things like, "Oh, I stopped drinking soda," or, "Oh, I just deleted the Uber Eats app." But I realized that I was super uncomfortable with the idea of giving anybody else advice on what to do with their body. And that's because when I was in the same boat, more advice was actually the last thing I needed. I used to seek out explanations online from people who were thinner than me. Hoping that if I just stumbled across the right combination of video tutorials and blog posts, I would eventually unlock the missing piece that would make me skinny again. But then one day it kind of hit me, the methods that other people use to lose weight don't matter to me at all because I am not those other people. I may have wanted to look the same in the end, but we have different bodies. They could give me dietary advice, but we might experience hunger in a completely different way. They could give me exercise advice, but I guarantee we would have two different body types in terms of musculature. And then I realized this, we could both lose the exact same amount of weight and look completely different in the end. So instead of telling you what to do with your body when I am not a doctor and also not you, here's my ultimate weight loss advice for anybody who may have wondered about it. What I eat and how much exercise I do are not as useful to the average person as the changes I experienced in my mindset. When I really started thinking about myself and what I wanted out of weight loss differently, I started having a much easier time achieving my goals. I basically had to lose the weight of comparison and expectations in my brain first. And so here's how I did it. Step one, I realized that I would have no idea what my ideal body actually looks like until I reached my healthiest point. And then whatever that is, that's me. My ideal body did not already exist on someone else. There are a lot of ideals that society idolizes 24/7, but those are not me. And I had to stop internalizing that idealization and holding it up as the end goal for myself. So a huge part of my motivation was just figuring out what would I look like at my healthiest. I had to accept the end product before I was able to even begin. And that right off the bat allowed me to let go of so much frustration that came from, "I'm following this person's advice, but I don't look like them." Honestly, I was never going to, but that doesn't mean that there's not an absolute best version of me and that version is not worth more or less than anybody else. Step two, I had to stop othering my ideal self. In my head, there was a really sharp distinction between me at that time and the person I was trying to be. If you ask me, I probably wouldn't have realized it, but in retrospect, I genuinely believed that current D'Angelo and skinny D'Angelo were two different people. There was this alternate, thinner version of me somewhere in the future who didn't struggle with any of this and was super active and ate intuitively and all that jazz. But literally just shifting my mindset and saying, "I am not skinnier D'Angelo, but healthier D'Angelo." And I am that person right now, was huge. I am that person who will one day be healthier. That person is not a more palatable alternative to me. Really, I had to look at myself in the mirror and say, "That person who I think is better than me is me." And a lot of things fell into place after that. Step three, I had to relearn my own feelings. So this last one I think was by far the most important. I grew up in an environment where I was never actually asked if I was hungry. It was more like, "Here, three meals a day, seconds of it tasted good and always finish your meal in its entirety." In my mind, that was what eating meant. Three meals a day, more if you want it, don't waste your food. And because I never really stopped to ask if I was hungry, I say with absolutely no exaggeration or humor that for 20 years I didn't really know what being hungry meant. I thought I knew, but I didn't. I knew what I want food felt like, and I thought that that was hunger, but I realized they're actually not the same thing at all. And so my solution for this one wasn't a one and done thing. This is something I still have to do. Whenever I think I'm hungry, I stop and ask myself, "How do you feel though?" And by the way, if the answer is "I feel hungry," and if you keep playing armchair psychologist, I'm about to fight, then the answer is, "I will just eat food." But over the years, I realized that nine times out of 10, that's not generally the answer for me. The three top things I realized that my brain interprets as hunger are boredom, being around food and here's the kicker. "A couple of hours ago when I was eating, I was happier than I feel right now. So I should probably just go back to doing that." That last one really put some things in perspective for me about how my brain works. But even just being somewhat conscious of what I was eating and more importantly why I was eating it and how that was going to make me feel before I did it, went a long So my ultimate workout plan was actually working out why I was unhealthy in the first place. And for me, a lot of this unhealthiness was completely mental. The diet and exercise of it all was frankly something I never would've been able to stick to when I was dealing with all of that confusion. It's like trying to follow a tutorial without knowing what it's. For you, it's your job to work out what works for you. Nobody can give you the secret, but that's actually a good thing. There is no secret. You can be healthy and you deserve to be healthy. It's no wonder that many people have difficulty maintaining a healthy weight given the availability of high-calorie foods and their strong advertising. There is also a theory that helps explain some of this difficulty people have in losing weight, and this theory is associated with inflammation in the hypothalamus, suggesting that excessive consumption of high-calorie foods can lead to damage to the nerves responsible for responding to weight-regulating hormones like leptin. This results in leptin resistance. The brain is unable to properly recognize satiety signals, leading to a reduction in metabolism and an increase in appetite. This is due to hypothalamic issues, resulting in significant difficulty for patients in losing weight, even when exercising. As I said, this is still a theory being studied by researchers, but we have a recent study, including one from this year, that provides further evidence of this causal effect of consuming high-calorie foods with brain damage in these weight regulation areas. This explains why many patients, when they stop taking the medication, regain all the weight they lost, some even more, because it's possible that their brain system is so damaged that they would have to take medication for life. We have everything in Nature, which shows precisely that patients can maintain this weight loss, but only while they are taking the medication. The moment they stop taking the medication, they regain the weight. Because that's not normal. >> No, it wasn't just value investors slowly waking up and trickling back in. It was a violent repricing event driven by underlying order flow. Right now, short interest on HIMS is sitting at an extremely high 40.2%. >> Okay, wait. Over 40% short interest is a staggering metric. >> It's massive. >> Walk us through the actual mechanism of a short squeeze here because a 41% jump is unnatural. How does high short interest act like rocket fuel when positive news hits? Okay, think of short selling like a highstakes game of musical shares. >> Okay, >> when short interest is at 40.2%, it means over 40% of the available tradable shares have been borrowed by investors who immediately sold them. They are actively betting the price will go down. >> So, they don't even own the shares, >> right? They don't own them. They owe them to someone else. Eventually, they are legally obligated to buy shares back in the open market to return them and close their position. >> So, they need the price to drop to buy them back cheaper and pocket the difference. >> Exactly. But when a surprise positive catalyst hits, like a major patent settlement with a massive pharmaceutical giant, the stock price starts to tick up. >> Oh boy. >> Suddenly, all those short sellers are losing money fast. The music stops. They panic and they rush into the market all at the exact same time to buy shares to cover their positions and stop the bleeding. >> And that forced buying pushes the price up even faster. >> Yes. which then forces the next level of short sellers to buy, creating this vicious mechanical cycle upwards. They're literally forced to buy a chair at whatever price the market demands. >> That is exactly what a squeeze is. That 40.2% short interest is essentially a powder keg. And the Novo Nordisk settlement was the match. As long as short interest remains that elevated, any positive news sets up the potential for further violent squeezes because so many traders are caught leaning the wrong way. >> Wait. Okay. They are, you know, buying a house or they are just diversifying their personal wealth because 90% of their net worth is tied up in company stock, >> right? RSUs and all that. Meaning investors are paying $39 for every $1 of expected future earnings. For a traditional value investor, that's a little rich. They can operate Amazon clinic as a loss leader. The goal isn't to make money on the doctor's visit. The goal is to keep you locked into the Amazon Prime ecosystem where they make their real billions. >> Hims cannot do that. Tellahalth is their only product. They have to make a profit on the actual medical transaction. That structural disadvantage is huge. But here's where it gets really interesting. Because even with Amazon and Eli Liy breathing down their necks, competition isn't the biggest elephant in the room. >> No, it's not. >> The ultimate downside risk here. The reason retail traders often get utterly trapped in these high growth names is regulatory risk. We're tracking ongoing Department of Justice and FDA probes into the marketing of compounded drugs. How quickly could that halt sales? Well, this is the primary headwind keeping the stock suppressed. To understand this risk, you have to understand the mechanism of compoundingarmacies. Okay. Hymns is legally allowed to sell compounded generic versions of these blockbuster GLP-1 drugs only because the FDA currently lists the name brand drugs as being in a national shortage. >> Oh, I see. >> Yeah. It is a regulatory loophole designed to ensure patients can get medicine when the primary manufacturer can't keep up with demand. But the second the primary manufacturer ramps up production and the FDA declares the shortage is over, that loopholes snap shut. >> Precisely. And this is why retail traders get trapped. They look at the massive revenue growth. They look at the cheap 2.13 price to sales ratio and they think they found a hidden gem. But they are ignoring the binary nature of this risk. >> Binary like a light switch. >> Exactly. A regulatory crackdown or the end of a shortage isn't like a slow, predictable decline in sales. It is a light switch. The FDA can issue guidance tomorrow that drastically restricts how compounded weight loss medications are prescribed or they just declare the shortage over. >> And if that happens, >> if that happens, a massive, highly profitable chunk of HIM's projected revenue vanishes instantly. The market prices in that binary risk by keeping the stock trading at a heavy discount. >> Wow. So the stock is cheap entirely because of the fear that the government will flip the switch. If the switch never gets flipped, the stock is massively undervalued. If it does get flipped, it is a classic value trap. >> That is the exact dilemma. >> So how do we synthesize this outlook? >> Well, you have to separate your timeline. In the short-term outlook, expect extreme volatility. Traders are going to be anxiously watching that $20 psychological level. If it breaks below that consistently, the technical damage will invite more short sellers to pile in. If they actually hit 6.5 billion in revenue, today's price is around $20 will look like an absolute steal in hindsight. Our goal today was to arm you with the underlying mechanics so you can think critically about those exact scenarios. Yeah, >> we want you to look at that 6.5 billion long-term target, look at the 40% short interest powder keg, understand the FDA shortage loophole, and make an informed decision about the reality of this company's future. >> This raises an important question though, one that should really guide your thinking as you track this stock moving forward. They hit that incredibly ambitious 6.5 billion revenue target by the year 2030. >> A massive victory for the bull, >> right? - Ozempic, Mounjaro, Wegovy, Zepbound: Who can take which one? | Ohio State Medical Center: Kind: captions Language: en-US Currently in the United States, there are two FDA-approved medications for treatment of obesity or overweight with individuals who have medical comorbidities like high blood pressure, sleep apnea or abnormal cholesterol levels. The two medications are semaglutide and tirzepatide. Semaglutide is also a brand named Wegovy when it's used just for the treatment of obesity or overweight with a comorbidity. The exact same medication is brand named as Ozempic when it's used to treat individuals with type 2 diabetes. Tirzepatide, the brand name is Zepbound when it's used, again — to treat obesity or overweight with comorbidities. The brand name, when used to treat individuals with type 2 diabetes, is Mounjaro. This is pretty confusing for a lot of people because all of these names are not necessarily interchangeable — because when your doctor's sending in a prescription, it needs to be for the right diagnosis. However, for individuals who are considering these medications, it is important to realize that they are the exact same molecule, depending on if it's semaglutide or tirzepatide. There are two GLP-1 medications available for weight loss treatment currently approved by the FDA: there's Wegovy and Zepbound. Wegovy and Zepbound are both approved for individuals with a body mass index [BMI] greater than 30, or a body mass index greater than 27 with a medical comorbidity. Wegovy also has indications for individuals with those body mass index criteria who are at risk of having a major adverse cardiovascular event — nonfatal heart attack; nonfatal stroke; or peripheral arterial disease. Clinical eligibility for either semaglutide or tirzepatide really depends on the FDA indications and if you meet the qualifications. Again, this can be very confusing for individuals because the medications semaglutide&nbsp;&nbsp; and tirzepatide have indications without diabetes, as well as treatment for type 2 diabetes. It's really important to talk to your doctor about your medical comorbidities or medical conditions that you want to treat. And your physician will be able to guide you best towards choosing the medication that's right for you.

- Diabetes drug Mounjaro shows heart benefits: Study: Kind: captions Language: en to a new study on the popular diabetes drug Mangaro showing more potential health benefits. So, there's been this constant trickle of like new data on the benefits of these GLP-1 drugs. Uh, this study you say though is different from what we've seen before. Why is that? >> Well, you know, this class of GLP medications has grown in popularity year after year. Well known for their injectable weight loss medications, ompic probably being the most popular. Well, now research is coming out more and more that their benefits are potential beyond diabetes, beyond weight loss, but in reducing the risk of death from cardiovascular disease. Its active ingredient is tzeptide, the same in zepp. And it compares it to truly, a more traditional version. It targets one hormone in a similar way that ompic does. This is really fascinating because we continue to hear more about what these drugs are capable of. But what did this study find in real numbers? >> Well, in real numbers, it found that both of these medications, Trulicity, the more traditional version, and Mjaro, the more modern version, reduce the risk of cardiovascular disease. But Mjaro seems to do it better in terms of reducing the risk of cardiovascular death from heart attack and stroke, 8% lower risk compared to Trulicity. And in terms of overall risk of death, a 16% drop. So there's going to be more medications in this class. I think it's important to understand that there's no clear winner. When you're trying to understand these medications, you need to know that they have variation in effectiveness. There's different dosages. Some have different insurance coverage options and also some have different side effects. So that's why it's important to talk to your provider. But you most important with these medications understand that they're lifelong medications in the most part because these are chronic diseases and conditions. - MOUNJARO (Tirzepatide) | What to Know Before Starting!: Kind: captions Language: en Today we're talking about Mjaro, one of the newest and most effective treatments for type 2 diabetes. But it's not for everybody and there are some side effects that you need to know about before starting it. So the question that you need to answer is if this medications for you. We're going to try and help you answer this today by discussing what exactly it is, how effective it can be, and most importantly, what kind of side effects can you expect so you can decide if you want to give it a try. Menjaro or Epatide was approved by the FDA in May of 2022 for the treatment of type 2 diabetes. And it's part of a class of medications known as GLP-1 and GIP receptor agonist. What this means is that it mimics two different gut hormones that help regulate blood sugar levels, making it one of the most powerful diabetes medications available. It works in three key ways. First, it helps stimulate insulin release when blood sugar is high. Second, it reduces how much glucose is produced by the liver. And third, it slows down digestion, which helps control appetite and leads to weight loss. Because of these mechanisms, it not only helps lower blood sugar, but also aids in weight management, which is a huge benefit for many people. Mjaro is an injectable medication that comes in a pre-filled single dose pin. It's taken once a week with doses starting at 2.5 millig. The typical dosing schedule includes increasing to 5 milligs and for those who need more blood sugar control, doses can go up to 7.5, 10, 12, and even the maximum of 15 milligs per week. The gradual dose escalation helps minimize side effects while improving the medication's effectiveness. The injection is typically administered in the abdomen, thigh, or upper arm. And it's important to rotate injection sites to reduce irritation. Now, let's talk about how effective it really is. Clinical studies have shown that Mjaro can lower A1C levels by up to 2.1%. What makes it one of the most effective diabetes treatments available? Now, an A1C is a lab that measures your average blood sugars over the past 3 months. and lowering it significantly reduces the risk of diabetes complications like nerve damage, kidney disease, and vision problems. Not only does it help with diabetes, but research also suggests that it may have cardiovascular benefits, reducing the risk of heart disease, heart attacks, and strokes. This makes it a medication that offers multiple health benefits beyond just blood sugar control. Now, let's get what you really want to know about, though, and that's the side effects. Most people tolerate Mjaro pretty well, but there are some common side effects to watch out for. The most frequent ones include nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, constipation, abdominal pain, bloating, and heartburn. These side effects happen because manaro slows digestion, which can cause food to sit in the stomach longer. But the good news is that for most people, these side effects tend to be mild and get better as your body adjusts the medication. Now, if you're experiencing nausea, there are things you can do to help, like eating smaller portions, avoiding greasy foods, and taking your time when eating can make a big difference. Some patients also find it helpful to stay upright for a while after eating instead of lying down right away. There's also a rare but serious risk of something called pancreatitis. That's when the pancreas gets really inflamed. It can cause symptoms like severe abdominal pain that doesn't go away. Nausea and vomiting. So if you ever experience any of these kind of symptoms, stop taking the medication and call your doctor right away. Another important consideration is who should not take. If you have a personal or family history of medularary thyroid cancer or something called multiple endocanoplasia syndrome type 2, you should avoid it. These conditions are rare, but since Monjaro has been associated with potential increased risk of thyroid tumors in animal studies, it's best to be cautious. It's also not recommended if you've had pancreatitis in the past since this medication can increase the risk of another attack. It's also important to keep a close watch on your blood sugars, especially if you're taking other diabetes medications. While Mjaro on its own does not usually cause dangerously low blood sugar levels, when it's combined with other diabetic medications like insulin or salonas, it can increase the risk of hypoglycemia. Now, symptoms of low blood sugars include dizziness, sweating, shakiness, and confusion. If you experience any of these kind of things, check your blood sugar right away and follow your doctor's instructions on managing low blood sugar episodes. Now, let's talk about cost because this is one of the biggest issues with the medication. Ando is expensive. If you're paying out of pocket, you're looking around $1,000 a month. Insurance will usually cover it, but it depends on your plan. If your insurance doesn't cover it, there are some manufacturer savings programs and some patient assistance programs that can help lower the cost. But definitely look at those if you're considering this medication. Some patients have been able to use savings cards to get their prescription for as little as $25 a month. So, it's worth going to their website to see if you can qualify for anything like that. So, the big question is, is Magaro right for you? Well, it is one of the most powerful diabetes medications available, offering significant blood sugar control and weight loss benefit. While it does come with some side effects and a high price tag, for many people, the benefits can outweigh the risk. So, if you're thinking about starting it, talk to your doctor and see if it's a good fit for you. And remember, medications are just one part of the equation. Lifestyle changes like diet and exercise are key to long-term success in managing diabetes. Even small changes like cutting back on processed foods, increasing daily activity can make a big difference. Now, if you're looking for ways to improve your health naturally, check out this video right here where I break down simple lifestyle changes that can help you take control of your diabetes. Thanks for watching and we'll see you in the next video.

- Scientists find that the weight-loss drug Mounjaro can reduce the risk of some diseases: Kind: captions Language: en Let's get you some health headlines now. More people are turning to weight loss drugs to shed unwanted pounds. -- And -- with so many options to choose from, a new study found that one type that does more for your overall health. Doctors at the Cleveland Clinic compared two popular medications, daglutide, which is sold under the brand name Trulicity, and Trizeettide, which is sold under Zebound and Manjaro. While they found both medications work the same way, doctors say that rizepetide was linked to lower risk for heart and kidney disease. There was about a 16% lower risk of all of the six endpoints pooled together, but very impressively, there was also a 16% reduction in death due to all causes. And the study also found that side effects in the stomach were more common when using medications like Zebound and Manjaro. A 3rd wave of weight loss drugs already in the works, and experts hope that it can push weight loss further without the health risks. Let's go.

- Study hints healthier school lunch can reduce obesity: Kind: captions Language: en That's when we make the news makes it the United States considering updates on school meals that would put more limits on added sugar and sodium. It comes as a new study found the last major push for better nutrition at school may have actually worked. Getting healthier school lunches really reduce obesity. Let's connect the dots. Childhood obesity has been rising for decades, and now it affects about one in five US kids. Back in 2010, a federal law championed by then First Lady Michelle Obama increased the quantity of fruits, vegetables and whole grains required in school meals. That change impacting millions of kids across the country. A national study found a small but significant decline in the average body mass index of more than. 14,000 school kids, their heights and weights were tracked before and after the healthy hunger Free Kids Act of 2010 was implemented. Experts note this flattening is important, as holding kids weight steady as they grow can help keep their obesity in check. Doctors add that more changes could have the potential to impact excessive weight gain over the course of an entire childhood, and that is connecting the dots.

it's not completely that person's fault you know i think that when somebody from the outside looking in sees somebody who's struggling with their weight they think well this person is lazy they just don't like to cook they just like fast food and it'd be so easy for them to just start eating more fruits more vegetables and having a healthier diet but it's not that easy right what people need to know is that there is research out there that backs up the fact that these highly palatable foods can be just as addictive as any narcotic on the market right now you know i i'm i'm working with dr mickey whit from the university of miami she's a neuroscientist she's brilliant and one of the things that she's taught me using actual brain scans is that the brain actually does react identically and she showed me she's like okay this is a picture of a brain somebody who's eating a highly palatable food for the very first time and this brain i mean it lights up like a christmas tree right and it's the same type of reaction for somebody who's using say cocaine for the first time that was what was used in this model and then she's like now this is a brain scan of somebody who is a food addict eating something that is highly palatable high fat high calorie just a heart attack and a sac kind of fast food meal this is what happens to the brain over time and so that really really illuminated area of the brain where you get that dopamine release it was really kind of blunted right so you're kind of chasing the high at that point you're never going to get that same high that you got the first time that you ate that food but you're going to be chasing it and then she showed me the scan of a cocaine addict and it was exactly the same it was exactly the same regions of the brain exactly the same type of reaction in the brain to the same level and so people don't think that it's a cinch to kick a cocaine habit people know that it's hell and it's torture and you go to rehab and So, isn't it the same thing to have this faith based off of really no empirical data that artificial intelligence where will improve to get rid of death? And why specifically does it have to improve to conquer that goal? Why can't it be any other goal? Is it are you saying that it's that goal? Because that's what you want or because there's actual evidence that we are going to achieve ending death. >> Yeah. I mean, to hope is to be human, right? Like that's our fuel. We we all feel hope that we can have tomorrow and that we have something to to gain tomorrow. And the reason why it's don't die is because anything even in the positive where I say live well or be well or live long, your version of that may be different than my version. And what happens when humans disagree on certain things is they kill each other. >> That's been the case all along is that humans kill each other over different ideas. Don't die is the single thing everybody can agree to. rich and poor, whatever nation, it is the single thing that humans want. Nobody wants to die right now. And that's what I'm trying to say. This is not about immortality. It's not about 50 years in the future. It's not about the rich getting mortality. This is about every human on the planet saying we value being alive right now. It acknowledges human dignity and human existence at the most humble layer possible. And it's the most friendly ideology where it says you can be Christian and don't die. You can be Muslim, don't die. You can be capitalist don't die. Socialist, don't die. Chinese and don't die. You can be anything you want and don't die. I assume you're a little bit afraid to die. But we don't know what it is. We don't know if it's a negative or a positive.
